question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the challenges facing the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any pressures in recruiting and retaining skilled staff in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any work being carried out through the Northern Ireland Bureau in Beijing to strengthen trade cooperation with China following the China-Northern Ireland Economic, Trade and Investment Cooperation Forum in 2025.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the draft Parking Restrictions Order is anticipated to be introduced to the Assembly.
answer_text: The recent environmental improvement scheme, completed in Banbridge, has required an update to the existing legislation to enable the continuation of parking restrictions enforcement in the town centre.  These changes are now being progressed and should take approximately 6 months to complete.  Enforcement will resume on completion of the necessary legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any quantifiable effects of the conflict in the Middle East on the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: I have been closely monitoring the economic effects of the conflict in the Middle East, which has resulted in sharp and immediate cost increases for households and businesses across the North and significant disruptions to business supply chains.   Global oil and gas markets have reacted strongly, with prices rising significantly. Given that more than 60% of households here rely on home heating oil, the impact has been particularly severe, with the average price almost doubling in under a week. These pressures will inevitably feed into wider inflation and higher operating costs for businesses, exacerbating the challenges already faced during an ongoing cost-of-living crisis. I fully recognise the strain this places on households and firms alike. I will continue to monitor developments closely and press the British Government to take further action to shield consumers and businesses from additional financial pressures as this situation evolves.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered granting chapels of rest and church sanctuaries exceptions from business rates, in line with the exemptions provided for places of public religious worship.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to his Oral question on the matter on Monday 16th March 2026, page 34 of the Hansard record of proceedings.plenary-16-03-2026.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether there are any dispensations in place to permit a burial or cremation to take place at a weekend without a GRO21 having been issued; (ii) any guidance that has been provided to local councils regarding the requirement for a GRO21 before burial or cremation; and (ii) any legal enforcement in place to ensure GRO21 are not issued retrospectively, after a burial or cremation.
answer_text: StormontAQW  42242/22-27Doug Beattie has asked:To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether there are any dispensations in place to permit a burial or cremation to take place at a weekend without a GRO21 having been issued; (ii) any guidance that has been provided to local councils regarding the requirement for a GRO21 before burial or cremation; and (iii) any legal enforcement in place to ensure GRO21 are not issued retrospectively, after a burial or cremation.	ANSWERWhile it is best practice that the GRO21 form is in place before a burial, there is nothing in law to prohibit a burial taking place at the weekend without a GRO21 form having been issued.As it is possible in principle for a burial to proceed without a GRO21 form, there are no sanctions in such circumstances. The death can be registered after the burial has taken place. A burial may not proceed without a Medical Certificate of Cause of Death having been issued. With regards to cremations, it is not possible for a cremation to take place until the GRO 21 form has been issued to the funeral director. Therefore, it would not be possible for a cremation to take place at the weekend as there are no dispensations in place to permit this.Registration staff are provided with a death registration handbook that outlines best practice for the registration of deaths. This advises that the GRO21 is to be issued to the informant or funeral director following the registration of the death.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider removing rates paid on funeral directors' chapels of rest.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to sentencing for animal cruelty.
answer_text: The maximum offences available for animal welfare offences are up to 5 years imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine. These penalties are on par with those available in the rest of the UK and I am satisfied that they remain proportionate.During my time in office, I have met with the Minister of Justice or her officials to discuss animal welfare matters. These discussions have included meeting with the USPCA, regarding their report on animal welfare sentencing, and with the LINKS Group, to examine the connection between domestic violence and animal abuse.I also asked my officials to engage with the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) to gain an understanding on how animal welfare cases are prosecuted. However, with regards to engagement on the matter of sentencing with the Minsiter of Justice, it remains the case that determining the level of sentence to be handed down is a matter for the judiciary, who operate independently to any Executive department.Nevertheless, I was pleased that that the matter of animal welfare will be included in a forthcoming review on sentencing guidelines in Northern Ireland and my officials remain ready to assist in that process in the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline how her Department engages with the Home Office to ensure foreign nationals that have been given a custodial sentence of 12 months or more are not released back into the community at the end of their sentence, but surrendered to the Foreign National Offenders Returns Command for deportation.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 39450/22-27.

answer_text: The table below details the number of custodial sentences of one year or more imposed on foreign national prisoners in the last five years. The total number of sentences in the last five years refer to 138 foreign national prisoners.Year of SentenceNo. of Sentences202122202228202338202434202523Total145The Home Office is responsible for any deportation of foreign nationals. The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) is responsible for holding those in their custody as directed under the warrant of committal issued by the sentencing court.The NIPS is responsible for informing the Home Office of all sentenced foreign national offenders within ten working days of sentencing: thereafter, the Home Office investigate the case and all decisions pertaining to the individual’s immigration and deportation status are a matter for them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) who makes the decision to release a prisoner unaccompanied, as part of pre-release testing; (ii) who attends any multi-agency meeting to agree such a release; and (iii) whether the Parole Commissioners are informed of this release.
answer_text: Pre-Release Testing (PRT) provides an opportunity to assess a prisoner’s suitability for release on Licence or Life Licence by allowing them to be tested in conditions which are as close to those in the community as possible. Significant emphasis is placed on responsibility, compliance, honesty and personal development with exposure to conditions that help prepare prisoners to resettle into the community. Unaccompanied Temporary Release (UTR) forms part of the PRT process.All decisions throughout the PRT process, including to release a prisoner unaccompanied, are taken by the Prisoner Development Unit (PDU) Governor managing the case. This follows a recommendation from the Parole Commissioners for Northern Ireland (PCNI) and the completion of a Case Conference. The Case Conference is chaired by the PDU Governor and normally includes representatives from the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI), NIPS Psychology and Healthcare in Prisons (primary and mental health). Additional business areas, such as Prisoner Safety and Support, Residential or Security, may attend when relevant. Following the Case Conference, a comprehensive multidisciplinary risk assessment is conducted to allow the PDU Governor to make an informed decision. The safety of the public and others remains the primary consideration at every stage.PCNI are not informed of every individual period of PRT following their initial recommendation. However, at the next point of referral PCNI will receive updated reports from all relevant business areas, which will include information on whether PRT has commenced and how the individual has engaged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any statutory duty on her to conduct pre-release testing for life term prisoners, prior to the end of their tariff.
answer_text: Life Sentence Prisoners have a statutory right to be considered for release by the Parole Commissioners (PCNI) as soon as they have served their tariff/minimum period as set down by the trial judge.  At the three years pre-tariff, (the point at which life sentence cases are referred to PCNI), PCNI can make a recommendation to NIPS that a prisoner engage in a period of pre-release testing in the community prior to final release. This is because pre-release testing for life sentence prisoners forms part of the risk assessment required by PCNI to enable them to make a determination whether the continued detention of a prisoner is necessary for public protection. After conducting its own risk assessment, NIPS may decide to implement a structured pre-release testing plan based on the PCNI recommendation.  The legislative basis for NIPS authority to provide prisoner pre-release testing lies in Rule 27 of the Prisoner and Young Offender Centre Rules 1995.  A programme of pre-release testing, if recommended by PCNI and risk assessed as appropriate by NIPS, assists in providing an essential evidence base for the overall assessment of a prisoner’s suitability for release from prison. Prisoners are initially tested under the supervision of prison staff, progressing to periods of unaccompanied release before potentially progressing to living and working in the community.  At every stage of this process, the level of risk they present is kept under review by prison and probation staff.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: Since the Scheme opened in August 2021, more than 12,000 applications have been submitted with almost £124 million paid to eligible applicants, offering meaningful recognition of the harm they have suffered. Both TEO and the Victims Payments Board continue to work closely with victims’ groups to keep the scheme under review and look for ways of improving the throughput of cases.A recent review of operational effectiveness and performance identified opportunities to streamline processes and enhance the service provided to applicants.The Board is actively progressing these recommendations through a dedicated Business Transformation Project Team, with the aim of delivering more efficient and responsive support to those engaging with the Scheme.The Board also continue to work closely with evidence provider partners and Capita to introduce improvements to these areas.A regional model for the gathering of secondary care evidence is working well, and engagement continues with statutory agencies involved in this work.    With the Scheme now in its final year, we would encourage people to apply. Details of groups providing support for applications are available on the Victims Payments Board website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any procedures allowing local councils to (a) suspend; or (b) curtail the statutory power of, the council Chief Executive; and (ii) whether the powers contained within the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 1972 remain the main primary legislation governing the powers of the Chief Executive.
answer_text: Questions (i)(a) & (b) are matters for councils to determine.In relation to question (ii), councils may delegate functions of a council to a Chief Executive under section 7(1)(a) of the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 2014 except for those listed in section 7(3) which may only be discharged by the council itself.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any measures she will take to ensure that roadside school bus stops have adequate street lighting.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any complaints made against the Local Government Staffing Commission, in each of the last 12 months; including (i) who submitted each complaint; (ii) who the complaints were against; and (iii) any outcome of the complaints.
answer_text: During the period 1 January 2025 – 31 December 2025, two complaints were made against the Local Government Staff Commission (the Commission), submitted by email to the Commission’s Chairperson on 27 November 2025 and 2 December 2025. The Commission has advised that:  One complaint was submitted by a senior officer from Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council and one complaint was submitted by the Lord Mayor, Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council on behalf of the Council.  The complaints related to an employee of the Commission.  The complaints were considered under stage 2 of the Commission’s Complaints Handling Procedure.  No aspect of either complaint was upheld.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of any instances of GPs and consultants refusing to complete medical declaration forms requested for completion by the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any systematic or widespread instances of GPs or consultants refusing to complete medical declaration forms requested by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA).Responsibility for assessing medical fitness to drive, including the administration of medical declaration forms, sits with the DVA, an agency of the Department for Infrastructure. Where medical information is required, the DVA may seek reports from relevant healthcare professionals, with the consent of the individual.Doctors and other healthcare professionals are expected to complete such requests in line with professional guidance on medical fitness to drive, recognising their clinical responsibilities and professional judgement. Decisions about the provision of medical information are made on a case-by-case basis, taking account of clinical relevance, consent and workload pressures.My Department continues to work with the Department for Infrastructure to support awareness among healthcare professionals of their roles and responsibilities in relation to medical fitness to drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of people who have had their driving licence revoked and are currently awaiting reinstatement, as a result of the dispute between GPs, consultants and Driver and Vehicle Agency's personnel, regarding the completion of medical declaration forms.
answer_text: The DVA does not collate, record, or report licence revocations or reinstatement waiting times by reference to any alleged disputes with medical practitioners regarding the completion of medical declaration forms. I am therefore unable to provide the requested information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason classroom assistants in Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools are not receiving the same uplift in wages as classroom assistants in special schools.
answer_text: Each SEN Classroom Assistant post is aligned to one of two grades - Classroom Assistant SEN or Classroom Assistant Additional SEN. To meet the Additional Special Needs Classroom Assistant rate, a Classroom Assistant must be working with a pupil(s) who hold(s) a Statement of Special Educational Needs.  Then, in working with that pupil, the Classroom Assistant should meet at least one of the first three points on the Additional Special Needs job description namely: Undertake more comprehensive or invasive medical/clinical procedures.Help pupils with specialist communication skills and/or sensory difficulties access the curriculum.Deal with pupils with very challenging behaviour as identified by the Educational Psychology Service as requiring additional provision. As the majority of pupils placed in special schools have significant and complex needs, the number of Classroom Assistants on the Additional Special Needs Classroom Assistant rate in special schools is higher than that of specialist provisions attached to mainstream schools. However, the percentage uplift in salary following pay award agreements is the same for Classroom Assistant SEN posts and Classroom Assistant Additional SEN posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 2905/22-27, whether this figure includes the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: Yes, the figure provided in AQW 2905/22-27 includes Prison Service figures as part of the overall Civil Service figures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether schools or governing bodies are allowed to make a surplus or profit from canteen food sales to pupils.
answer_text: The provision of school meals is the responsibility of the Education Authority (EA) in all grant-aided schools other than voluntary grammar (VG) and grant-maintained integrated (GMI) schools. In VG and GMI schools, the responsibility for school meals provision rests with each school’s Board of Governors.The prices for school meals in schools where the EA is responsible for the catering are determined by the EA. The prices in VG and GMI schools are determined by each schools’ Board of Governors. In its announcement of the rise in its school meals prices to take effect from January 2026, the EA noted that its new school meal price levels remain well below its costs of production. In VG/GMI schools, the prices of food and refreshment items should be sufficient to recover the full cost of production. Whilst these schools are not prevented from setting their prices at a level higher than their full cost of production, they are also continuing to manage similar increases in production costs that have been experienced by the EA in recent years. Around 90,000 children and young people are entitled to receive free school meals (FSM) each day and will be unaffected by the EA decision to raise its school meal prices. All low-income families are encouraged to apply for FSM through the EA, and it provides guidance and support to facilitate all such families making an application. Families not entitled to FSM retain the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch. Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch. I recognise the importance of pupils being able to access a healthy lunch in terms of the positive impact this has on both their educational and health outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the assessment of gender identity services provision in Northern Ireland carried out by Baroness Hilary Cass.
answer_text: Members will be aware that following concerns raised by Executive colleagues, Baroness Hilary Cass has been asked to conduct an assessment of the service model in NI.   A draft report has been submitted to my officials for consideration.  Baroness Cass and her team have determined that the networked model that currently exists between the CAMHS, Knowing our Identity (KOI) service and integrated social service processes is a key asset of the NI approach, as is the chance to have a seamless interface across the age range.Baroness Cass and her team have made six key recommendations in the report.  Recommendations 1-3 relate to workforce, integration with paediatric services and clinical leadership within the under-18’s service, and are suggestions to make the NI service consistent with the English services, regardless of whether or not there is a decision to join the UK National Provider Network and research programme. These include securing proposed staffing and strengthening links to RBHSC, including funding a clinical lead for the service.Recommendations 4-6 are required to enable NI to join the UK National Provider Network and participate in the research programme which we have already outlined our intention to do. The recruitment and training of new staff will be the immediate priority, and it will take some time for the new arrangements and model of care to bed in. Fuller alignment with the National Provider Network and engagement in PATHWAYS research programme is urgent and should be led by the Paediatric Service Manager in liaison with the Lifespan Service manager and the paediatric endocrinologist. This visit by Baroness Cass and her colleagues represents a first step in a future collaborative relationship.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any progress made in the Northern Ireland Civil Service People Strategy 2025-2030 to deal with the high rates in the turnover of staff.
answer_text: Turnover rates within the Civil Service here are comparatively low when compared to the Civil Service in Britain.  The most recent official statistics, from April 2024, show an overall leaver rate of 4.8%, which includes retirements and other departures. The voluntary resignation figure is just 1.6%. For context, the Civil Service in Britain reported a resignation rate of 4.2% and an overall turnover rate of 7.5% in 2024.The Civil Service People Strategy sets out our ambition to create a modern, inclusive and high-performing organisation. A key part of that is strengthening recruitment and retention through measures such as improving career pathways, investing in skills and enhancing employee engagement. These actions will help us continue to attract and retain the best people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36602/22-27, whether any remission is provided for (i) lessons; and (ii) instrument rental, for a child who choses music as one of their GCSEs.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that EA Music Service provides a structured remission policy for pupils entitled to Free School Meals (FSME) who receive tuition through the EA Music Service.(i) LessonsFor pupils registered as FSME and receiving tuition directly from the EA Music Service, a remission of 70% is applied to lesson charges. This ensures that children from lower-income families can access music education at a significantly reduced cost.However, where a pupil receives lessons from a privately engaged music tutor, no remission is available, as the EA Music Service does not fund or subsidise private provision.(ii) Instrument RentalSimilarly, for FSME-registered pupils receiving tuition from the EA Music Service, a 70% remission is applied to instrument hire charges. This applies regardless of whether the pupil is studying music as part of their GCSE curriculum or not.As indicated in relation to i. above, no remission is provided by the EA in relation to instrument hire where tuition is sourced privately.This approach reflects the EA’s commitment to supporting access to music education for disadvantaged pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the procedure for the deportation of foreign nationals who have been sentenced to over 12 months in custody; and (ii) , if they are given time served which was over 12 months, who is responsible for ensuring Home Office officials are present in court to ensure the individual is taken into custody for deportation.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) is responsible for informing the Immigration Officer (IO) from the Home Office (HO) Foreign National Offenders Returns Command (FNORC) of all sentenced Foreign National Offenders within ten working days of sentencing.  Thereafter, the IO investigates the case and all decisions pertaining to their immigration and deportation status are a matter for FNORC.  NIPS advise the Home Office when a prisoner who is time-served will be released at court.  It is the responsibility of the Home Office to decide whether to attend court to take the individual into their custody for deportation following release.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of staff development days each school is permitted to take in an academic year, broken down by primary and post-primary education and; (ii) the content of these development days; and; (iii) why these take place during term-time.
answer_text: In the current academic year, my Department has granted all schools permission to take up to five School Development Days.  These days are intended to provide school staff with additional non-contact time to use to engage in whole-school self-evaluation and continuing professional development, in pursuit of school improvement and raising of standards.  Further information is available in the published circular: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-school-development-days-20252026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she will take to reform Class C taxi legislation relating to funeral vehicles, to ensure it is fair, proportionate and puts the needs of bereaved people first.
answer_text: On 25 March 2025, I announced a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with Class C Taxi Licensing. As part of this review, I recently met with representatives of the National Association of Funeral Directors to gain a better understanding of the issues facing the sector.My Officials are currently drawing up an options paper and any proposed changes will be subject to a public consultation in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of small businesses, in Upper Bann, that have availed of support from Invest NI, in the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2025 being the most recent available.  Over the period 1st April 2020 to the 31st March 2025 Invest NI has supported 225 businesses within the Upper Bann Parliamentary Constituency area, 186 (83%) of these were small businesses.	In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail any improvements being made by her Department to ensure that court proceedings, in domestic and sexual violence cases, are victim-centered and trauma-informed; and; (ii) whether she has any plans to expand specialist domestic abuse or sexual offences courts.
answer_text: I recognise that court proceedings can be stressful for all victims of crime, but particularly for victims of domestic and sexual violence. As Justice Minister, it is a key priority of mine to introduce victim-centred, trauma-informed measures aimed at increasing victim confidence and easing their concerns.The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and services that victims of crime in Northern Ireland can expect to receive from criminal justice organisations. It includes information on victims’ entitlements in relation to court proceedings, such as the right to ask for a court familiarisation visit before the case is heard, to enter the building through a different entrance from the suspect and to sit in a separate waiting area, where possible. The Charter also provides information about support services available, and what to do should anyone feel their Charter rights are not being upheld. In addition, measures under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy are intended to protect those at risk, but also to support increased reporting and reduced attrition, by ensuring that victims know they will be listened to and supported, and that robust measures are in place to hold perpetrators to account.Some of the transformative measures introduced under the Gillen Review Implementation Programme are also helping to support victims during court proceedings. These include:The introduction of Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) to provide a safe, comfortable space away from the court building for vulnerable witnesses to give evidence directly to the court from a video link room within the REC. Attending the REC also ensures that the witness will not encounter the defendant or the defendant’s family / supporters in or around the court building on the day of the trial. RECs are intended to reduce the stress of giving evidence and increase victim confidence. This year, well over 500 vulnerable witnesses have attended a REC with the conviction rate for REC cases consistently sitting around 90%;The Belfast REC also hosts a monthly Domestic Abuse Contest Court (DACC) which sees multiple cases listed consecutively on the same day with all victims based in the REC. To date, there have been 14 DACC days and 69 cases listed and the conviction rate stands at 91%. Feedback from the complainants involved in the DACC has been universally positive, with many saying that without access to the REC, they would not have been able to give evidence or continue with their case.Measures under the Justice (Sexual Offences And Trafficking Victims) NI Act 2022 that make provision for the exclusion of the public from court in serious sexual offence trials and appeal hearings; for the extension of existing lifelong anonymity for victims until 25 years after death and for increased penalties for breach on anonymity; The establishment of the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) scheme to provide publicly funded legal advice to adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases. Since its

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps being taken (i) to support the PSNI to respond more effectively to domestic and sexual violence reports; and (ii) to encourage victims to come forward, especially in communities where reporting rates are low.
answer_text: I welcome the focus that the PSNI has placed on responding to domestic and sexual abuse. My Department is continuing to take forward a range of measures to support this and to encourage and support victims to report abuse and to participate in criminal proceedings. Specific examples of these steps include:My Department currently provides PSNI with annual funding to support the implementation of Gillen recommendations aimed at improving the police response to sexual violence;My Department is also currently working with PSNI to update Achieving Best Evidence practitioner guidance which will ensure that vulnerable victims and witnesses are supported effectively when providing evidence.I have introduced significant legislative changes, including new offences to tackle domestic abuse, stalking, non-fatal strangulation, voyeurism and cyber-flashing which have provided law enforcement with a wider suite of tools that can be used to hold perpetrators of domestic and sexual abuse to account.In addition, my Department is also working with PSNI and other criminal justice agencies to establish a pilot to test Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPN/O), which will provide police with additional powers to offer immediate protection to domestic abuse victims at the point of crisis; and My Department is leading a project to put in place new, enhanced arrangements to improve multi-agency risk assessment and mitigation for high risk victims of domestic abuse which is currently provided by the Multi Agency Risk Assessment Conference (MARAC) system.  The new arrangements will close identified weaknesses in the current process and will strengthen risk assessment, action plans and oversight, whilst also applying a wider whole family lens and considering how to reduce risk posed by perpetrators.Increasing reporting of domestic and sexual abuse, especially in communities where under-reporting is prevalent, remains a key priority for my Department. This work is guided by the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which is jointly led by my Department and the Department of Health. The strategy aims to create a safer, more supportive environment that encourages victims, especially in communities with low reporting rates, to come forward. By promoting trauma-informed support, improving trust in services, and reducing stigma through community-focused engagement, the strategy seeks to remove the barriers that prevent people from reporting abuse. This approach ensures victims feel believed, protected, and supported throughout the reporting and justice process. Specific steps include:Ongoing provision of specialist advocacy services, through ASSIST NI, to provide tailored support to high risk / high harm victims of domestic and sexual abuse throughout the justice journey. Advocates work closely with PSNI to improve safety planning, enhance communication, and ensure victims’ needs are understood and met. This partnership approach directly strengthens police responses and

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any new measures being implemented to support victims under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: The Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 represents a significant strengthening of protections for victims of domestic abuse, and the majority of its provisions are now in force and operating.However, there are a small number of important further measures still to be implemented, which will deliver additional support and safeguards for victims.A key provision is contained in section 27, which enables the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Domestic Abuse Protection Orders, known as DAPNs and DAPOs. These are intended to provide fast, flexible and victim-centred protection in the immediate aftermath of domestic abuse, including protective conditions and positive requirements on perpetrators. My Department continues to work closely with operational partners, including the PSNI and the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service, to develop a model that is both effective for victims and operationally sustainable in the Northern Ireland context. This work has been informed by fact-finding visits to pilot areas in England, including Croydon and Greater Manchester, with the intention of piloting DAPNs and DAPOs in Northern Ireland during the second half of the 2026/27 financial year.In addition, further protections are being developed under sections 36, 38 and 39, which will prohibit cross-examination in person by alleged perpetrators in family and civil proceedings and provide for special measures for victims in civil cases. These measures are particularly important in reducing re-traumatisation for victims engaging with the court process. Implementation work is currently focused on family proceedings, where the impact for victims will be greatest, and includes the development of court rules and secondary legislation, as well as arrangements for court-appointed legal representatives.Furthermore, through my Departments Enabling Access to Justice Programme I am exploring the introduction of a one-stop model of support for specific vulnerable groups starting with victims of domestic abuse.  The Programme also seeks to enhance access to justice by simplifying the process for accessing legal aid and ensuring that scope and financial eligibility rules are balanced in a way which enables the most acute needs to be addressed for the widest possible number of citizens, with specific focus on support for vulnerable groups.  This includes simplifying application processes and introducing measures to prevent perpetrators of domestic abuse weaponising legal aid to perpetuate abuse further through Article 8 Children Order proceedings.Taken together, these remaining provisions represent important next steps in strengthening victim protection, improving safety, and ensuring that victims of domestic abuse are better supported both at the point of crisis and throughout the justice process. My Department remains committed to progressing these measures as soon as practicable, subject to operational readiness and available r

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of buildings within his remit that currently utilise renewable energy sources; and (ii) the types of renewable technologies in use.
answer_text: One hundred and fifty-seven buildings within the estates of the Health and Social Care Trusts, Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, Northern Ireland Fire & Rescue Service and HSC Business Services Organisation utilise renewable energy sources. The types of renewable energy sources used include biomass, solar photovoltaic panels, air source heat pumps, solar thermal panels, and wind. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any support her Department provides to help young offenders reintegrate into education and employment.
answer_text: The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) is the primary provider of services to children aged 10 -17 who offend or are on the cusp of involvement in the justice system across Northern Ireland.  While YJA has no statutory responsibility for the provision of education, training and employment (ETE) to the children they work with, YJA recognises that ETE is one of the fundamental pillars that helps support children to desist from offending or re-offending.  YJA has recently appointed a specialist Senior Practitioner to promote and develop ETE throughout the Agency.  YJA has also developed relationships with the Education Authority, training providers and Labour Market Partnerships in each council areas in support of this work. In Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre, all children attend the on-site school. There is a 98% attendance rate. In addition to the provision of the mainstream school curriculum, a range of vocational learning opportunities are provided.  All children also have, as part of their discharge planning, an aim to have an identified ETE placement on release. Data for 2024/2025 show that on admission to Woodlands only 37% of children had an ETE placement. On discharge that figure had risen to 68%, a considerable achievement given many children are in Woodlands for a very short period.  In YJA’s community-based Youth Justice Services, a needs assessment is undertaken for every child.  This aims to assess the child’s wider needs, including any educational needs that may be contributing to their offending behaviour.  YJA then works with the family and other relevant agencies to access appropriate services. In 2024/25, ETE support was prioritised and delivered for 79% of children new to Youth Justice Services.  Survey feedback showed well over 90% of children and their parents either strongly agreed or agreed that YJA helped with ETE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of buildings within his remit that currently utilise renewable energy sources; and (ii) the types of renewable technologies in use.
answer_text: AnswerTwo buildings within my Department’s remit currently utilise renewable energy sources.  For both buildings, solar panels are the type of renewable technology in use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work being carried out between her Department and schools to prevent youth crime.
answer_text: As part of its earlier stage diversion work, the Youth Justice Agency (YJA) delivers targeted sessions in post-primary schools across Northern Ireland, educating children on a number of justice related topics, including internet safety and sexting, One Punch, anti-social behaviour, hate crime and vaping.  These sessions are often delivered in partnership with others, including PSNI.Sessions are responsive to emerging needs and schools either self-refer to the agency due to a specific issue or concern, or YJA makes contact with schools due to an issue presenting within the community where the school resides. So far this year, YJA has delivered 39 educational sessions to over 2000 post-primary school children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail, in the event that non-disclosure agreements are part of an Employment or Industrial Tribunal Case, the reason why they are not recorded as such in the judgment.
answer_text: The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal are independent judicial bodies that hear and determine claims concerning a range of employment matters.  		A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) is a private legal contract drawn up between parties.  The Tribunals do not play a role in the creation of NDAs, or have any jurisdiction to consider the substance of such agreements.  Therefore the Tribunal does not record the detail of an NDA within a judgment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the reason why employment tribunal cases that are settled involving a non-disclosure agreement are not recorded as such on the public register.
answer_text: Where parties agree to ‘settle’ a claim they must withdraw their claim and the Tribunal shall then issue a judgment ‘dismissing’ the claim.  Under the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020 as amended, judgments for withdrawn claims are exempt from the requirement to be recorded on the Register.		This exemption is intended to address concerns previously expressed by parties about withdrawn claims being visible to the public online and having unintended, potentially detrimental consequences for those involved. The public register will continue to contain substantive judgments and written reasons for the decisions made by the tribunal.		A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) is a private legal contract drawn up between parties, and is separate from any judgment made by a Tribunal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail whether the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020 contain any provisions that protect a complainant who has entered into a non-disclosure agreement from being disadvantaged in obtaining future employment.
answer_text: The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal are independent judicial bodies in the North that hear and determine claims concerning a range of employment matters.	The overriding objective of the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020, as amended, is to enable tribunals to deal with cases fairly and justly.			A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) is a legal contract drawn up between parties.   The Tribunals do not play any role in the creation of NDAs and these agreements are not subject to the Tribunal regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020 ensure that non-disclosure agreements do not cover up criminal activities.
answer_text: A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) is a legal contract drawn up between parties.   The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal do not play any role in the creation of NDAs and these agreements are not subject to the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (Constitution and Rules of Procedure) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how a claimant in an industrial tribunal, proves to any future employer that the judgment was a non-disclosure agreement which (a) does not proportion blame; and (b) does not implicate either the claimant or the respondent.
answer_text: The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal are independent judicial bodies in the North that hear and determine claims concerning employment matters. A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) is a private legal contract drawn up between parties, and is separate from any judgment made by a Tribunal.  The Tribunals do not play any role in the creation of such an agreement or how it may be presented to any future employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how the criminal justice system ensures that non-disclosure agreements are not covering up criminal activities.
answer_text: Policy or legislation governing the circumstances in which Non-Disclosure Agreements can be used are primarily employment matters and, as the Department of Justice does not have responsibility for the Industrial Tribunal and Fair Employment Tribunal, it does not hold any information on non-disclosure agreements. The Member may wish to write to the Department for the Economy for further information on NDAs in the employment context.However, the Criminal Law Act (Northern Ireland) 1967 places a duty on anyone who knows or believes that a ‘relevant offence’ has been committed, where they have information which is likely to assist the apprehension, prosecution or conviction of the person who committed the offence, to provide that information to the police within a reasonable time. Failure to provide such information without reasonable excuse is an offence under Section 5 of the Act.This duty is subject to the exemptions in Section 5 of the Act, including where a person has a reasonable excuse or where the injured party has been reasonably recompensed.Any provision in a non-disclosure agreement which prevents a person from reporting a crime to the police may be lawfully challenged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of non-disclosure agreements, in the last five years, broken down by sex.
answer_text: Policy or legislation governing the circumstances in which Non-Disclosure Agreements can be used are primarily employment matters and, as the Department of Justice does not have responsibility for the Industrial Tribunal and Fair Employment Tribunal, it does not hold any information on non-disclosure agreements.  The Member may wish to write to the Department for the Economy for further information on NDAs in the employment context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how non-disclosure agreements are publicly recorded, when they form part of an industrial tribunal.
answer_text: The Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunal do not play any role in the creation or regulation of non-disclosure agreements or in how they may be publicly recorded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the rationale for not naming a convicted murderer in a Domestic Homicide Review if the family of the victim have waived their right to anonymity.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the Paroles Commission inform families that an individual life term prisoner is not to be released (ii) does pre-release testing then return to phase 1 for that prisoner.
answer_text: Registered victims may request the Victims Information Unit to inform them of the outcome of a Parole Commissioners NI (PCNI) hearing.  When such a request is received, Victims Information Unit staff will contact the PCNI on their behalf and arrange for a summary of the decision to be provided to the victim.Conditions attached to Pre Release Testing (PRT) are dependent on the PCNI’s recommendations.  In most cases, if PCNI do not recommend release, they may make a recommendation for PRT and progression to phased testing at a working out unit at the appropriate time, as assessed by a Governor through appropriate risk assessment and case conferencing processes.  PCNI may also make further recommendations which can include, but are not limited to, engagement with psychology services, offending behaviour programmes, purposeful activity and maintaining good conduct.  NIPS will then either initiate or continue with the risk assessment process, in collaboration with the various departments, to determine if it is appropriate for PRT to commence or if the current pathway requires amendment.
