question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the legislative framework that covers pre-release testing; and (ii) who gives final authority for a life sentence prisoner to be allowed on unaccompanied day release, prior to the completion of their sentence tariff date.
answer_text: Rule 27 of the Prisons and Young Offenders Centres Rules (NI) 1995 provides the legislative authority for a sentenced prisoner to be temporarily released for any special purpose or to enable him to have health care, to engage in employment, to receive instruction or training or to assist him in his transition from prison to outside life.A prison governor gives authority for a life sentence prisoner to be temporarily released unaccompanied prior to their Tariff Expiry Date.  These decisions are informed by any relevant recommendations from the Parole Commissioners for Northern Ireland, a multi-disciplinary case conference and an appropriate and thorough assessment of risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has for the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough council area, broken down by household size.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The tables below provide waiting list data, as at end September 2025, for Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Local Government District.All ApplicantsElderlyLarge AdultLarge FamilySingleSmall AdultSmall FamilyTotalArmagh, Banbridge & Craigavon6101713571,7782269974,139Supporting Notes:Applicant Information is as at 30th September 2025.  Please note that household group is recorded at point of application and may not reflect the current composition of the household.Social housing waiting list and allocations data held by the Housing Executive, form part of the Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin, which is published quarterly as a National Statistic by the Department for Communities. Therefore, in order to avoid breaches of the code of practice for statistics, the most recent data provided is based on the most recently published data (end Sept 2025)The following table provides details for each household descriptor.Single Person Household(1 person aged 16-59)Small Adult Households(2 persons aged 16-59)Small Families(1 or 2 persons aged 16 or over and 1 or 2 child/children aged 0-15)Large Families(1 or 2 persons aged 16 or over and 3 or more children aged 0-15 or 3 or more persons aged 16 or over and 2 or more children aged 0-15)Large Adult Households(3 or more persons aged 16 or over with or without 1 child aged 0-15)Older Small Households(1 or 2 persons aged 16 or over.  1 or both of whom aged 60 or over)”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners that have been released on unaccompanied pre-release testing, in the last two years, where the victims have not been informed.
answer_text: I am aware of only two cases where Registered Victims were not informed that a prisoner had been granted Pre Release Testing in the last two years.  One of the cases refers to the Cawdery family with whom I have met to apologise in person and to listen to their concerns.  Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) officials have ensured all of these victims are now in receipt of all the information they are entitled to under the Prisoner Release Victim Information Scheme (PRVIS). In response to this case, the Director General of NIPS ordered a review of systems and records to establish if any other victims have not been informed when a prisoner has participated in pre-release testing.  This exercise is ongoing and will be completed in the coming weeks. In relation to the only other case of which I am aware, I wrote to the Registered Victims to apologise and explain the circumstances in their case.  The error in this case related to retrospective information only. While the victims registered in July 2025 and were informed of any periods of release after that date, they should have also been informed of periods prior to their registration in July; however, this did not happen until October 2025.  They are now in full receipt of all information and will continue to be informed of any future releases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will ensure that transformation plans focus on prevention and community care rather than just managing hospital pressures.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has considered piloting the use of Artificial Intelligence or digital risk analysis tools, to complement existing AccessNI vetting processes.
answer_text: AccessNI remains committed to exploring advancements in technology and process improvement.  However, at present, there are no plans to introduce artificial intelligence or digital risk analysis tools into its operational framework.It is important to clarify that AccessNI does not undertake vetting of applicants.  Instead, it provides disclosure certificates directly to applicants.  Theses certificates support employers and voluntary organisations in making more-informed recruitment decisions but AccessNI itself does not participate in the decision-making process.AccessNI maintains regular engagement with its regional counterparts - the Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) in England and Wales and Disclosure Scotland- to discuss innovation, strategic planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether, in light of the successful passage of the Assembly motion in support of service leavers, veterans, and their families through the Armed Forces Covenant, he will appoint an armed forces liaison officer within his department.
answer_text: Since 2022, UK public authorities responsible for education, health and housing services have had a duty to have regard to the principles of The Armed Forces Covenant. In Northern Ireland, the responsible authorities are the Housing Executive, the Education Authority and Boards of Governors of Grant-aided schools, and relevant health bodies including the Department of Health and Health Trusts. I am committed to ensuring that my Department effectively supports members of our armed forces community. I recently convened a meeting of our local council Veterans Champions to discuss future opportunities for my Department and the support we give our veteran community. Work is ongoing to assign an Armed Forces Liaison Officer and I will be making an announcement on this in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether, in light of the successful passage of the Assembly motion in support of service leavers, veterans, and their families through the Armed Forces Covenant, he will appoint an armed forces liaison officer within his department.
answer_text: While the Department does not have a designated Armed Forces Liaison Officer, a Senior Official within the Department holds policy responsibility for Children of Service Personnel, including oversight of the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant, within their broader remit.I am acutely aware of the unique and often complex challenges faced by children from Armed Forces families. These can include frequent relocations, disruptions to schooling, and the emotional impact of parental deployment. Such factors can significantly affect a child’s educational experience and outcomes. I am committed to ensuring that both the Department and the Education Authority do all that they can to support these children in accessing high-quality education and in fulfilling their potential.Officials will shortly be submitting proposals concerning the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant in areas that fall within my remit. I expect to be able to share further information in due course regarding the Department’s support measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any cross-departmental work being undertaken between her Department, the Department of Education and the Department of Health, to address emerging digital safeguarding risks.
answer_text: AccessNI is in regular contact with officials from the Department of Education and the Department of Health and participates in a safeguarding forum to discuss and resolve areas of mutual interest.  There is some crossover with AccessNI and Department of Health as the Department of Health owns the safeguarding legislation (Safeguarding Vulnerable Groups Order (NI) 2007) and defines what is known as Regulated Activity.  AccessNI may from time to time seek clarification on issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will consider a (i) review; or (ii) public consultation, on modernising AccessNI, to reflect new and emerging forms of harm.
answer_text: I consider that the AccessNI disclosure service continues to perform strongly in fulfilling its statutory functions. AccessNI is currently progressing plans to onboard to the Law Enforcement Data Service (LEDS) and the Police National Database (PND), alongside ongoing work to develop a mechanism for continuous monitoring of disclosure certificates.While I remain committed to identifying opportunities for continuous improvement, I do not intend to initiate a broader review of the AccessNI service at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how examination boards and teachers identify whether students have been using artificial intelligence to do their coursework; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to introduce measures to support examination boards and teachers in detecting work that has been produced using artificial intelligence and subsequently edited to humanise it.
answer_text: CCEA, like other awarding bodies in the UK, adheres to the regulations and guidance set by the Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ). This provides a common framework for administering qualifications to ensure the consistent approach towards all candidates taking external examinations in the United Kingdom.  Guidance relating to CCEA qualifications references and aligns with the JCQ guidance for schools and colleges on the permitted use and detection of the misuse of AI by students in their coursework.  Further information on the JCQ AI guidance is available at:https://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice/artificial-intelligence/As JCQ has already issued comprehensive guidance on the use of AI in assessments, including how to detect and respond to AI-generated or AI-edited student work, it would not be appropriate for my Department to introduce separate or additional measures related to the conduct of coursework undertaken as part of examinations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether Artificial Intelligence will be expanded into the school curriculum, including in literacy teachings and student support, to create personalised teaching for students, adapted to the needs of the student.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is already being used in schools across Northern Ireland to support teaching and learning. This includes personalised learning tools, literacy support, and real-time feedback that helps to identify pupil development and areas for improvement. Further, following a Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, my department has launched a major curriculum reform programme, led by a new Curriculum Taskforce. This includes development of a digital skills framework to support teaching of coding, computational thinking, data handling, and digital literacy, including AI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of enforcement cases for overhanging (i) hedges; and (ii) trees, that have been opened in the Upper Bann area, in the last three years; and of these (ii) the number of cases that have resulted in further action being taken.
answer_text: There are only a limited number of trees in my Department’s ownership.  The ownership and primary maintenance responsibility for almost all roadside trees lies with the adjacent landowner.During regular routine maintenance inspections, officials will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction; obscuring road signs; or have the potential to create danger for the road user. In those instances, landowners will be contacted and asked to address the issue.  If the landowner fails to respond, the Department can instigate legal action to have the danger addressed, although this action is not usually required. The Department also places notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding landowners of their responsibility and the need to carry out maintenance during the early part of the year, avoiding the bird nesting season from 1 March to 31 August. My Department has issued numerous written notifications to landowners, urging them to carry out the necessary remedial work. Alongside this, officials have also held direct discussions with landowners to clarify their responsibilities. In general, this resulted in the necessary work being done without the need to revert to a formal process.The Department does not hold information on a constituency basis, however in the last three years, my Department has not taken forward any formal enforcement cases for overgrown hedges or trees in the Upper Bann area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment on whether AccessNI provides schools, charities, and border control agencies with sufficient assurance, in the context of evolving online and digital threats.
answer_text: Response to AQW 34417 and 34418 also refers.AccessNI’s role is to undertake the disclosure of criminal history information in Northern Ireland in accordance with provisions in Part V of the Police Act 1997.  AccessNI seeks to enable employers and voluntary organisations in Northern Ireland to make safer and more-informed recruitment decisions. This role does not extend to investigating or commenting on allegations relating to online or digital threats or other offences.AccessNI collates criminal history information from central records and third-party law enforcement agencies and does not communicate disclosure information to anyone other than the applicant through the normal application process. Where AccessNI discovers, through its searches as part of the application process, that an applicant is seeking to, or has been working in regulated activity whilst barred from doing so, it will inform Disclosure & Barring Service for appropriate action.AccessNI fulfils its full statutory functions in processing applications for Enhanced checks.  These functions extend to:-Disclosure of criminal record information, subject to filtering rules, including convictions, cautions and other non-court disposals.The disclosure of barred list information where the applicant is present on a Disclosure and Barring Service, and/or Scottish Government barred list.Disclosure of relevant police (and other law enforcement) information which the Chief Officer determines ought to be disclosed in the interests of safeguarding or public safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the mechanisms in place to ensure AccessNI captures safeguarding intelligence from across the United Kingdom and internationally, particularly in cases where applicants have worked or lived overseas.
answer_text: In undertaking Enhanced disclosure checks, AccessNI searches the Police Local Exchange (PLX) database.  This is a UK-wide nominal names index that is used to identify which police forces, and other law enforcement agencies, hold records / information about named individuals.   If AccessNI identifies a positive ‘match’ on the PLX database, a referral is issued to that police force (or other law enforcement agency) so that they can consider if the information they hold is relevant to the role the individual is seeking to undertake, and ought to be disclosed.AccessNI makes referrals to An Garda Síochána if the Enhanced disclosure applicant has lived in the Republic of Ireland within the last five years.AccessNI also makes referrals to one of 15 EU countries if the Enhanced disclosure applicant is seeking to work with children in Northern Ireland and that EU country is their country of origin.  The 15 EU countries are France, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Portugal, Slovakia, Spain, Bulgaria, Hungary, Latvia, Poland, Romania, Czech Republic, Greece, and the Netherlands.In addition, through ACRO Criminal Records Office (ACRO), arrangements are in place to ensure that the Police National Computer (PNC) is updated to include details of UK nationals convicted of offences in the EU.There are currently no other mechanisms used by AccessNI to avail of international criminal history information.  AccessNI advice to employers and voluntary / community organisations is that they should ask potential employees / volunteers from overseas to obtain a Certificate of Good Conduct from the relevant Embassy or High Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how AccessNI accounts for individuals engaged in online (i) grooming; (ii) radicalisation; and (iii) exploitation, who have not yet come to the PSNI's attention.
answer_text: Response to AQW 34417 also refers.   A comprehensive safeguarding approach requires all society to respond in the correct way when matters of concern are identified with regards to the care of vulnerable individuals.  Only by reporting to police and making a Disclosure and Barring Service referral will AccessNI be able to pick up such matters and ensure they are considered as part of the Enhanced disclosure process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how AccessNI integrates information from national safeguarding bodies, such as the National Crime Agency and the Child Exploitation and Online Protection Centre.
answer_text: Response to AQW 34420 refers.  Both the National Crime Agency and the Child Exploitation and Online Protection Centre provide nominal data into the Police Local Exchange (PLX) database.  AccessNI referrals are issued accordingly, based on confirmed matches identified on the PLX.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any plans to expand the definition of relevant information, disclosed by AccessNI, to include verified digital or behavioural risk indicators.
answer_text: Anyone with concerns about another person’s digital or behavioural risk indicators should report such matters to the police without delay.  The police will then determine if the information is relevant and ought to be disclosed on an enhanced disclosure certificate.Employers, Statutory Bodies and voluntary / community organisations should also consider whether it would be appropriate to make a referral to the Disclosure and Barring Service – the DBS can then consider the need for the individual to be added to a barred list, which can, in turn, be picked up by AccessNI when processing Enhanced disclosure checks.To be clear, the role of AccessNI is in issuing criminal record certificates following an appropriate application; it does not manage wider safeguarding or determine what is relevant information – this is the role of the police force.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of AccessNI’s ability to identify safeguarding risks, in cases when no criminal conviction or formal police record exists.
answer_text: AccessNI fulfils its full statutory functions in processing applications for Enhanced checks.  These functions extend to:-Disclosure of criminal record information, subject to filtering rules, including convictions, cautions and other non-court disposals.The disclosure of barred list information where the applicant is present on a Disclosure and Barring Service, and/or Scottish Government barred list.Disclosure of relevant police (and other law enforcement) information which the Chief Officer determines ought to be disclosed in the interests of safeguarding or public safety.The AccessNI disclosure process is not meant to be seen as a ‘one-stop’ safeguarding service.  Employers, community / voluntary and statutory organisations all have key roles to play in protecting the vulnerable groups in their care, including making barring referrals to the Disclosure and Barring Service if any concerning matters or behaviours come to their attention.  This collaborative approach is designed to reduce safeguarding gaps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to reintroduce the league table of those firms who have received numeration from the legal aid budget.
answer_text: I set out my intention to reintroduce the publication of ranked payments for the highest-earning legal aid practitioners in Northern Ireland in 2021. Under my direction, the Legal Service Agency has been working to compile a comprehensive archive of annual legal aid payments at supplier level.  This commenced with the 2015/16 financial year, the point at which previous series was halted, and will form the basis for future releases.While this exercise has taken longer than initially anticipated, primarily due to complexities involved in reconciling multiple financial databases covering the 2019/20 financial year - the year in which the new, digital legal aid case management system (LAMS) was introduced - the Agency has now reached a position whereby it can initiate the first steps in the process to publicly release this information.However, as there are two legal challenges before the courts which may have an impact on publication, I do not intend to formally publish a road map which will set out timelines for publication of the information in respect of these years until these cases conclude.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of early guilty pleas for sexual offences, registered prior to arraignment; (ii) the number of late guilty pleas registered for the same offences; and (ii) the total number of not guilty pleas, over the last three years.
answer_text: In Crown Court cases, arraignment is the first formal opportunity to register a guilty plea.  Figures for cases where a guilty plea was entered at that point, compared to cases where a guilty plea was entered at a time subsequent to that date, and where no guilty plea was entered at all, have been provided in the following table.  Number of cases where a guilty plea was entered in sexual offences cases dealt with at Crown Court, 2022/23 to 2024/25YearGuilty Plea entered at arraignment dateGuilty Plea entered after arraignment dateNo guilty Plea EnteredTotal cases2022/235941482472023/249901212202024/251370118201Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where the main offence at disposal was a sexual offence.3. Figures for guilty pleas relate to cases where a guilty plea was entered at arraignment date or at any subsequent point.4. Figures for cases where a guilty plea was entered relate to cases where a guilty plea was entered against all offences.Where no guilty plea was entered for cases dealt with at Crown Court and where the main offence was a sexual offence, a number of cases still resulted in a conviction: 72 cases in 2022/23, 57 cases in 2023/24; and 62 cases in 2024/25.A number of cases where a sexual offence was the main offence at disposal were also dealt with at magistrates’ courts over the period.   Figures for the number of cases where a guilty plea was entered at some point, and where no guilty plea was entered, have been provided.  Number of cases where a guilty plea was entered in sexual offences cases dealt with at adult magistrates' courts, 2022/23 to 2024/25YearGuilty Plea enteredNo Guilty Plea EnteredTotal cases2022/23481091572023/24461101562024/2548113161Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where the main offence at disposal was a sexual offence and where a guilty plea was entered at any point.3. Figures relate to cases dealt with at adult magistrates’ courts.4. Figures for cases where a guilty plea was entered relate to cases where a guilty plea was entered against all offences.Where no guilty plea was entered for cases dealt with at adult magistrates’ courts and where the main offence was a sexual offence, a number of cases still resulted in a conviction: 48 cases in 2022/23, 47 cases in 2023/24; and 59 cases in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the reasons for the imbalance between the sexes of those who have applied for and been awarded legal aid in 2024-25; and (ii) whether these differences translate in civil and criminal cases.
answer_text: The latest Official Statistics published by the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland, in September 2025, details that the 2024/25 legal aid grant rate for males was 45.9 grants per 1,000 population, compared to 18.5 per 1,000 population for females. This disparity is primarily driven by the significantly higher number of criminal legal aid applications involving male defendants.When disaggregated by type of legal aid, statistics show that civil legal aid grant rates were similar for males and females - 10.3 and 10.5 per 1,000 population, respectively. However, in criminal cases, males were over four times more likely than females to be recipients of legal aid, displaying grant rates of 35.6 per 1,000 population compared to 8.1 per 1,000 population for females.This difference in criminal legal aid uptake is reflective of trends across criminal justice organisations – for example, Public Prosecution Service Official Statistics that show males are statistically more likely to be involved in criminal proceedings that result in subsequent grants of criminal legal aid.In Northern Ireland, civil legal aid is granted by the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland under a statutory framework designed to ensure access to justice for individuals who are deemed eligible through a combination of means testing and merits assessment. In contrast, criminal legal aid in Northern Ireland is granted by the judiciary if the individual has insufficient means to fund their own defence and if it desirable in the interests of justice to do so. In both of these scenarios, the sex of any applicant is irrelevant to the adjudication process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the level of fraud and error found in the 2024-25 legal aid budget; and (i) the amount that has been recovered.
answer_text: Within the realm of legal aid, fraud and error can result from three potential sources:Official error – where the financial eligibility of an applicant is incorrectly determined resulting in either an erroneous grant of legal aid, or a miscalculation in expected contribution.  It may also be evident through an error in a payment made to a supplier;Practitioner error and fraud – by inaccurate claims or claims with insufficient supporting evidence from members of the legal profession carrying out legal aid work; andApplicant fraud and error – by people who are/were in receipt of legal aid services.To estimate the level of fraud and error in the legal aid budget, the Legal Services Agency NI work in collaboration with the Standards Assurance Unit (SAU) of the Department for Communities (DfC) to test and validate a representative sample of legal aid payments on a monthly basis.  This statistically-based approach is similar to that used by DfC, to monitor the levels of fraud and error within the wider benefits system in Northern Ireland.  Determination of fraud by SAU is based on a methodological definition used in DfC and does not constitute a legal definition. In terms of part (i), the latest estimates of fraud and error, covering the 2024 calendar year, are set out in detail at pages 51 to 56 of the LSA’s 2024/25 Annual Report and Accounts. The reported estimates of Official and Practitioner Fraud and Error presented in the Annual Report and Accounts have been extrapolated using raw findings from the 712 cases sampled during 2024 to estimate the total errors across the full population of eligible payments authorised by LSA in that reporting year. Thus, any recoupment can only be applied against any of these 712 sampled cases. Focusing on Official Error, annual estimates include “deemed” as well as “notional” errors:Deemed errors – spend that cannot be verified as either correct or incorrect as a result of further information being required but not provided within the reporting year; andNotional errors – spend that appears to be incorrect at the point in time of SAU testing but, by year-end, and through following embedded business processes, did not result in any error or loss to the legal aid fund or practitioners. As deemed errors, by nature, cannot be verified as incorrect and notional errors are naturally corrected in-year, both fall outside of recoupment parameters.  In 2024, this resulted in a raw monetary value of error within sampled cases of £79,919, (excluding deemed and notional errors) which is comprised of both underpayments (£45,172) and overpayments (£34,748). The individual cases which comprise these amounts are then considered against the LSANI - Fees Adjustment & Recoupment of Overpayment Policy and recouped if appropriate. There is a time lag in processing the recoupment of overpayments as not all cases for the 2024 sample will be returned until April 2025 and if a recoupment has been identified practitioners have the statutor

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she will take to stem the percentage decrease of young people who feel protected by the law and justice system, in their local area.
answer_text: Through the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), the Department of Justice has been working to help embed a public health approach to violence prevention. This whole of government approach enables the Executive to understand the root causes of violence and vulnerability among young people and to design targeted interventions that build protective factors, reduce exploitation, and foster resilience. The Programme invests in a range of youth initiatives that support vulnerable young people, including targeted and agile youth outreach work and practical support, as well as community-based support for education; these are evidence-based, cross-sectoral initiatives which help to improve young people’s perceptions of safety and justice. EPPOC is committed to embedding and scaling what works; these efforts are central to building trust, reducing harm, and ensuring that all young people feel protected by the law in their communities. I would take this opportunity to reiterate my strong belief that the paramilitary interlocuter plans being progressed by the UK and Irish Governments, and supported by other parties in the Assembly, risks undermining this important work.My Department alongside the NI Policing Board, via a Joint Committee, also provides funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) which are located in each council area. PCSPs engage with local communities, including young people, to identify and help address community safety issues and confidence in policing. PCSP membership comprises of elected councillors and Independent members as well as seven Designated Organisations which include the PSNI, Youth Justice Agency, Probation Board NI, Health Trusts, NI Housing Executive, Education Authority (EA) and NI Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS). There are many PCSP initiatives in each local area which directly engage with young people to help identify and address all aspects of community safety issues of concern and aid an understanding of the wider criminal justice system. Initiatives include youth engagement and diversionary programmes, and education and awareness raising forums and events, including many to address confidence in policing. A recent example is the delivery of Shantallow Community Resident’s Association (SCRA) Youth Educated in Safety (YES) Project to over 1,700 young people across the district.  This project brought together pupils from a number of schools to a community venue where they participated in interactive multi-agency workshops from a range of statutory and community organisations, including Community Safety Wardens, PSNI, NIFRS, EA, youth workers and the council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions she will take to address the increase of those who feel paramilitary groups create fear and intimidation in their local area.
answer_text: I have consistently said that there is and never has been any place in our society for paramilitary, terrorist or organised crime groups. Addressing the fear and harm caused by these groups remains a priority for the Executive and is a Programme for Government commitment.As a result of work undertaken through the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) we have ensured that questions about fear and intimidation are included in the Northern Ireland and Young Life and Times Surveys. This helps us to understand the extent of this challenge, how the situation is evolving over time and whether positive change is taking hold. It is clear drawing on many data sources that projects such as those delivered via EPPOC are effective in addressing fear and intimidation but that it remains a significant challenge. Wider EPPOC initiatives such as its recent reports on  trauma,  Child Criminal Exploitation and Drug Related Intimidation are helping to drive better system-wide responses beyond programmatic investment. It is important, however, to note that EPPOC alone cannot address this problem. I have been consistent in making the point that a whole of government approach which implements a public health response to the harm caused by paramilitaries is required. While EPPOC can and does address fear and intimidation, it is a complex problem that requires all of us to address the conditions that allow paramilitarism and organised crime to persist in our communities. I also believe that the intimidation and coercive control paramilitary groups exercise over local communities is enabled when they are treated like legitimate negotiating partners in group transition processes or stakeholders by politicians and statutory bodies. I have been unequivocal in rejecting the latest UK and Irish Government plans for a paramilitary interlocutor and would encourage every other party and representative in the Assembly to do so as well.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reoffending rates of (i) adult; and (ii) youth, offenders beyond the one-year observation period, over the last two years.
answer_text: Given the need for a balance between timely information and making the reoffending rate as meaningful as possible, Official Statistics for reoffending are measured and reported using a one year proven reoffending rate across Northern Ireland and, more widely, across Great Britain. The Department does not routinely report on reoffending beyond this methodological approach.With the above in mind, we have used data from the two most recent cohorts which can be used to calculate estimated reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for those released from custody or given a non-custodial disposal in 2020/21 and 2021/22. Estimated reoffending rates for these individuals are detailed below.Table 1: Adult reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for the 2020/21 and 2021/22 cohortsYearAdults in cohortAdults who reoffended within one yearAdultone-year proven reoffending rate (%)Adults who reoffended within two yearsAdulttwo-year proven reoffending rate (%)Adults who reoffended within three yearsAdult three-year proven reoffending rate (%)2020/2114,9232,75818.5%4,59530.8%5,55737.2%2021/2218,3353,18417.4%5,52830.1%N/AN/ATable 2: Youth reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for the 2020/21 and 2021/22 cohortsYearYouths in cohortYouths who reoffended within one yearYouthone-year proven reoffending rate (%)Youths who reoffended within two yearsYouthtwo-year proven reoffending rate (%)Youths who reoffended within three yearsYouth three-year proven reoffending rate (%)2020/2170817725.0%26637.6%31344.2%2021/2286120223.5%34239.7%N/AN/ANote:An offence is counted as a reoffence if (i) it occurs within the given period following custody release or non-custodial disposal, (ii) it has been committed in Northern Ireland, (iii) it has been prosecuted via PSNI and not a third party, (iv) the offence is not a breach offence, and (v) the offence has been ‘proven’, meaning that a court conviction or diversionary disposal has been imposed.Adults are defined as those aged 18 over and youths are defined as those aged 17 and under on release from custody or receipt of the disposal which led to inclusion in the annual cohort.Differences in the offending related characteristics of those included in each cohort make comparing reoffending rates problematic, across cohorts and jurisdictions.My Department continues to work on multiple fronts to address and reduce reoffending rates. This process requires buy-in and coordination across multiple policy areas, including many that rest with other Executive departments. I must also highlight that one of the most effective ways of reducing reoffending is the use of a range of appropriate sentencing options which facilitate meaningful rehabilitation, including in the community. The pursuit of lower reoffending rates would be greatly aided if all stakeholders were committed to that evidence-based approach, and it is therefore regrettable that some choose to focus instead on the misguided and unsub

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any targeted reforms being prioritised to improve fiscal sustainability, while protecting frontline services such as health and justice.

To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 31355 22-27, to detail (i) the three areas of good practice; (i) the three areas deemed as weaknesses; and (ii) given that parole reports are based on finding from pre-release testing, why the Parole Commission was not kept informed of the reviews outcome.
answer_text: To disclose further detail in respect of the findings of this review, could undermine the benefits it had in strengthening NIPS procedures, by placing sensitive decision-making factors considered by NIPS into the public domain. .  The findings were not relevant to the responsibilities or actions of the Parole Commissioners for Northern Ireland and, therefore, were not shared for that reason.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 32318 22-27, for her assessment of the McCullough Review report into the conduct of the PSNI.
answer_text: As I outlined in my previous response to the Member, my assessment is that the McCullough Review was both thorough and detailed.  While the Review found no evidence of any systemic surveillance of journalists, it did not amount to a clean bill of health and highlighted several instances of unlawful surveillance and poor practice.  I believe it is important that the Policing Board is now given the time it has requested to review the report’s findings, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for formal implementation of the recommendations, before taking a decision on next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the processes for removing someone’s liberty for safeguarding reasons if they are deemed not competent due to mental health reasons; and (i) whether the process is (a) ECHR Article 14; and (b) ECHR Article 7 compliant.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to outline the processes for removing someone’s liberty for safeguarding reasons if they are deemed not competent due to mental health reasons; and (i) whether the process is (a) ECHR Article 14; and (b) ECHR Article 7 compliant.
answer_text: The Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 provides the legislative basis for the detention of people who are not deemed competent due to mental health reasons. The Department of Health (DoH) leads on policy, implementation, and oversight of the Order, including the development of the Code of Practice. You may, therefore, wish to raise your query with the Health Minister.The Department of Justice (DoJ) has powers under Part III of the Order, which concerns individuals on remand for criminal proceedings or under sentence.  Given these individuals are already in the custodial environment, their liberty is not removed when the DoJ discharges functions under this part of the Order.There are a variety of safeguards contained within the Order, including medical assessments, reviews by a dedicated tribunal and rights of appeal, which underpin compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR), including Article 7 (no punishment without law) and Article 14 (prohibition of discrimination).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline a timeline of the planning meetings (i) organised; or (ii) attended by (a) his officials; (b) or members of the Education Authority, in relation to Storm Amy
answer_text: I have commissioned a review following Storm Amy to consider the communication methods used by the Education Authority and to fully assess the decision-making processes that were utilised. Members of the Education Authority attended a range of meetings following the upgrade of the storm impacts to an amber wind warning.  The timeline of planning meetings is outlined below: -02/10/2025 at 2130 – a Local Impact Assessment Call (LIAC) indicated a decision to revert to PSNI Tactical Coordination Group (TCG) coordination due to risk to life potential impacts.02/10/2025 - 2310 - Follow up to LIAC chair with the Early Closure decision03/10/2025 - 0750 - Met Office - Confirmation of Weather Impacts03/10/2025 - 0845 – meeting of the Multi-agency Tactical Coordination Group03/10/2025 - 1000 – meeting of EA’s Tactical Coordination Group03/10/2025 - 1400 – meeting of EA’s Tactical Coordination Group03/10/2025 - 1430 – meeting of EA’s Multi-agency Strategic Coordination Group03/10/2025 - 1900 – meeting of the Multi-agency Tactical Coordination Group04/10/2025 - 0930 – meeting of the Multi-agency Tactical Coordination Group04/10/2025 - 1100 – meeting of EA’s Tactical Coordination GroupA review of the emergency decision-making protocols is currently underway to ensure that our procedures are robust and fit for purpose in responding to future storms or severe weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the action he is taking to increase tree coverage in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department offers a number of support schemes to encourage landowners to plant new woodlands. The Department’s Forest Service provides grant aid under the Forest Expansion Scheme for a wide range of woodland types and also through the Small Woodland Grant Scheme aimed specifically at creating new native woodlands. In addition, earlier this year my Department launched its Farming with Nature Transition Scheme to support landowners to integrate nature-based solutions within their business activities. Applications have already been received that include the tree planting option and we expect to see these being taken forward in the forthcoming planting season. Forest Service also seeks opportunities, where appropriate, to acquire additional land for planting to help increase the overall forest cover in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the benefits of the Causeway system.
answer_text: AQO 2515 22-27 - ANSWER The Causeway Data Sharing Mechanism is strategically critical to the Northern Ireland criminal justice sector. It is a complex, bespoke database and digital service which provides real-time information sharing between the main NI Criminal Justice Organisations. Causeway is managed by the Department of Justice on behalf of the department, Police Service of Northern Ireland, Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunal Service, Forensic Science Northern Ireland, Northern Ireland Prison Service, Public Prosecution Service, and Probation Board for Northern Ireland.   To facilitate sharing of criminal justice case information digitally these Criminal Justice Organisations have configured their own systems to communicate and receive messages, to and from other criminal justice partners.   Causeway enables information created and shared by one Criminal Justice Organisation to be shared with or made immediately available for use by the others, based on agreed sharing rules.  Causeway also creates, maintains, and delivers digital access to the Northern Ireland Criminal History Database, which is accessed via the Criminal Record Viewer by organisations in Northern Ireland and Great Britain that have a legitimate right to access this information. The Criminal Record Viewer is automatically generated from Court results, non-court disposals and other relevant processes messaged into Causeway.   From its inception Causeway has delivered multiple benefits to the criminal justice system including: real time access to a single view of case information for Causeway users;  increased accuracy and consistency of data;  digitalisation of services resulting in a significant reduction in the use of paper;  efficiencies including reduction in manual processing and removal of duplication of processes and information;  improved collaboration with Criminal Justice Organisation partners; and  assisting in criminal justice related decision-making process through use of enhanced data analytics on the aggregated data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number outstanding warrants for individuals who were under the supervision of the Northern Ireland Probation Board.
answer_text: The Police Service Northern Ireland (PSNI) and Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) work closely together to address outstanding warrants and review each case to consider how best to manage them.  As at the 06 October 2025, there were 391 PBNI individual service users with outstanding orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of the legal aid budget for 2024-25 that was used for cases related to the legacy of the troubles.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency records and administers legal aid cases on the basis of specific Natures and Matters.  In turn, and due to the resultant formatting of data within the legal aid case management system, it is not possible to identify or extract cases which may be ‘related to the legacy of the troubles’ as such a generic classification does not exist in the database.  Accordingly, it is not possible to attribute legal aid expenditure exclusively to these case types.However, it is possible to extract costs of legacy inquests, which can be objectively identified from a list of cases originally drawn up by the former Lord Chief Justice, and which continues to be maintained. Under this definition, a total of £2,882,324 was expended on legacy inquests during 2024/25.

answer_text: (i)(a)	Information on the number of prosecutions and convictions at courts for non-fatal strangulation offences in the period to end 2024, the most recent point for which statistics are available has been provided in the following table.Prosecutions and convictions at courts for non-fatal strangulation offences, 2023 – 2024Court TypeProsecutionsConvictionsCrown154Magistrates'32965Total34469Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a prosecution or conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. Figures for magistrates’ courts include adult and youth magistrates’ courts.(i)(b) Main Disposal type imposed following conviction for non-fatal strangulation offences, 2023 – 2024Main DisposalCrown CourtMagistrates' CourtsAll CourtsImprisonment43438Suspended Imprisonment-2121Community Sentence-##Monetary penalty---Discharge/Other-**Total convictions46569Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was a prosecution or conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. Figures relate to main disposal type imposed at conviction. *  means a figure of less than 3 has been treated under rules of disclosure. #  means a figure of 3 or more has been treated to prevent disclosure of a small number elsewhere.(ii) All cases dealt with at Crown Court resulted in a custodial sentence, ranging from a Determinate Custodial Sentence of 3 years 8 months, of which 1 year 8 months was to be spent in custody to 2 years 6 months, of which 1 year 3 months was to be spent in custody. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the (i) findings; and (ii) conclusions, of the McCullough Review report.
answer_text: I wish to place on record my thanks to Angus McCullough KC and his team for what is a thorough and detailed review and to the group of experts and stakeholders for their contribution.While the McCullough Review found no evidence of systemic surveillance of journalists, which is to be welcomed, it did not amount to a clean bill of health, with several findings of poor or unlawful practice.  This is not solely a matter of operational policing, for which the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, but also impacts on wider issues of confidence. The Policing Board has stated that it views the Report as part of fulfilling its duties under Section 59 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.  The Board has made clear it wishes to carefully review the findings of the McCullough Review, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for implementing the recommendations, before deciding on next steps.  I believe it is important that the Board is given the time and space to fully consider the report and its preferred way forward. As I have said previously, I share the serious concerns which have been expressed by the Board in relation to this issue and stand ready to support the Board in whatever actions it considers necessary.  In addition, the Investigatory Powers Tribunal has yet to report, after which a further update to McCullough will be made. Ahead of the Tribunal findings and the Board’s considerations, it would be premature to comment on what actions may be required on my part.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on an action plan for perinatal and paediatric pathology services in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he will take to amend the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to allow legal authority for bonfires.
answer_text: The Waste and Contaminated land Order 1997 is primary legislation designed, in part, to tackle the illegal dumping of waste and thereby protect the environment. As such, I have no plans to amend the 1997 Order to provide an exemption for any activity that could present a risk to the environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an assessment has been made on (i) the effects on the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 if all bonfires were given legal authority; and (i) whether legal authority is compatible with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: No assessment has been made by DAERA on the effects on the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 if all bonfires were given legal authority, or whether any proposed legal authority would be compatible with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to move the Rathfriland depot bus stop, to the Hilltown Road junction, to mitigate the health and safety concerns of buses having to turn in a built-up pedestrianised street.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink currently has no plans to relocate the bus stop at Rathfriland Bus Depot to the Hilltown Road junction as this would inconvenience customers, requiring them to walk a significant distance to gain access to the bus service.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the outcomes of the review held at HMP Magilligan, following the prisoner who absconded for nine months while on unaccompanied day release; and (i) when these findings were passed to other prison establishments, including the Paroles Commissioners and the Probation Board.
answer_text: An internal review conducted by a senior Northern Ireland Prison Service Governor, with extensive experience within the resettlement field, highlighted three areas of good practice in respect of the management of this individual. Three weaknesses, across different departments, were identified and remedial actions put in place. This learning was disseminated to other prison establishments.  PBNI officers within Magilligan were made aware of the findings of this operational review of process that required remedial action.  The outcomes were not shared with Parole Commissioners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the timeframe for each of the three phases of the pre-release testing arrangements for prisoners in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Pre-release testing (PRT) forms a fundamental part of the preparatory work a prisoner undertakes when the timing of their release on licence is subject to a direction from the Parole Commissioner of NI (PCNI). Prisoners who undergo PRT have usually been committed to prison for long periods and will be subject to either a Life Sentence, an Indeterminate Custodial Sentence or an Extended Custodial Sentence.Pre-Release Testing (PRT) begins at the three-year pre-tariff date. At that point the focus is on multi-disciplinary risk management to agree the individual pre-release testing pathway. Once agreed, the first 15-month period encompasses a series of Accompanied and Unaccompanied Temporary Release (ATR/UTR) activities, allowing the prisoner the opportunity to demonstrate responsibility and progression.During the second fifteen-month period a structured programme of Unaccompanied Temporary Release (UTR) activities takes place, with the duration of release extended if steady progress is made. The three stages of UTR testing are: 8-hour UTR; 24-hour UTR and 48-hour UTR and there will be multiple occasions at each level.Following successful completion of the UTR activities, and taking account of recommendations made by PCNI, the prisoner can then progress to Open Conditions which is delivered in three phases:Phase 1: This is normally up to four weeks to allow the prisoner to get their personal affairs in place such as Bank Account, GP Registration, etc;Phase 2: This is a period of 6 – 9 months where the prisoner works in the community full time; andPhase 3:  This is the remaining period up until release from custody, either by PCNI on licence or through being time served.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30064/22-27, to outline the last time there was an agreed shared future vision and strategic priorities for the Criminal Justice Board; and (i) whether she can list these.
answer_text: While a formalised vision has not been adopted before, the Criminal Justice Board’s work has been guided by agreed Terms of Reference. These set out key areas of focus that require a cross-system response, including the oversight of justice-led Programme for Government commitments and promoting improvements in effectiveness, public confidence, and the experience of victims, witnesses and defendants, across the justice system.As such, the Board in recent years has concentrated on areas where joint action is most needed. This has meant a strong focus on the work of the Speeding Up Justice Programme, which is structured around five projects: Committal Reform, Early Engagement, Out of Court Disposals, the Remit of the Magistrates’ Court, and Digital. The Board also considers other priorities as they arise, such as pressures on the prison system’s capacity and the wider financial pressures being faced across the justice system, to ensure a coordinated response.The vision noted in AQW 30064/22-27, relates to a Criminal Justice Inspection NI (CJINI) recommendation, and will provide a valuable tool which can be used to define cross-system strategy, objectives and actions. This will shape the work of the Criminal Justice Board going forward.   The Criminal Justice Board are a key element in the development of this shared vision along with colleagues across the wider system and those who come into contact with justice services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 30178/22-27, to outline the number of occasions HM Prison and Probation staff have visited Northern Ireland to meet terrorist related offenders in person (i) in their home setting; (ii) outside of their home setting; and (iii) within prison, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: HMPPS Supervising Officers work within the Multi Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) to assess and manage risk posed by Terrorist Risk Offenders (TROs) in custody  and in the community Northern Ireland. When the offender is in custody contact may be either in person or by video link.  When the offender is released into the community contact visits are in person and generally do not take place within the offender’s home unless there is a case specific need to do so.    The requested information is not currently collated centrally, however, a review of records by HMPPS indicates the following data: 1 January 2022 – 31 December 2022147 community visitsNo record of prison or home visits1 January 2023 – 31 December 202386 community visitsNo record of prison or home visits1 January 2024 – 31 December 2024104 community visits3 home visits4 prison visits

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any action he can take to encourage private developers to use vacant Housing Executive land to improve the social housing stock.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) what mechanisms are in place to engage with the EU’s Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed; and (i) what delays are encountered by Northern Ireland not being able to engage directly to the EU.
answer_text: My Department engages with the EU’s Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed in relation to imports via the Food Standards Agency. Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the import of food and feed into Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the steps her Department has taken to review the prisoner pre-release legislation; and (ii) what changes will be made.
answer_text: ANSWERI have asked the Northern Ireland Prison Service to review what further steps could be taken to manage individuals who raise serious concerns during pre-release testing.  This includes what can be done to strengthen the risk management for these individuals, and the sanctions they will face if they go unlawfully at large.That work is still in progress, and I can provide further information once it is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the amount her Department has spent on His Majesties Prisons and Probation Board over the last three years; and (ii) the number of terrorist related offenders currently being supervised by her Department.
answer_text: The Department is responsible for the deployment of Supervising Officers whose role is to engage with Terrorist Risk Offenders (TROs) to support the assessment and management of their risk.  This is in line with statutory guidance which is available at: Guidance to agencies on Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)   The Department has worked with His Majesty’s Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS) to secure Supervising Officers and administrative support to undertake this function. The amount of funding the Department has provided to HMPPS over the last three financial years is £1,299,799.07, broken down as follows:	Financial YearFunding2022/23£408,545.372023/24£399,194.762024/25£492,058.94Total£1,299,799.07There are currently 36 individuals classified as Terrorist Risk Offenders who are subject to management under these arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the financial oversight mechanisms in place regarding funding for Police Community Safety Partnerships.
answer_text: The Department of Justice and Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) jointly fund Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), via a PCSP Joint Committee, to address community safety and confidence in policing issues in each council area. While PCSPs are statutory stand-alone bodies established under the Justice Act (NI) 2011, it is recognised that councils play a fundamental role in ensuring that the work of each PCSP is properly supported and facilitated. This is evidenced in part by the fact that each PCSP Manager is a council employee, with council processes used to enable the smooth running of PCSPs and many council buildings also used to facilitate PCSP meetings. Given the important role of councils in this respect, PCSP Joint Committee issues the letter of offer and associated funding agreement to each council Chief Executive who acts as the Accounting Officer for PCSP funding and procurement arrangements, signing off annual accounts and an annual assurance statement for Joint Committee to ensure that proper processes have been followed for PCSP funding.  The PCSP funding agreement is an extensive document, with funding being conditional on PCSPs, via each council, providing Joint Committee with an annual internal audit report. This helps to provide assurance on the probity and proper use of PCSP funds, which must conform to Public Sector Internal Audit Standards and Managing Public Money NI guidance. Each year, PCSPs produce an annual Action Plan and a phased budget for review and approval by Joint Committee (DoJ and NIPB) officials, with quarterly updates on activity scrutinised by Joint Committee officials before PCSP funding is provided to council.Each council is also required to have appropriate accounting systems in place  and undertake a verification process for all funding provided by PCSP Joint Committee. They must also retain supporting documentation relating to financing, and any premises, documentation or equipment used for PCSP activities must be available for access by PCSP Joint Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what action she has taken, following the report by the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland on Transforming the Criminal Justice System in Northern Ireland - A Strategic Overview; and (ii) what vision and strategic priorities have been set following the report.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring, as with all recommendations from Criminal Justice Inspection NI (CJNI), that these are fully considered and implemented to the benefit of the justice system.I welcome the Transforming the Criminal Justice System in Northern Ireland report which was published in November 2024. The report made one strategic and two operational recommendations aimed at driving system-wide improvement.The Strategic Recommendation made was:“Within six months from the publication of this report, the Department of Justice should facilitate Criminal Justice Board members’ agreement of a shared future vision and strategic priorities to deliver transformational change and innovation across the criminal justice system. These should be clearly communicated and reflected appropriately in organisations’ Corporate and Business Plans.”In response, my Department is leading work to develop a vision which can be used to define cross-system strategy, objectives and actions. The Criminal Justice Board have been briefed and are a key element in the development of the shared vision.Whilst CJINI recommended that this work be completed within six months, given the scale and significance of the ambition, combined with the complexity of the system, it is important to do this right and with the input of key stakeholders. CJNI are aware of the ongoing work and are being engaged as part of the wider consultation process to develop a shared vision.  It is important this process is collaborative and inclusive.In terms of timescales, CJINI has been informed and accept the position. As the Vision remains under development, the associated strategic priorities have not yet been finalised. Once agreed, they will be communicated across the system and embedded in the future planning of justice organisations so that they provide a clear, shared sense of direction.In terms of the remaining operational recommendations, an update is provided below.Operational Recommendation 1“The Police Service of Northern Ireland Strategic Transformation Board should continue to monitor and regularly review the leadership and governance of transformational strategies and projects across all organisational areas to maximise the benefits of centralisation, avoid duplication and ensure alignment with agreed strategic priorities.”PSNI has advised that the existing Terms of Reference for their Strategic Transformation Board already cover the governance arrangements in question and therefore no further action is required.  CJINI have been updated of the position directly by PSNI.Operational Recommendation 2“Within six months from the publication of this report the Department of Justice, members of the Criminal Justice Board, the Director General of the Northern Ireland Prison Service, and the Chief Executives of the Probation Board for Northern Ireland, the Youth Justice Agency and Forensic Science Northern Ireland should review the purpose and membership of existing programme and project boards, wo

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any discussions she has had with the Northern Ireland Prison Service in relation to safeguarding issues, specifically in circumstances where victims of abuse who subsequently enter the criminal justice system are confronted with their abuser in prison, and (ii) whether the Northern Ireland Prison Service has reviewed its transgender policy in light of the recent Supreme Court decision in For Women Scotland Ltd v The Scottish Ministers.
answer_text: ANSWER The Northern Ireland Prison Service has a clear focus on ensuring that the safety, security, rights and dignity of every individual in custody are upheld.  This often requires careful consideration of a combination of complex factors which are unique to each case.  I am content that the Prison Service takes that responsibility seriously and invests significant effort in getting it right.On the 27 June 2025 the ECNI published a paper giving its views on the Supreme Court Judgement and its potential impact given the unique legal position in Northern Ireland. “Their response included a wider consultation process for all interested stakeholders. The NIPS takes its responsibilities under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 seriously and so following the outcome of the consultation process full consideration will be given to the need to update any policies or operational processes.In the meantime, the NIPS are continuing with their guidance on the care and management of transgender prisoners that was introduced in August 2022.  This is used to effectively address the needs of all prisoners who may find individual challenges in custody and is based primarily on risk-assessments, and the use of case conferences to consider the risks both to a transgender prisoner in addition to the risks they pose to others, rather than gender identity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the business plan to grow the Police Service of Northern Ireland workforce to 8,000 officers; and (i) how long it will take to grow the service to 7,500, given the plan for the next three years is to grow the workforce to 7,000 and then stabilise that workforce for a further two years.
answer_text: The PSNI Workforce Recovery business case details the Chief Constable’s plans to rebuild workforce levels to 7,000 police officers and 2,572 police staff over the next three years and maintain them numbers at that level for a further two years. However, affordability remains a significant challenge; without additional resource allocation, it will not be possible to implement the plan in full. I welcomed the Finance Minister’s commitment to provide my Department with first call on up to £7m in future monitoring rounds to support the first year of the Business Case. I continue to engage with DoF to deliver this funding and ensure that the subsequent years of the Business Case are funded in full through the pending multi-year Executive budget. All parties around the Executive table have said that policing is a priority and it is vital that that commitment is followed up with the funding the PSNI needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the personal and professional development training undertaken by the Northern Ireland Judiciary over the last five years, as laid out by the Judicial Studies Board; and (i) the attendance rate at each training event.
answer_text: ANSWERInformation on the training and development undertaken and the attendance rate is not held by my Department. The judiciary is independent and judicial training and development is a matter for the Lady Chief Justice, delivered through the Judicial Studies Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what action she is taking, following the review of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.
answer_text: This review is very important to ensure effective and accountable policing for the people of Northern Ireland.  I am due to meet with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board to continue discussions on the recommendations from the review and I hope to be able to publish a way forward in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what the Historic Environment Division is doing to protect historical sites not related to buildings.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of fraud cases, detected for legally aided family justice cases, over the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last five years there have been no detected fraud cases for legally aided family justice cases.The Legal Services Agency has a zero tolerance to fraud and investigates all cases involving an allegation or suspicion of fraud. If the Agency’s investigation substantiates an allegation or suspicion of fraud, the Agency will refer the matter to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The Agency will also notify the appropriate regulatory body where a referral is made to the Police. It is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service whether a practitioner or applicant is prosecuted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) number; and (ii) associated cost, for statutory charges applied and recouped for family justice cases, which have been legally aided over the last 10 years.
answer_text: The Legal Services Agency operates an internal Debt Recovery Unit that determines and applies a Statutory Charge on property recovered, as appropriate, on an individual case basis. However, due to the formatting and retention of Statutory Charge data held by the Agency, it is not possible to identify or readily extract the information requested without necessitating a manual case by case review of all paper (pre-2019) and electronic files, together with supporting documents, as complete information does not exist in the current case management system.In addition, as a Statutory Charge is applied against an individual, rather than against a legal aid case per se, the one charge can cover several grants of legal aid to the same applicant which can in turn comprise a mix of both family and non-family civil matters.Accordingly, it is not possible to provide the number and associated costs applied or recouped for family justice cases granted legal aid over the past 10 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to outline the operation of providing civil legal aid for cases in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether family justice cases are funded in a similar way in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Legal aid in Northern Ireland is administered through the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland. The Agency decide on applications for civil legal services based on statutory financial eligibility and legal merits tests to determine whether an individual should receive support.  They are also responsible for the payments to the legal profession for services provided under both the criminal legal aid and civil legal services schemes. Assisted persons may also be required to pay a personal contribution towards their case.  These contributions are calculated by reference to statutory provisions that aim to ensure that they are proportionate and affordable.The financial eligibility test does not apply to certain categories of application, and the Director of Legal Aid has discretion to waive financial eligibility limits in other cases.In many ways the system operating across the other jurisdictions in the UK are broadly very similar. Entitlement to full, non-contributory legal aid is provided to those whose total disposable income (gross income less allowances and deductions) are below a certain threshold. Legal Aid is refused if their capital or disposable income exceeds an upper threshold.Regarding cases relating to family justice matters, in England and Wales, the introduction of the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders (LASPO) Act 2021 removed most private family law cases (i.e. disputes between parents) from scope, providing an exemption in cases involving domestic abuse or child abuse. Public law proceedings (i.e. cases involving social services intervention) and the representation of children remain in scope.In Scotland, legal aid is regulated by the Legal Aid (Scotland) Act 1986 and covers divorce, ending a civil partnership and disputes about children but does not provide legal aid for simplified divorces.The financial eligibility thresholds used in each jurisdiction are also different.  Scotland has the most liberal provision.  England and Wales limits are broadly comparable with Northern Ireland, though they are currently under review.Finally, each jurisdiction also has regulations covering how clients who settle or win their case and are to receive money or assets are handled. In each system, money or assets recovered or secured with the assistance of the legal aid fund are used to repay the cost of the assistance provided. The one distinct difference being that both England and Wales, and Scotland charge interest on the amount to be repaid, but Northern Ireland do not.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to her Department's recent publication, Legal Aid in Northern Ireland: Headline Statistics 2024/25, for a breakdown of the legal aid provided for civil cases, broken down into sub-categories, in particular, civil and family justice cases.
answer_text: The information you have requested for 2024/25 is currently being prepared by NISRA statisticians within the Legal Services Agency in line with the Code of Practice for Statistics and will be available in a more detailed Annual Statistics publication, due to be released in Autumn 2025. As an Official Statistics output, the release of this information is regulated by the UK Statistics Authority and is subject to strict pre-release access (PRA) conditions.  Details of the future publication schedule for this annual series can be found on the LSA website: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/lsani-official-statistics-future-publication-schedule.In the meantime, the information you have requested is readily accessible for the years 2020/21 to 2023/24 (namely Table A4), also via the LSA website: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/justice/legal-aid-in-northern-ireland-annual-statistics-to-march-2024.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why funding within the Department of Justice budget allocation for PSNI recruitment was not ring-fenced, similar to funding within the Department of Health budget for waiting lists.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the positive impacts of her Department's policies on tourism.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the occasions on which his Department has (a) sought advice; and (b) received advice from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland under its statutory legislative advisory role, over the last three years; and (ii) whether such advice was followed.
answer_text: Over the last three years, my Department has sought and received advice from the Equality Commission  on an ongoing basis via phone calls, emails and meetings. The advice has been sought and received in relation to:-development of policy, strategy and legislation;consultations;equality screening and Equality Impact Assessments (EQIAs);changes to our Equality Scheme;development of our Equality and Disability Action Plans, andour Annual Report to the Equality Commission.The nature of the advice takes many forms, including:- guidance on our obligations under the law to promote equality of opportunity;advice on good practice when highlighting equality issues and how to address inequalities that may exist, andadvice on policy making to ensure equality is central to the policy, as well as to ensure compliance with equality legislation.Depending upon the nature of the advice, it is followed accordingly.
