answer_text: My Department has sought and received advice from the Equality Commission Northern Ireland (ECNI) on the following occasions over the last three years:DateAdvice soughtAdvice receivedMay 2022Reasonable adjustments in respect of benefits informationYesAugust 2022Access to information and services for customersYesDecember 2022 Application of S75 duties in respect of the social inclusion strategiesDec 2022 – Mar 2023Engagement on embedding equality considerations into DfC 22-23 budget processYesFebruary 2023Section 75 screening – NIHE rent increasesYesFeb – Oct 2023Engagement on embedding equality considerations into DfC 23-24 budget processYesMarch 2023More detail on Due Regards dutyYesApril 2023Section 75 screening – Housing policyYesAugust 2023Department’s Business PlanYesOctober 2023Section 75 screening – Logo policyYesDecember 2023Equality Impact Assessment - BudgetYesDecember 2023Section 75 screening – Rate Support GrantYesMarch 2024Monitoring adverse impactsYesMay – Dec 2024Engagement on embedding equality considerations into DfC 24-25 budget processYesAugust 2024Review of the Department’s Equality SchemeYesSeptember 2024Consultees required for an internal staffing policyYesSeptember 2024Update of the Audit of InequalitiesYesOctober 2024Equality Impact Assessment – Winter Fuel PaymentsYesNovember 2024Designation of Public BodiesYesFebruary 2025Equality Action PlanYesMarch 2025Investigatory powers of ECNIYesMarch 2025Equality Impact Assessment – Employment Support SchemeYesOn all occasions, the advice was given due consideration as the policy was developed and/or the assessment carried out. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the occasions on which their Department has (a) sought advice; and (b) received advice, from the Equality Commission under its statutory legislative advisory role; and (ii) whether such advice was followed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any actions he has taken in relation to (i) the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce; and (ii) determining the way forward on the Taskforce
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answers provided to AQW 17411/22-27 and AQW 24999/22-27 on this subject.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he has taken in relation to (i) the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce; and (ii) determining the way forward on the Taskforce.
answer_text: (i) Of the 14 recommendations in the High Street Task Force Report, three could be identified where DfC would be seen as a significant lead department.  These are Recommendations 2, 5 and 12.Recommendation 2 endorses the Living High Streets Craft Kit and recommends that it is rolled out with the necessary support for implementation in each high street, village, town and city centre that is prioritised for action by the relevant district council.  This recommendation was recognised as one where DfC, given its relationship to the Ministerial Advisory Group on Architecture and the Built Environment (MAG), would have a leading role.On that basis, the Department undertook, subject to available resources, to carry out up to three Pilots of the “Living High Streets Craft Kit”.  The first pilot was conducted in Downpatrick and resulted in the launch of the Downpatrick Living High Streets Framework in September 2023.The second pilot project, to create the Greater Shankill Living High Streets Framework commenced in September 2024.  To date it has delivered an intense series of community ‘Place’ workshops; seeking to understand the positive and negative aspects of the Greater Shankill area. This information informed the basis of an ‘Imagining’ event in April 2025 which looked at how the area can be improved and its priorities.  It is expected that the process will conclude in June 2025, when all findings will shape a Physical Development Framework and Action Plan.Recommendation 5 urges the Executive to prioritise the delivery of place-making by developing and implementing an investment programme for blue-green infrastructure, cleaner and greener public realm and sustainable transport.An Outline Business Case for a cross-departmental regeneration programme is currently in development by DfC in partnership with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). The vision is to implement a strategic and integrated approach to tackling the challenges facing our village, town and city centres. The proposals will be consulted upon in 2025 and the overall Programme will require Executive approval.Recommendation 12 proposes that the Executive should devolve further powers to local government, including those needed for regeneration.  It recommends that Ministers engage with elected representatives from local government and the Assembly to explore the benefits of extending powers in this way.Due to the significant period, which has passed since the introduction of the last Regeneration Bill (2014), and the experience of recent initiatives in response to Covid-19 e.g. the Covid-19 Recovery Revitalisation and Small Settlements Programmes in partnership DfI and DAERA, the cross-departmental nature of regeneration activities has been clearly demonstrated, confirming the need for Executive consideration of this matter.SOLACE is currently engaged in work to set out the local government position on this matte

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions she has taken in relation to (i) the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce; and (ii) determining the way forward on the Taskforce.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my Department to progress this recommendation includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter. This work will have a positive impact across the north.The Executive Office, which commissioned the Taskforce, recently stated that the High Street Task Force concluded its work when it published its Report with recommendations in March 2022. In the same statement they referred to the work I am taking forward regarding Recommendation 8.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to confirm: (i) when the High Street Task Force last met; and (ii) if there are any plans to make it a permanent initiative.
answer_text: The last meeting of the High Street Task Force took place on 15 February 2022. The work of the Task Force concluded in March 2022 with the presentation of their report to Junior Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the occasions on which their Department has: (a) sought advice; and (b) received advice, from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, under its statutory legislative advisory role; and (ii) to confirm whether such advice was followed.
answer_text: Over the last three years, the occasions on which my Department has sought or received advice from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI) is set out below.March 2023, the Department sought and received advice from the ECNI in relation to the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy; the advice received is currently being considered as part of the development of this strategy.Between June and December 2023, my Department sought and received advice from the ECNI when conducting its Five-Year Review of the DE Equality Scheme.  My Department did not make any substantial changes to the Equality Scheme following ECNI feedback.September 2023, following the publication of my Department’s Relationship and Sexuality Education (RSE) Consultation, the ECNI provided advice in response to the consultation in respect of Equality Impact Assessments (EQIAs). This advice did not result in any changes to the guidance.   October 2024, my Department sought and received advice from the ECNI in relation to the Northern Ireland Childcare Survey; the advice received influenced the development of the survey questions.December 2024, my Department sought feedback on its’ Equality Impact Assessment and consultation process for DE's annual budget. DE received feedback from the ECNI in relation to this matter both at a meeting and later in writing; and the Department is now attempting to iteratively incorporate the ECNI's feedback on how we can improve this annual consultation process.  As part of the Department's work on progressing legislation to provide for all learners to participate in education, apprenticeship or training until age 18, the Department undertook 3 months of pre-consultation (from January to March 2025) which included discussions with the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI). As part of those discussions, ECNI recommended that the Department conduct an EQIA, which it did, and to share the EQIA with other departments. The EQIA was published as part of the public consultation that is taking place between 14 April and 4 July 2025.  Following ECNI’s advice, the Department is also considering the longer-term impact of its legislation on a range of Section 75 groups and produced a summary of the EQIA for children and young people. More generally, my Department sought and received advice from ECNI on the use of 'mixed ethnic group' in the collection and publication of data and subsequently used that advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she has taken in relation to (i) the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce; and (ii) determining the way forward on the Taskforce.
answer_text: (i) Of the 14 recommendations contained in the High Street Task Force Report, 4 of these relate to the work and responsibilities of my department to varying degrees, as follows. Recommendation 1 concerns Executive support for our cities and city regions and for my part I can advise that the Department for Infrastructure remains committed to ensuring that through the delivery of its wide range of functions in relation to water, transport and planning it supports the wider efforts of government in reinvigorating town centres and high streets across the North. In relation to Recommendation 5, I can advise that discussions are ongoing between my officials, and officials in the Department for Communities in relation to investing in high streets. For example, to encourage the incorporation of nature-based sustainable drainage interventions within new public realm schemes. Discussions will continue as policy development work in this area progresses.Recommendation 6 is relevant to my department given the plan oversight function it has, as councils bring forward their local plan documents. Whilst councils are sovereign autonomous planning authorities with ultimate responsibility for the delivery of their planning functions and wider powers, my officials will continue to encourage councils to bring forward appropriate local responses to issues arising in their areas, such as the challenges facing city and town centres. Recommendation 11 concerns the operation of the planning system. My Department is committed to ensuring that the planning system operates to the benefit of all, and officials will continue working closely with key stakeholders through the Planning Improvement Programme to improve processes and increase the efficiency and effectiveness of the system.  (ii) The First Minister and deputy First Minister have been advised that my department is content to be represented on any cross-departmental group along with other relevant departments and councils to explore the opportunities for progressing the recommendations contained in the Task Force Report and potential avenues to better facilitate successful, sustainable and attractive town centres across the North. However, whilst my department is committed to continuing to play its part, it continues to face ongoing pressures on budgets and resources, and the difficult reality is that this has an impact on my ability to take on new and drive forward every workstream I would like to. Certain outcomes may only be achievable over a longer period than I would want.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the occasions on which their Department has (a) sought advice; and (b) received advice, from the Equality Commission under its statutory legislative advisory role; and (ii) whether such advice was followed.

answer_text: In the last three years my Department has sought and received advice from the Equality Commission on six occasions. On all six occasions the advice provided by the Equality Commission was followed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given the decision by the Supreme Court on the definition of a woman in relation to the Equality Act (2010), (i) whether a woman inmate will have the right to take a case against her Department if a transgender person of the opposite biological sex is placed in the same accommodation; and (ii) whether she will be reviewing the guidance for transgender prisoners given this judgment.
answer_text: The legal representative of any prisoner is best placed to advise their client on the issue of rights of any litigation against the Department. Whilst the Equality Act 2010 does not extend to Northern Ireland, the Northern Ireland Prison Service is considering the recent Supreme Court ruling and its implications.  The Northern Ireland Equality Commission is also due to publish guidance on this ruling.  This will inform any review of current policy and guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 24847/22-27, to detail (i) his assessment of the Through Her Eyes charity's call for a mandatory seven-day custodial sentence for anyone who breaches a non-molestation order; and (ii) any other measures his Department could use to ensure non-molestation orders are enforced.
answer_text: ANSWERPursuant to AQW 24847/22-27, my Department was responsible for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998, which sets out the statutory criteria relating to non-molestation orders. I share both the Charity’s concerns with the breach of such orders and support the Justice Minister’s response on the devastating impact on victims. Article 25 of the 1998 Order provides that the contravention of a non-molestation order is a criminal offence. The facility to impose a custodial sentence is, therefore, already available to the courts. Sentencing procedures relating to breaches of non-molestation orders are a matter for the independent judiciary. That sentence was originally set at a maximum of 3 months but following a review by my Department in the years following the Order, was increased to 6 months.I understand the concerns that the option of prison is not mandatory but as advised by the Justice Minister in response to AQW 24847/22-27, judges will take into consideration all of the details of the case presented to them in making sentencing decisions, and in doing so, take account of both aggravating and mitigating factors, with the former including factors indicating higher culpability, or where there is a more than usual degree of harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why the JobStart 50+ scheme has been closed; and (ii) whether he has any plans to reinstate it.
answer_text: The JobStart 50+ Scheme was funded by HM Treasury’s Labour Market Evaluations and Pilots fund (LMEP) as a test and learn pilot scheme for 180 participants of which all 180 opportunities were filled. The last participant completed their JobStart opportunity on 31 March 2025. Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the average response time for the NIEA planning response department; and (ii) the reasons for delays.
answer_text: From 1 April 2024 to 31 March 2025 the average time taken for NIEA to respond to a planning application is 22 days.The primary reason for NIEA delay in responding to planning applications is that the number of complex planning consultations receipted exceeds the capacity that staff in post have to respond. Contributing factors include the number of re-consultations received, the quality of information submitted, and the level of non-statutory work associated with the delivery of our planning role and responsibilities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the pressure upon housing in Northern Ireland, to detail (i) why there is a delay in response times from the NIEA planning response department; and (ii) any actions his Department will take to accelerate response times.
answer_text: The primary reason for NIEA delay in responding to planning applications is that the number of complex planning consultations receipted exceeds the capacity that staff in post have to respond. Contributing factors include the number of re-consultations received, the quality of information submitted, and the level of non-statutory work associated with the delivery of our planning role and responsibilities.Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to improve performance on planning applications including:A review and update of guidance and internal processes and procedures.Analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of re-consultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation.Engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements with agents to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation, and Temporary redeployment of additional caseworkers. Additional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates to allow staff to focus on the processing of consultations. In addition, the Department is seeking to recruit additional permanent staff members for this work area and has been working in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which aims to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Regional Trauma Network in supporting victims and survivors of the Troubles in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline whether the Northern Ireland Prison Service stores personnel files outside of the secure document storage facilities provided by his Department.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of Northern Ireland Prison Service staff used to secure floor six of Dundonald House after the recent break-in; and (ii) whether any files that could identify Northern Ireland Prison Service staff were left in the building prior to the break-in.
answer_text: Dundonald House served as the Headquarters of the Northern Ireland Prison Service until June 2023, when the normal operation of the building ceased due to health and safety concerns.No staff were required to secure the Prison Service accommodation on Floor 6 of Dundonald House as it was not compromised by the incidents of authorised access to Dundonald House.  However, in response to the incidents of unauthorised access and following a review of risk, the Prison Service put in place a range of additional physical security measures until the building could be fully vacated on Friday 28 March.(ii) 	All files retained in Dundonald House since June 2023, including those with personal information, were held securely at all times.  Whilst there was no breach of data or information, those files have been relocated to a secure site within the Prison Service estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the Through Her Eyes charity's call for a mandatory seven-day custodial sentence for anyone who breaches a non-molestation order; and (ii) to detail any other measures her Department could use to ensure non-molestation orders are enforced.
answer_text: I recognise that breaches of non-molestation orders can have a devastating impact on victims and can retraumatise individuals who are already in vulnerable and dangerous situations. I am committed to ensuring that the justice system offers robust protection and reassurance to those affected by domestic abuse.As you will be aware, the Department of Finance is the lead department for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, which is the relevant legislation that makes provision for non-molestation orders. Any proposal to introduce a mandatory custodial sentence, such as the seven-day sentence proposed by the charity, would therefore be a matter for the Minister of Finance to consider. Such a proposal would also need to take account of a range of legal and policy issues, including compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights.It is important to emphasise that any breach of a court order, including non-molestation orders, should be dealt with through the proper judicial process. Sentencing in individual cases is and must remain a matter for the independent judiciary, who are bound to consider the unique circumstances of each case.In doing so, the judiciary are required to consider both aggravating and mitigating factors as well as any relevant sentencing guidance that is available. Aggravating factors may include higher culpability or a greater degree of harm, both of which could be relevant in cases of persistent or serious breaches. The current maximum sentence for breaching a non-molestation order is up to six months’ imprisonment and/or a Level 5 fine.My Department is actively working to identify where gaps in protection exist and is currently exploring how Domestic Abuse Protection Orders could be introduced in Northern Ireland, with the aim of providing enhanced protection for victims. I am also aware that the Commissioner for Victims of Crime has also commissioned a researcher to carry out some quantitative analysis on data relating to the application, granting and outcomes in cases of breach of NMOs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24585/22-27, (i) to provide a time frame for the newbuild of Portadown Integrated Primary School; and (ii) whether this is likely to be in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The project is at an advanced stage of development, however, given the severe constraints on my Department’s capital budget, regrettably I am unable to provide you with a timeframe for the new build of Portadown Integrated Primary School at this time. I will of course continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained funding for Education, to permit this and many other much needed school build projects to proceed to construction as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether she will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: NISRA and my officials are considering this review and its recommendations. (ii) The same applies to data collected through public consultations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether their Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether they will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: NISRA is currently considering the Independent Report and its recommendations, in discussion with officials across Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether he will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) are considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed.My officials will consider the report when taking forward future research and consultations to ensure the Department maintains consistency of approach with colleagues in other regions of the UK to aid continued comparability of relevant data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether his Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether he will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: (i) The Review of data, statistics and research on sex and gender was commissioned in February 2024 by the UK’s Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, and was led by Professor Alice Sullivan. It aimed to (a) identify obstacles to accurate data collection and research on sex and gender identity in public bodies and in the research systems, and (b) set out good practice guidance for how to collect data on sex and gender identity.A large proportion of the statistics and research which is published by the Department of Education is produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA). NISRA are considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed.(ii) Any public consultation will be undertaken in line with the requirements of the NICS Effective Policy Making Toolkit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether she will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: The Review of data, statistics and research on sex and gender was commissioned in February 2024 by the UK’s Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, and was led by Professor Alice Sullivan. It aimed to (a) identify obstacles to accurate data collection and research on sex and gender identity in public bodies and in the research systems, and (b) set out good practice guidance for how to collect data on sex and gender identityA large proportion of the statistics and research which is published by the Department of Justice is produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA). NISRA are considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system, to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed. Departmental consultations will continue to consider the impact on different groups identified by Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether her Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether she will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: Alongside NISRA, officials in my department are considering the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender, and its recommendations on data collection and reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department will implement the recommendations of the Independent Report on the Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender; and (ii) whether he will ensure any public consultations will register biological sex.
answer_text: The Review of Data, Statistics and Research on Sex and Gender was commissioned in February 2024 by the UK’s Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, and was led by Professor Alice Sullivan. It aimed to (a) identify obstacles to accurate data collection and research on sex and gender identity in public bodies and in the research systems, and (b) set out good practice guidance for how to collect data on sex and gender identity A large proportion of the statistics and research published by the Department of Health is produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA). NISRA are considering this review and its recommendations in discussion with officials in relevant Departments and with partners across the UK government statistical system to determine the most appropriate way to implement the recommendations from the review, where they are needed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the Executive Office's recent engagements in Washington DC during St. Patrick's week.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will review the practice of day release for those prisoners who have abused the system.
answer_text: The practice of day release is a component part of NIPS Pre-Release Testing (PRT) arrangements. Life/Indeterminate Sentenced Prisoners who have served their tariff/minimum period in custody have a statutory right to have the Parole Commissioners consider if they are suitable to be released back into the community. To apply their test, the Parole Commissioners almost always recommend a prisoner demonstrates a period of successful PRT, and any refusal of this opportunity by the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) could result in a public law challenge.The current approach to PRT is based upon careful assessments of risk, and graduated releases into the community, firstly under supervision then progressing to short periods of unaccompanied release.Prisoners who do not comply with the conditions of their PRT face consequences of their actions which will ultimately impact on their ability to progress towards their release from custody. I am content that these arrangements remain appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the newbuild of Portadown Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: The technical design (RIBA Stage 4) for the new school building has been completed and planning approval for the project was granted in July 2024. However, progression to site acquisition, building contractor procurement and construction can only be progressed when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19025/22-27, to detail (i) why the Housing Executive does not appear to be adhering to their own policy on this matter; and (ii) the role his Department has to ensure policy compliance on this and other matters such as complaint handling.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive takes all matters of complaint seriously and ensures that any complaint is robustly investigated in line with its internal complaints policies and procedures. As this is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, should the member have a specific case he wishes to raise, the Housing Executive would be happy to meet with him.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 22975/22-27, to clarify her assessment of whether the sick absence policy contained in the Civil Service Staff Handbook is reflective of the experiences of prison officers and prison staff.
answer_text: As previously explained, the Civil Service Staff Handbook sets out the sickness absence policies which apply across a broad spectrum of grades and specialisms within Northern Ireland departments, and there is no separate policy for operational prison grades. The policy sets out the procedures to be followed irrespective of the reason for absence or the type of employee. Departments can refer individual cases to the Occupational Health Service to manage cases of sickness absence. This includes advising on fitness for work, likely return to work, job adjustment, rehabilitation, and early retirement on medical grounds.In addition to the above, the Civil Service provides a range of entitlements including sick pay, in excess of statutory limits, excusal for periods of absence due to injury, and temporary and permanent injury allowances. Therefore, it is reflective of, and takes account of the specific experiences of prison officers and staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 22976/22-27, to detail (i) whether terrorists and members of paramilitary organisations, who have past convictions for sexual violence or child abuse as part of the legacy of our troubles, are able to avail of Communities in Transition funding; (ii) how her Department keeps a record of this; and (i) how her Department warns other agencies of past convictions.
answer_text: My Department hosts the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime but this is a cross-Departmental initiative. Departments and agencies in receipt of investment from the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime are required, as part of a Memorandum of Understanding, to ensure that investment provided on behalf of the Programme is used by the recipient in a manner that is consistent with and upholds the Programme’s Values. These are to: •	work collectively to achieve a society free of paramilitarism.•	support the rule of law unequivocally in word and deed and support all efforts to uphold it.•	challenge all paramilitary activity and associated criminality.•	call for, and work together to achieve, the disbandment of all paramilitary organisations and their structures.•	challenge paramilitary attempts to control communities.  •	support those who are determined to make the transition away from paramilitarism.•	accept no authority, direction or control on our political activities other than our democratic mandate alongside our own personal and party judgement.  The MoU also makes clear that in addition to these requirements, everyone associated with the Programme is encouraged to raise risks or any issues of concern in line with the Public Interest Disclosure Guidance. In the specific case of The Executive Office-led Communities in Transition project, recipients of investment must also, as a contractual condition, agree to the Behaviours Framework, which mirrors the Programme values and Ministerial Pledge of Office. In addition to signing up to the Behaviours Framework referenced in the response to AQW 22976/22-27, Communities in Transition delivery partners are required to security vet staff. Security vetting must be carried out in accordance with the requirements outlined in Schedule 4 (Security Schedule) of the Communities in Transition Commercial Conditions of Contract. Contractors must ensure that appropriate AccessNI checks are undertaken by those employed or volunteering.  Contractors in doubt with regard to the level of check to be sought are advised to contact AccessNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many Northern Ireland Civil Service employees have been assaulted while on duty, over the last 12 months.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail when a review of the Police Ombudsman’s Office last took place; and (ii) for her assessment of the impact an independent review of the Police Ombudsman’s Office would have on the (a) effectiveness of; and (b) confidence in, the office.
answer_text: No such review has taken place.  There is no legislative basis for me as Minister or my Department to carry out a review of the Police Ombudsman’s Office outside of the normal governance scrutiny process that applies to any Arm’s Length Body. Section 61 (4) of the Police NI Act 1998 requires the Ombudsman to carry out a review of the legislation every five years. That process will be taken forward later this calendar year. This is not a review of the Office.I cannot assess the potential impact of a hypothetical review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) how they have ensured that those eligible for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme living in Great Britain have been informed about its status; and (i) the number of applications to the scheme received from individuals living in Great Britain in the last 12 months, compared to applications received from individuals living in Northern Ireland,
answer_text: We recognise that many relevant incidents took place outside this jurisdiction and would encourage anyone who may have suffered a permanent disablement (physical or psychological) from a relevant incident to consider making an application to the Scheme.The Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) has undertaken awareness raising activities in February and November 2024, which involved issuing information on the Scheme and how to apply to wider victims’ sector groups, healthcare professionals and relevant authorities in England, Scotland, and Wales, including all local councils. The VPB has also engaged with the Victims’ Commissioner for England and Wales and delivered an e-campaign to further raise awareness of the Scheme across England, Scotland, and Wales. Information packs on the Scheme have been distributed to all MPs, members of the House of Lords, Scottish MSPs and members of the Welsh Senedd. Between 1 February 2024 and 1 February 2025, the Scheme has received 3,934 applications from here and 178 applications from England, Scotland, and Wales.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the funding provided through the Communities in Transition programme in the last four years, broken down by area; and (ii) the main themes for funding for 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Communities in Transition (CIT) operates across eight designated areas. From 2021-2025 the programme has provided funding of £15,168,465. This budget was awarded across the eight areas as follows: CIT AreaBudgetCarrickfergus/Larne£1,447,519Derry/Londonderry£1,436,963North Down£1,029,666Lurgan£618,931West Belfast£1,884,209North Belfast£876,022Shankill£1,063,186East Belfast£1,189,082Regional Projects£5,622,887Total£15,168,465Issues to be funded for 2025-2026 will be Mental Health and Addictions, Exploitation, Youth, Impact of Paramilitarism on the Physical Landscape, Ex-Prisoner Advocacy & Support and Regional Restorative Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department is aware that, according to the Department of Finance, the Northern Ireland Civil Service Staff Handbook does not need to be amended to take into account the unique role of the Northern Ireland Prison Service; and (ii) for her assessment of whether the sick absence policy contained within the Civil Service Staff Handbook is reflective of the experiences of prison officers and prison staff.
answer_text: The NI Civil Service Staff Handbook sets out the sickness absence policies which apply across a broad spectrum of grades and specialisms within departments, including prison service operational grades.NI Civil Service sickness absence policies which apply to all civil servants, including Prison Service staff, are currently being reviewed.  Any changes to these policies will be subject to consultation with Central Trade Union Side, including the Prison Officers’ Association which represents the interests of Prison Service staff.  For Prison Service staff, the application of the sickness absence policies is undertaken by a dedicated HR Strategic Business Partnering team which is embedded within the Prison Service.  The staff in that team provide a bespoke service which is informed by a sound working experience of the prison environment and knowledge of the specific issues faced by Prison Service staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether her Department is aware that, according to the Executive Office, there is no requirement to keep a record of those with past terrorist convictions when deciding on the allocation of funds for the Communities in Transition programme; and (ii) for her assessment of the impact this will have on the programme for ending violence against women and girls.
answer_text: All recipients of investment via the Communities in Transition Project are obliged as part of contractual arrangements to abide by a Behaviours Framework. The commitments in the Framework reflect those contained in the Pledge of Office. This includes confirming that they are not engaged in paramilitary activity and will uphold the rule of law.  Communities in Transition has helped many individuals and communities become more resilient to paramilitary and criminal control and the wider Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime has made great strides forward in documenting and supporting women who have experienced violence and harm at the hands of, or linked to, paramilitary organisations and crime groups. The Programme works across the Executive and is closely aligned to the work being undertaken under the Executive’s Strategic Framework for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22204/22-27, how many of the 10,000 pupils with special educational needs are transported to school by those directly employed by (i) the Education Authority; (ii) local authorities; and (iii) private providers to whom these services have been contracted out.
answer_text: In 2023/24, approximatively 10,000 pupils who had a statement of special educational needs, with additional transport requirements, were provided with transport assistance as follows:(i)  Around 5,500 pupils received transport assistance directly from the Education Authority (EA)* and a further 250 pupils travelled on Translink services, funded by the EA. (ii)  The responsibility for the operation and delivery of home to school transport in Northern Ireland rests with the EA and no school transport is delivered by councils/local authorities. (iii) Around 4,800 pupils travelled to school on private operator transport delivered through contracts with the EA, 3,900 by taxi and 900 by bus. * includes pupils who are in receipt of a parental allowance.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 15673/22-27, to detail (i) how they ensure that the Communities in Transition project and the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls do not provide funds to groups led by or employing individuals with a terrorist-related background, including coercive control and violence against women and girls, if they do not keep a record of such individuals when allocating funds; and (ii) whether this applies to other convictions such as domestic abuse or sexual crimes or solely in relation to terrorist-related activi
answer_text: All contracts for CIT are awarded in accordance with procurement regulations. A condition of award is that individuals working on CIT funded projects are required to sign a Behaviours Framework confirming that they are not engaged in paramilitary activity and will uphold the rule of law. The Framework also requires that projects and individuals accept no authority, direction or control over activities associated with delivery of the project which have not been agreed with TEO.In relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, all payments are made in accordance with public sector expenditure requirements. Specifically in relation to grant funding, this is paid directly to local councils who have each their own procedures to decide the allocation of funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the accreditation for HPV screening at Belfast Health and Social Care Trust was awarded.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) currently holds UK Accreditation Service (UKAS) accreditation for cervical cytology testing and has applied for an extension to the scope of its accreditation, to include HPV testing.Applications can only be assessed by the UKAS once a service is up and running so that UKAS can observe it in operation. A visit by UKAS has recently taken place and I understand that their assessment was positive. We await further assessment and their final decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 22126/22-27,  (i) to detail the types of offences that are being heard in magistrates courts in each year since 2020; (ii) how many received immediate custodial sentences; and (iii) whether any cases that would normally be held in the Crown Court are being held in magistrates courts in order to reduce the backlog of cases.
answer_text: (i) The magistrates’ courts deal with less serious criminal offences, while more serious offences are dealt with in the Crown Court.  Cases that can only be heard in a magistrates’ court are known as summary offences, whereas cases that are only be triable in the Crown Court are known as indictable offences.  Some cases can be dealt with either in a magistrates’ court or the Crown Court.  In determining whether these hybrid offences should be prosecuted in a magistrates’ court or the Crown Court, the Public Prosecution Service will consider whether the magistrates’ courts have sufficient sentencing powers to reflect the gravity of the offence and will also take into account any aggravating or mitigating factors that are relevant.Under the Magistrates’ Courts (NI) Order 1981 a defendant may elect to be tried by a jury in the Crown Court for certain offences which carry a maximum term of imprisonment in the magistrates’ courts of over six months; and for certain less serious indictable offences District Judges have the power, with the consent of the prosecution and the accused, to try the case summarily.  (ii) The number of prosecutions at a magistrates’ court resulting in an immediate custodial sentence in each year since 2020 is given in the table below.Custodial disposals at magistrates' courts, 2020 - 2024YearProsecutionsConvictionsCustodial disposals202020,09217,0912,050202128,26923,3932,366202228,43223,4352,586202329,43724,5333,002202430,12323,4562,709Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at adult and youth magistrates' courts.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by Public Prosecution Service on behalf of police, National Crime Agency, Probation Board, Airport Constabulary, or Harbour Police.4. Figures for 2024 are provisional.(iii) Decisions on whether a hybrid offence should be prosecuted in the   magistrates’ courts or the Crown Court are a matter for the Public Prosecution Service.  The Public Prosecution Service is operationally independent of my Department.Decisions under the magistrates’ courts (NI) Order 1981 on whether an indictable offence may be dealt with summarily are a matter for the independent judiciary.I am not aware of any cases that would normally be held in the Crown Court having been prosecuted in the magistrates’ courts in order to reduce backlogs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the impact he anticipates the National Insurance Contributions (Secondary Class 1 Contributions) Bill will have on special educational needs transport; and (ii) any mitigating actions his Department will take to safeguard this service.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the delivery of school transport and this includes the management of contracts with private transport operators. Transport is provided for around 10,000 pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) who have additional transport requirements and over half of these pupils are transported through the use of the EA’s own bus fleet. Transport for the remainder of these pupils with SEN is provided through contracts the EA holds with private bus and taxi operators.The increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions represents an additional cost to education, including the home to school transport service delivered by the EA. The delivery of transport to eligible pupils, however, is a statutory duty and there is no indication that pupils with SEN will be negatively impacted by this increase. The Chancellor has stated that support will be provided for public sector employers in relation to the upcoming changes to employers' National Insurance Contributions. That said, the quantum has not yet been confirmed by HM Treasury. I can assure you that I will strongly advocate for these additional costs to the education sector to be met in full, as will my Executive colleagues for their departments.
