question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action his Department is taking to reduce microplastics in Northern Ireland waterways.
answer_text: Microplastics, like any pollutants, are best controlled at the point of origin, by reducing or eliminating the source. In 2019, my department brought in the Environmental Protection (Microbeads) Regulations (Northern Ireland) to prohibit the use of microbeads in the manufacture of rinse-off personal care products. 	My department has also funded two postgraduate research projects on microplastics at Queen’s University Belfast: ‘Microbial remediation of microplastics within peat, soils, and coastal and riverine sediments’ and ‘The distribution, abundance and impacts of plastic pollution in Northern Ireland freshwater ecosystems’. 	In addition, I am committed to taking action to restrict the consumption of a range of single-use plastic products which are commonly littered or inappropriately disposed of, leading to plastic and microplastic pollution. My department has recently introduced legislation to ban the sale and supply of single use vapes in Northern Ireland, and officials are working to bring forward regulations to ban the supply and sale of wet wipes containing plastic.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21519/22-27, to detail why short-term temporary hire or emergency cover classroom assistants do not have an Access NI check prior to being placed on the list of available staff.
answer_text: Schools have access to the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) when seeking emergency or short-term cover for teaching posts. However, there is currently no similar register of available support staff. Schools source their own temporary emergency cover and must comply with the provisions of DE Circular 2024/10 – Pre Employment / Access NI Check Requirements and Safer Recruitment Practices for Staff and Volunteers Working in or Providing a Service for Schools/Education Settings.A school leader is responsible for safeguarding within their school and must ensure that any temporary staff member with an outstanding Access NI check is supervised by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity.The Education Authority has submitted a bid for Public Sector Transformation Funding to develop and extend NISTR to a range of support staff roles, including classroom assistants. If the bid is successful, this will mean that in the future schools will be provided with a register of fully vetted support staff for temporary emergency cover.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of teachers and teaching staff that have been physically assaulted in the last three years, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: Assaults on school staff are not reported to the Department of Education (DE) as DE is not the employer of school-based staff.  My officials asked the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), for information pertaining to their school-based staff.The EA Health and Safety Service cannot provide this data by District Council Area, only by Locality, however the District Councils covered within each Locality are:Locality South West: Armagh City, Banbridge & Craigavon; Mid-Ulster; Fermanagh & Omagh.Locality North: Antrim & Newtownabbey; Mid & East Antrim; Causeway Coast & Glens; Derry City and Strabane.Locality East: Belfast; Ards & North Down; Lisburn & Castlereagh; Newry, Mourne & Down.Area202220232024Grand TotalLocality East247369375991Locality North71159186416Locality South West5875137270Total3766036981677The figures provided are for both controlled and catholic maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the actions she will take to ensure the free access of sentencing remarks in written form for all victims of serious crime.
answer_text: At my request, my Department has convened a working group to look at options for providing victims of serious offences with access to free sentencing remarks in written form. The group, which includes the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, is considering whether it may be possible to establish a pilot on the provision of transcripts. Officials are gathering data to help inform these discussions on potential scope and costs of a pilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action she will take to ensure unduly lenient sentence applications will apply to sentences made by the magistrates courts.
answer_text: My Department’s Sentencing Policy Review examined the existing  arrangements for referral of unduly lenient sentences and discounted their extension to sentences imposed in the magistrates’ courts for reasons set out at Chapter 5 of the public consultation document sentencing-review-northern-ireland-consultation.pdf.   My Sentencing Bill will extend the arrangements to apply to all sentences imposed in the Crown Court.  I aim to introduce the Sentencing Bill in the autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the definition of grazed woodlands; and (ii) the criteria needed for landowners to claim farmland subsidies for grazing woodlands.
answer_text: (i)	For the purpose of claiming Area-based Schemes payments, grazed woodland is defined as an area of land with a tree density not exceeding 50 trees per hectare and on which agricultural activities can be carried out in a similar way as land without trees. (ii)	The criteria to claim grazed woodlands under an Area-based Scheme is outlined in the Guide to Land Eligibility published on the DAERA website.The Guide states “If trees are present throughout the field, the tree density is applied at field level. In these cases, if the tree density is greater than 50 trees per hectare the entire field will be ineligible.If trees are present in part of the field, the tree density is applied to the part of the field containing trees. If the tree density is greater than 50 trees per hectare for the part of the field containing trees and this is not a single tree, line of trees or small clump of trees, then that part of the field will be ineligible. The remainder of the field may be eligible providing it complies with all the eligibility rules.If there is a single tree, a line of trees or small clump of trees with grazing available underneath right up to the trunk, ineligible area deductions are only required for areas of bare ground or mulch under the trees where no grazing is available.If there is no grazing available under the trees or woodland is not currently being grazed/used for agricultural activity or the tree density exceeds 50 trees per hectare, then it is ineligible even if it had previously been used to support Area-based Scheme claims in the past.”As with all applications for farm support, applicants must meet the eligibility criteria for each scheme claimed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he will take to (i) regulate dog homing charities; and (ii) end the automatic destruction of healthy dogs in local council-run dog pounds after just five days.
answer_text: I am actively considering my animal welfare priorities and have been meeting with key stakeholders to outline my proposals.  One of the areas under consideration is the regulation of rescue and rehoming organisations.  Following completion of these meetings, I intend to publish my Animal Welfare Pathway which will detail the key priority areas for reform.  The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 provides councils with powers to deal with unwanted or unclaimed straying dogs, including the possibility of humane destruction.  It is up to individual councils to determine how they use these powers, nevertheless, I am aware that councils work very closely with the rehoming sector and the overwhelming majority of unwanted or unclaimed stray dogs are passed to these organisations, so that new owners can be sought.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether reparations to be paid by a perpetrator to a victim on the order of a court should be paid in full to the victim by the state, with the state recouping the full amount from the perpetrator.
answer_text: This proposal is not under active consideration; however, I note the arguments for such a change and remain open to considering it in line with developments in other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the associated cost for a sentencing council to be established in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether her Department is considering its establishment.
answer_text: The Department has not yet costed the establishment of a sentencing council for Northern Ireland but is aware the Sentencing Council for England & Wales had a funding allocation of £1,918k in 2023-24.A number of options to improve public confidence in sentencing are under active consideration, including the potential establishment of a sentencing council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 21187/22-27, to detail (i) who made the final decision to move a transgender prisoner from a male facility to a female facility; (ii) where the decision was recorded; (iii) whether she was involved in the most recent example of the movement of a transgender prisoner from a male facility to a female facility; and (iv) whether the decision was ultimately cleared through the Minister.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service does not discuss individual cases. Under the Guidance on the Care and Management of Transgender Prisoners, any decision to move a transgender prisoner from one facility to another is made at a Case Conference, chaired by the Director of Prisons.	Any such decisions are recorded in the notes of the Case Conference.	I have no role in operational matters involving individual prisoners, including decisions taken at Case Conferences.	See (iii) above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any steps she will take to address the issue of good character references in serious sexual offence cases; and (ii) how the present system of good character references promotes the Executive's Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: It is a matter for the judiciary to determine what is relevant to the sentencing process. I have acknowledged the potential inequalities, highlighted by the Gillen Review,  between complainants and defendants in serious sexual offence cases, including the fact that the complainant’s testimony is often treated with disbelief and the defendant, who can call good character evidence, may seek to undermine their character or actions whereas the complainant is not accorded the right to call character evidence or witnesses on their behalf. It is my preference to end or limit the use of good character references in certain categories of offending, particularly domestic and sexual offending.  My Department is, therefore, actively seeking to address the use of good character references, which will require legislation.  In the meantime, I would urge everyone to exercise personal responsibility when it comes to providing character references for use by a court.The responsibility for the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) rests with The Executive Office. However, my Department, alongside the Department of Health, jointly leads on the Domestic and Sexual Abuse (DSA) Strategy, which supports the EVAWG Framework’s aims. A key priority of the DSA Strategy is ensuring tailored, specialised support for victims, helping them recover from trauma, engage with the justice system safely, and reduce the risk and impact of abuse.The strategy acknowledges that navigating the criminal justice system can be daunting for victims and seeks to address under-reporting and case attrition by increasing awareness of support services and improving confidence in the justice system. Key factors include speedier case progression, stronger legal protections, and enhanced victim support.As part of the DSA Strategy, we are committed to implementing Gillen Review recommendations, ensuring fairer treatment of complainants and strengthening legal protections. This directly supports The Executive Office’s Strategic Framework by fostering a justice system that is more responsive, equitable, and supportive of victims, ultimately increasing trust and engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she will revisit the Victim Information Scheme to make it an opt-out process instead of an opt-in process; and (ii) whether she will bring forward guidance or regulations to ensure victims are permitted to not only attend parole hearings but also give impact statements.
answer_text: I have no plans at present to amend the current statutory framework for the Victim Information Schemes to adopt an opt-in process. The opt in design of the Victim Information Schemes ensures that all eligible victims are contacted at the point of sentence and are provided with information about the Victim Schemes. This ensures that they are fully informed of what each Scheme involves and what information they may be entitled to, allowing them to opt-in based on informed consent.Each victim will have different needs following a court process.   Some will wish to put it behind them and move on, while others will want to be contacted at various points during the sentence and be updated on progress. The benefit of an opt-in scheme is that a victim can decide at any point in the person’s sentence to register.  This is particularly relevant in the case of long sentences when victims often choose to register only in the final years or months so they are notified of any upcoming release.Under the Victims Information Schemes, registered victims are made aware of parole proceedings and are offered the opportunity to submit a victim report.  My Department provides these reports to the independent Parole Commissioners, who make determinations on release.   Parole hearings are heard in private. If requested to do so the Parole Commissioners can, however, at their discretion, admit individuals to attend hearings. Following changes to the Parole Commissioners (Northern Ireland) Rules 2022, registered victims may request a summary of reasons to explain how the Parole Commissioners reached a decision to release or not release a prisoner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any advice he is giving to applicants of the Northern Ireland Football Fund in relation to the impact of the EU Regulation preventing the sale of polymeric infill materials from 2031 on 3G and 4G football pitches.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether decisions made by the special case conference in relation to transgender prisoners is supported by legislation that provides legal standing.
answer_text: Decisions made by the case conference which is held under the Northern Ireland Prison Service’s Guidance on the Care and Management of Transgender Prisoners are focused on protecting the safety and dignity of prisoners in the care of the Prison Service. 		Legislative authority is provided under Section 2(1) of the Prisons Act (Northern Ireland) 1953 which states: 		2 Administrative powers of the Department	The Department shall have and may exercise all such powers as appear to it to be necessary for the proper administration and maintenance of any prison including the equipment, provisioning and supply thereof and the making of and giving effect to arrangements for the welfare, employment and training of prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to any relevant previous cases, to detail (i) who makes the decision to move a transgender prisoner from HMP Maghaberry to HMP Hydebank Wood women's prison; and (ii) the make up of the special case conference in relation to that prisoner.
answer_text: (i) Decisions on the movement of prisoners between HMP Maghaberry and HMP Hydebank Wood are made at a case conference, chaired by the Director of Prisons.  	(ii) Typically, attendees at such conferences include the Governing Governors of HMP Maghaberry and Hydebank Wood and the Residential Governors of both establishments along with the Prison Service Legal Adviser.  Medical information will be obtained from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to inform decisions and formulation of a management care plan outlining how the individual will be looked after safely and decently within the prison environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to the detail the items, apart from clothing, made available to a transgender prisoner by the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: There is no inventory of items to be provided to support transgender prisoners.  		Each request from prisoners, irrespective of how they identify, will be considered on its own merits taking into account, amongst other considerations, advice from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and any identified risks which must be managed appropriately.  		A management care plan will be produced outlining how the individual will be looked after safely and decently within the prison environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to any case where an individual identifies as a transgender female while already on remand, whether (i) that individual will be afforded the same rights as those committed to prison who already identify as transgender; and (ii) that individual will be able to attend their trial in the identity they are espousing.
answer_text: Every prisoner is afforded the same rights whether they are on remand or they have been convicted.  If during their time in custody, their gender is subject to change or they express a desire to change, a case conference will be convened to agree how their time in custody will be managed.The gender/identity of the individual is not relevant to the running of the trial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the reported case of Max the puppy, whether he will commit to ending Breed Specific Legislation to ensure dogs are not mistakenly judged as a banned breed based on their physical characteristics.
answer_text: AQO 1489 /22-27 Answer: Legislation is available to each government across the UK to restrict dog types, in situations where a specific type presents a sustained danger to public safety. This can be evidenced by a disproportionate number of attacks on other animals or humans, by the dog type in question, when compared to other dogs, or, in the most serious cases, fatalities. Only five breeds of dog are subject to restrictions in Northern Ireland, as is the case in the rest of the UK. Following increasing public concerns in 2024, restrictions were placed on the ownership of XL Bully type dogs. Prior to this, no additional breeds have been restricted under this legislation since its inception in 1991. Dog wardens from local councils have recently completed a comprehensive classroom and practical training programme, delivered by the Metropolitan Police, to provide them with the skills and competence to identify and type dogs which fall under the restrictions contained within the Dogs Order (Northern Ireland) 1983 (as amended). That training is identical to that undertaken by Dog Legislation Officers in England and Wales and is recognised as the best available in the UK. Given the displacement of dogs that arose across the UK when the differing jurisdictions put in place prohibitions on XL Bully ownership at different times, any review or reform would need to be taken forward in collaboration across all administration.  Until that time, I am satisfied that legislation is a necessary tool to support and encourage responsible dog ownership.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of his Department's proposed allocation in the Draft Budget 2025-26.
answer_text: My department’s proposed allocation for 2025-26 represents a small increase in funding compared to the 2024-25 position.However, it will not be sufficient to cover the significant increases in costs we will face due to a number of factors including pay and price inflation, increased National Insurance contributions for GPs, pharmacists and social care providers, and rising demand.As a result, my department is projecting a funding gap of some £400 million for 2025-26 with the HSC system asked to deliver in the region of £200m in new savings for the second year running. Even then, it is anticipated that significant in-year monitoring round allocations will be required to achieve a breakeven position.The assumption that savings on this scale are achievable is not without considerable risk. They will inevitably have consequences for service delivery, including waiting lists.The draft budget provides no additional funding for waiting list reduction initiatives. This will be the second consecutive year where no such money has been provided by the Executive. This is despite waiting lists having been identified as a Programme for Government priority. Indeed, the draft Programme for Government states: “It will not be possible to reduce our lengthy waiting lists within the funding currently available.”With this Draft Budget allocation, I would again challenge the Assembly and Executive to honestly consider if they are truly prioritising Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the Northern Ireland Civil Service Human Resources will (i) cease using the term inefficiency as part of their Sickness Absence Management; and (ii) stop the issuing of written warnings to members of the Northern Ireland Prison Service who are suffering from diagnosed mental health conditions.
answer_text: (i) The Civil Service sickness absence policies are currently under review. An early stage of the review changed the language and tone of communications with staff during the absence management process. New arrangements, including revised invitations to meetings, warnings, user guides and support guidance were introduced in all Civil Service departments by November 2021.  The final stage of the review, which is a priority for 2024/25, will result in the replacement of the existing Sickness Absence and Inefficiency Sickness Absence policies and removal of the term “inefficiency” from the policy.  (ii) Prison Service staff are civil servants and so subject to the policies and procedures outlined in the Civil Service HR Handbook. Consideration of warnings will continue to be made for all Civil Service staff who are absent with physical and mental health conditions in line with policy and on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the process for a short promotion, known as a substitution, in the Prison Service to cover critical roles; and (i) the role that a Prison Governor has in those promotions to ensure critical positions are filled at short notice.
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Prison Service applies the same provisions as the wider NICS with regard to internal promotion and selection arrangements.  These are outlined within the Career Opportunities and Promotion Policy, which covers such circumstances as described where staff are selected to undertake temporary service at a higher grade.  This can be through deputising, which is a short-term arrangement of less than one month, to cover for example sick absence or annual leave or through a temporary promotion, which is a longer-term arrangement, more than one month and normally up to 12 months.  This means of filling a temporary vacancy can be to cover matters such as maternity leave or a specific project and is reviewed on a regular basis. (ii) In all such circumstances, the staff selected must be deemed fit for promotion to the higher grade and/or capable of undertaking the full range of duties.  Within the Prison Service, the assessment of the need for deputising or temporary promotions and the assessment process to be applied is made at Senior Manager/Prison Governor level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether Prison Officers are subject to the Prison Act (Northern Ireland) 1953; and (ii) whether the Prison Act (Northern Ireland) 1953 takes precedence over the Northern Ireland Civil Service Handbook.
answer_text: (i) Prison Officers are subject to the Prison Act (Northern Ireland) 1953 (“the 1953 Act”) (as amended by the Northern Ireland Act 1998 (Devolution of Policing and Justice Functions) Order 2010)).  Specifically the Act provides the Department of Justice with the legislative authority to recruit prison governors and prison officers.  The Act also gives the department the authority to remunerate and set the conditions of service of staff so recruited.  These powers are subject to the approval of the Department of Finance.(ii) While the 1953 Act provides for certain conditions of service to be set in relation to individuals’ specific employment as prison governors and prison officers, the Civil Service (Northern Ireland) Order 1999 sets out how the NI Civil Service is managed and controlled.  These terms are set out in the Civil Service Handbook.  It applies to all employees of Northern Ireland Departments, including prison governors and prison officers recruited under the Prison Act (NI) 1953.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any plans to enhance the roads infrastructure leading to Craigavon Area Hospital, to facilitate the construction of a rear entrance to the hospital.
answer_text: I am aware of the proposal to upgrade Lisnisky Lane to facilitate a new secondary access for Craigavon Hospital by creating a new roundabout which would also facilitate a new housing development.	My Department is taking the lead in the project and I can confirm that it is effectively ‘shelf ready’.  	However, my Department awaits confirmation that finances are in place from the Southern Health Trust and the Developer, who are the major funding contributors for the project, to allow procurement to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) when the Northern Ireland Prison Service booklet entitled Sick Absence - A Guide to Your Roles and Responsibilities, was replaced by the Northern Ireland Civil Service Staff Handbook; (ii) any consultation that was conducted with the Northern Ireland Prison Service in relation to this change; and (i) whether the (a) Prison Officers' Association; and (b) Prison Governors' Association was consulted about this change.
answer_text: I can advise that the former NI Prison Service Departmental HR team (which was in place prior to the centralisation of HR Services into NICSHR in 2017) had developed the “Sick Absence - A Guide to Your Roles and Responsibilities” in 2012 to act as an aide memoire to the 2010 NICS Sickness Absence and Inefficiency Sickness Absence policies. These policies have not changed since 2010 and have continued to be applied across NICS Departments, including the Department of Justice and within the Prison Service. As referred in the response to part (i) this local guidance document pre-dated the review of the HR operating model in 2015, when the NICS Board (which comprises all Departmental Permanent Secretaries) agreed a move to a centralised HR function for the NICS. NICSHR was created in 2017 and has the remit of delivering the services previously provided by the separate functions of Corporate HR, Centre for Applied Learning (CAL) and individual departmental HR functions.  This includes responsibility for the standardisation and implementation of the above sickness absence polices.   Consultation on Policies with Trade Unions within the NICS is through agreed Whitley structures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of post-mortems that have been carried out on children from Northern Ireland in (i) Northern Ireland; and (ii) England since 2019.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to reinstate the Paediatric Pathology Service in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is important for any parent going through the terrible experience of losing a child to have the option of a post-mortem examination to help provide answers about their loss, and that they are fully supported at all stages of this process if they decide to proceed.  The current service for hospital-consented post-mortems for Northern Ireland families is provided by Alder Hey Children’s Hospital in Liverpool, since 3 January 2019. Whilst my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, this remains incredibly challenging as the services are under pressure across the UK and globally due to a shortage of paediatric pathologists.I remain very keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services in the longer term that might mitigate the need for infant remains to be transported overseas.  I had a number of conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation on this issue and I recently raised this with the new Irish Health Minister Jennifer Carroll MacNeill TD.  These conversations have been very positive. In the meantime, my priority is to ensure that bereaved parents continue to have access to a high-quality post-mortem service that provides the medical answers as well as the care and support that families need.  Access has therefore been secured by a further extension of these arrangements with Alder Hey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the status of the Paediatric Pathology Service in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is important for any parent going through the terrible experience of losing a child to have the option of a post-mortem examination to help provide answers about their loss, and that they are fully supported at all stages of this process if they decide to proceed.  The current service for hospital-consented post-mortems for Northern Ireland families is provided by Alder Hey Children’s Hospital in Liverpool, since 3 January 2019. Whilst my first preference would be the restoration of a dedicated paediatric pathology service for Northern Ireland, this remains incredibly challenging as the services are under pressure across the UK and globally due to a shortage of paediatric pathologists.I remain very keen to explore the feasibility of an all-island approach to these services in the longer term that might mitigate the need for infant remains to be transported overseas.  I had a number of conversations with Minister Donnelly about the possibility of future co-operation on this issue and I recently raised this with the new Irish Health Minister Jennifer Carroll MacNeill TD.  These conversations have been very positive. In the meantime, my priority is to ensure that bereaved parents continue to have access to a high-quality post-mortem service that provides the medical answers as well as the care and support that families need.  Access has therefore been secured by a further extension of these arrangements with Alder Hey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19285/22-27, (i) whether the Northern Ireland Prison Service has been included in the e-learning for Domestic and Sexual Abuse; (ii) whether Northern Ireland Prison Service personnel, including operational staff, are included in the 10 days paid special leave if they are victims of domestic and sexual abuse; and (iii) how many of the 69 staff trained Safe Place Advocates are from the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: Civil Service staff can access the Domestic Abuse e-learning via the LInKS learning platform.  Prison operational grades are due to commence a pilot in the New Year to extend their access to this e-learning.Prison Service personnel, including operational staff, are included in the 10 days paid special leave if they are victims of domestic and sexual abuse. There are currently no Safe Place Advocates in NI Prison Service (NIPS); however, delivery of further Safe Place Advocate training is planned before the end of March 2025 and volunteers will be sought from across the Civil Service, including NIPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how many of those from the Northern Ireland Civil Service have had an excusal of sick absence in the last three years; and (ii) the percentage of those from the Northern Ireland Prison Service who have been granted an excusal over that same period.
answer_text: The NICS policy on ‘Injuries, Diseases and Assaults’ gives provision for excusal of sick pay for periods of absence due to industrial injuries and diseases, including stress/psychological illnesses. This provides an extension of sick pay of up to six months where the qualifying conditions of the Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme are met. This is known as a Temporary Injury Award. In addition, the policy permits periods of absence due to injury or assault to be excused from reckoning against the maximum periods of paid absence. Details of all excusals are in the table below. The time periods are based on the date the extension of pay was effective. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in his Department; and (ii) whether his Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: I fully support the aims and objectives of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, and would respond to the two elements of your question as follows:(i) –The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Directorate within TEO created and launched its first delivery plan on 16 September 2024 that commits to creating an EVAWG training portfolio for the NICS, with a first-year focus on raising awareness of what VAWG is and how to take action to prevent or respond.A new Cross Sectoral Workplace Forum has also been established as part of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework 2024-31, to better understand and identify actions which can be taken to prevent violence against women and girls in the workplace context. The EVAWG directorate will continue to work with departments as this develops, to consider relevant NICS training requirements. There have been no DfI specific workplace-based programmes or initiatives arranged to date.(ii) My Department’s Raising a Concern policy will be kept under review and will be updated, as necessary, to take account of this. The complaints policy will not be updated as this policy deals solely with the dissatisfaction about a service provided by the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in her Department; and (ii) whether her Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: The Civil Service as an employer is committed to supporting the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls 2024-31.  The Civil Service signed the White Ribbon Charter, pledging as an employer, its commitment to ending violence against women and girls. Alongside this, the Civil Service has: implemented a Domestic and Sexual Abuse Policy and e-learning for staff;introduced support measures for staff who are victims of domestic or sexual abuse, including 10 days paid special leave to facilitate practical arrangements such as seeking advice or securing alternative accommodation;trained 69 staff as Safe Place Advocates and provided domestic and sexual abuse training to over 600 Jobs and Benefits office colleagues to better support customers affected by domestic abuse; anddeveloped a designated section on the Civil Service intranet and delivered ongoing internal communications to raise awareness of violence against women and girls and signpost staff to sources of support and help.In recognition of this work, the Civil Service is accredited as a Safe Employer and was awarded the Gold Workplace Charter by ONUS, (a social enterprise that offers specialist training and consultancy services to businesses, churches and communities to support victims of domestic violence or abuse). The Department of Finance’s Complaints policy and Raising a Concern policy are kept under review and will be updated, as necessary, to take account of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in his Department; and (ii) whether his Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: I fully support the aims and objectives of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, and would respond to the two elements of your question as follows:(i) –The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Directorate within TEO created and launched its first delivery plan on 16 September 2024 that commits to creating an EVAWG training portfolio for the NICS, with a first-year focus on raising awareness of what VAWG is and how to take action to prevent or respond.A new Cross Sectoral Workplace Forum has also been established as part of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework 2024-31, to better understand and identify actions which can be taken to prevent violence against women and girls in the workplace context. The EVAWG directorate will continue to work with departments as this develops, to consider relevant NICS training requirements. There have been no DfI specific workplace-based programmes or initiatives arranged to date.(ii) My Department’s Raising a Concern policy will be kept under review and will be updated, as necessary, to take account of this. The complaints policy will not be updated as this policy deals solely with the dissatisfaction about a service provided by the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in his Department; and (ii) whether his Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: The Department has promoted to its staff events hosted by the NICS Women’s Network to raise awareness of work in and beyond the NICS to prevent gender-based violence. If the Department receives an allegation, under either the complaints policy or the raising concerns guidance, which pertains to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy, it will be assessed and referred to an appropriate point of contact to be dealt with under the relevant NICS policy or procedure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in his Department; and (ii) whether his Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: My department is committed to playing its part and working together with other departments and stakeholders to tackle this all-pervasive problem. All staff were encouraged to attend information sessions on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework and the work of White Ribbon NI which were organised by the NICS Women’s Network, during the 16 Days of Activism for the Elimination of Violence Against Women commencing 25 November 2025.My Department’s Complaints policy and Raising a Concern policy are kept under review and will be updated, if necessary, to take account of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in his Department; and (ii) whether his Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: The DfC Management team is working closely with NIPSA colleagues to highlight the seriousness of this subject though a collaborative campaign.  We have marked International Day for the Elimination of Violence Against Women and senior officials have contributed to internal communications that raise awareness and promote support services. The campaign also promoted the national 16 Days of Activism initiative that took place between 25 November and 10 December 2024.Through the NICS ‘Safe Place Advocates’ initiative we also highlight the support available to colleagues affected by or at risk of abuse. Harassment Contact Officers are also in place to provide guidance, support and advice to those who have been subjected to, or accused of, inappropriate behaviours. My Department’s Complaints and Raising a Concern policies are subject to ongoing review to ensure alignment with government policy and take account of all statutory requirements and best practice.  These policies will be updated, where necessary, to take account of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place to reduce the Education Authority maintenance budget.
answer_text: The Education Authority were allocated funding for maintenance in Financial Year 2024/25 and there are no plans to reduce this allocation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any mechanisms in place to record the impact and outcome of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: A monitoring and evaluation plan has been developed by TEO for the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework which provides a robust framework for measuring the impact of the Programme by systematically tracking actions and success metrics. It incorporates lessons learned from ongoing analysis, enabling continuous improvement and informed decision-making. This approach ensures that the Programme adapts to challenges, maximises benefits, and effectively works towards its intended outcomes.The EVAWG measurement framework combines high-level, population-wide impact metrics with action-level evaluations to assess the strategy’s effectiveness. It will use Outcome-Based Accountability (OBA) for comprehensive monitoring and evaluation and will be informed by official statistics at a population level as well as aggregated project level data.Ongoing monitoring will include quarterly updates on the Delivery Plan, and annual updates on the population level metrics. Aggregated project level measurements will also contribute to the measurement and evaluation of strategic outcomes through the use of common OBA questions across multiple projects. As recognised by the sector, transformation at a societal level is likely to take years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will change the rules in relation to Education Authority maintenance budgets to allow schools to procure their own maintenance companies who can provide the service at a significantly lower price.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Maintenance Service is currently reviewing how it can provide the most efficient and effective model for schools to procure low value maintenance works (under £5k) while also ensuring compliance with health & safety and quality standards. It is currently anticipated that any changes will be implemented in the coming academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of XL Bully dogs in Northern Ireland; (ii) the number of these dogs that do not have an exemption certificate; and (iii) his Department's next steps in relation to those without an exemption certificate.
answer_text: As of 6 December 2024, information provided by local councils indicates that there are 417 licenced XL Bully type dogs here.  Of these, 250 have not yet applied for an Exemption Certificate.My Department is working intensively with councils, who are responsible for enforcing the legislation, to encourage all owners to submit applications before the 31 December deadline.  Owners who do not apply for an exemption in time will be in breach of the legal requirements and risk having their dog seized and facing court action.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in relation to the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, to detail (i) any workplace-based programmes and initiatives that have been arranged for staff in her Department; and (ii) whether her Department's complaints and whistleblowing policies will change to reflect the framework.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework and first Delivery Plan 2024-26 addresses the whole range of gender-based violence, abuse and harm which is disproportionately experienced by women and girls and rooted in gender inequality. The Strategic Framework has a focus on prevention, stopping the violence, abuse and harm before it starts and sits alongside the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy led by my Department and the Department of Health. The Delivery Plan commits to creating an EVAWG training portfolio for the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS), with a first-year focus on raising awareness of what violence against women and girls is and how to take action to prevent or respond.  The NICS is also represented on a new Cross Sectoral Workplace Forum that has been established to better understand and identify actions which can be taken to prevent violence against women and girls in the workplace context. In addition, the NICS has made a pledge to the White Ribbon NI campaign and the ONUS Gold Workplace Charter on Domestic Violence.  The number of trained Safe Place Advocates within the NICS has also been increased and are available to support colleagues who have been affected by domestic or sexual abuse.My Department’s Complaints policy and Raising a Concern policy are kept under review and will be updated, as necessary, to take account of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how a matter can be raised by a concerned tenant directly to the board of the NI Housing Executive (NIHE) rather than to the NIHE Chief Executive Officer's office.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“In general terms, any member of the public can write to the Chair of the NI Housing Executive Board at any time.Specifically, as per section 7 (raising a concern externally) of the Housing Executives’ ‘Raising Concerns – Whistleblowing Policy’, a member of the public can raise a concern directly by writing to any member of the Housing Executive Board, detailing the concern and including contact details if they wish. Section 7.2 specifically details the following.“A member of the public can raise a concern directly with the Housing Executive in a number of ways: Using the online “report a concern” form. This form is directed to the Head of CISU  (Corporate Investigation and Security Unit) who will take action immediately upon receipt. There are fields within this form for contact details however if you wish to remain anonymous these need not be completed in order to submit your information. Write to the Director of Finance, Audit and Assurance or any member of the Housing Executive Board detailing the concern and including contact details if they wish. The Housing Executive encourages those raising a concern to supply a contact email address at the very minimum in order to provide feedback and to ask for further information if required.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities under what circumstances can the Chief Executive Officer of the NI Housing Executive (NIHE) refuse, when requested, to pass on matters of concern to the board of the NIHE.
answer_text: The role of the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive is outlined in the Partnership Agreement between the Department for Communities and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at section 8.Anyone can raise a concern directly to the Housing Executive Board as outlined in the answer to your separate question (AQW/19025/22-27).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether the existing funding model for Northern Ireland Water is sustainable and fit for purpose.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any action he is taking in relation to the ban on rubber infill for school 3G and 4G synthetic pitches by 2030 under EU legislation; and (ii) any action he will take in relation to new 3G and 4G pitches planned before 2030.
answer_text: The Department of Education is following the current regulations and guidance from the Sports Councils (Sport England, Sport Wales, Sportscotland and Sport NI) with regard to addressing the environmental impact of rubber infill in 3G pitches. Viable alternatives to the rubber infill used and improved recycling opportunities and processes are being considered, and research is underway by the Sports Councils and The Sport and Play Construction Association (SAPCA). The Department will take advice from its technical advisers in Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) and from industry experts in agreeing suitable alternatives for use on the schools’ estate. In the interim, contractors providing synthetic pitches on the schools’ estate are advised to follow best practice guidance on how to minimise infill dispersion into the environment during construction, use, maintenance and disposal

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any action he is taking in relation to the implementation of EU legislation banning rubber infill from 2030 for 3G and 4G synthetic pitches; and (ii) whether there will be support for community groups, centres, schools and local authorities who presently maintain 3G and 4G synthetic pitches and will be unable to obtain rubber infill for maintenance post-2030.
answer_text: My officials are currently engaging with other stakeholders and assessing the impact of the ban on the sale of rubber crumb for 3G artificial grass pitches from 2031 in Northern Ireland and I await an update on next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) why K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland has been refused membership of the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners Group; and (ii) whether this decision was taken in consultation with the PSNI and the Policing Board.
answer_text: The decision to decline the application from K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland for membership in the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners’ Group was based on an assessment against the agreed criteria set out in the application process.  Based on this process and an assessment of current service provisions, it was determined that there are already established canine search and rescue services in place, and no service gap in this area was identified that would necessitate additional resources.As part of the established process those statutory organisations with responsibility for search and rescue operations, including PSNI, were consulted.  The Policing Board was not involved as the agreed process does not specify them as part of the decision-making framework for such matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether every primary school has the infrastructure to provide hot meals for their full school enrolment beyond the present entitlement.
answer_text: All primary schools would not currently have the necessary catering and dining hall infrastructure to enable the provision of a school meal to all pupils in those schools.  The public consultation document on the review of free school meals and uniform grant eligibility criteria published by my Department on 22 November 2024 referenced that “a large increase in the number of meals to be provided would require significant capital investment to increase the capacity of both kitchens and dining halls.” This is one of a number of affordability issues that I will take into account in making my decision on the way forward with this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether all roads leading to a school are gritted during wintry conditions, regardless of the classification of road.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by the Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. My Department has also introduced a priority “secondary” salting to rural schools to ensure they receive a level of special attention throughout the winter period and currently there are 62 rural schools on the list for secondary gritting.I fully understand and appreciate the concerns of those who use the more lightly trafficked roads and I would like to be able to expand our gritting service to many other routes including school bus routes, however it is simply not feasible to do so given the severe budget constraints facing my Department and continued reliance on in-year monitoring funds being made available to allow the current service to be met.I will nevertheless continue to make representations to the Executive for increased funding for infrastructure given its importance to communities, the economy and environment.

answer_text: The number of students entitled to free school meals can be found in the table below.YearNumberPercentage2023/2494,97527%2022/2396,31928%2021/2298,80429%Source: Annual school censusNote: Figures include funded children in voluntary and private preschool centres, nursery schools, primary (including nursery classes, reception and year 1-7 classes), post primary and special schools, and EOTAS centres.

answer_text: (i) The annual turnover of uniformed prison staff in the last three years is shown in the table below.Average Staff in Post1Number of Leavers2Turnover Rate31 April 2021 - 31 March 20221,293.21007.7%1 April 2022 - 31 March 20231,261.2927.3%1 April 2023 - 31 March 20241,320.4695.2%(ii) I am seeking to clarify the information in relation to the second part of the question and will write to you shortly with a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will carry out a review to ascertain why repeated errors continue to occur within the Child Maintenance Service.
answer_text: The standard of decision making accuracy within the Child Maintenance Service (CMS) is subject to review by the independent Joint Standards Committee for Northern Ireland and is audited independently by the Northern Ireland Audit Office. I take assurance from these bodies on the standards provided and therefore have no plans to instigate a review into the work carried out by CMS.  The most recent figure on the accuracy of maintenance assessments is  published in the  Departments Annual Report and Accounts (page 259) -  https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-annual-report-and-accounts-2023-24.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any improvements the continuous improvements team have identified for the Prisoner Transport and Prisoner Escorting Service; and (ii) the cost of the work conducted by this improvements team.
answer_text: The Prisoner Escorting and Court Custody Service (PECCS) is part of the Northern Ireland Prison Service, bringing prisoners to and from court, and looking after them while they are in court cells.The Continuous Improvement Team was established within PECCS in response to a Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland (CJI) report into court custody, published in March 2022.The work-streams being taken forward by the Team are aligned to the findings of the 2022 CJI Report and a number of improvements are being implemented.  These include strengthened arrangements for internal oversight and audit; better use of data to inform decision making and the operation of the PECCS; the development and review of service level agreements and processes with partners to ensure effective joined up working; and the introduction of joint risk assessments for the safe, secure and decent management of prisoners in court custody suites. The work of this team is being delivered through redeployment and restructuring and without a requirement for additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of cases referred to the Public Prosecution Service in relation to unduly lenient sentences in the last three years; and (ii) the number of these applications that where upheld.
answer_text: (i) The Department does not hold information on the number of cases referred to the Public Prosecution Service.  The Public Prosecution Service is operationally independent from my Department.  You may wish to contact the Public Prosecution Service directly for this information.(ii) The number of cases referred by the Director of Public Prosecutions to the Court of Appeal that were disposed of, and found to be unduly lenient in each of the last three years is provided in the table below.YearCases Referred to Court of Appeal & Disposed OfGranted 1202175 2022312023422024 (Jan – Sep)53Source : Integrated Court Operations System1 Figures relate to cases where an appeal was allowed, and the sentence reviewed on at least one charge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the Outline Business Case, in partnership with the PSNI, to address an urgent need to recover workforce levels, will be presented to the (i) Executive; and (ii) Assembly.
answer_text: On 20 November 2024, the PSNI submitted the Outline Business Case for the stabilisation and recovery of its workforce.My officials are currently reviewing the business case and will continue to work alongside PSNI at pace to ensure that the case can be approved by my Department.  Thereafter, the business case will be presented to the Department of Finance for approval. I will continue to make the case for additional funding for PSNI through the budget process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the support provided to (i) prisoners; and (ii) prison officers following deaths in custody.
answer_text: Any death in custody is a tragedy and I would like to extend my sympathy to any families who have been affected by the death of a loved one in prison.  A death in custody can effect both those who work in and reside in prisons in Northern Ireland.  (i) NIPS works with a range of partners to ensure that support is available for prisoners following a death in custody.  This includes Prisoner Safety and Support Teams in each establishment, Chaplains, Cruse Bereavement Service and access to Listeners, the Samaritans and Lifeline.  A booklet has also been provided for prisoners on help and support that is available to them. (ii) Prison Officers have access to the NICS Welfare Support Services and the NICS Occupational Health Service.  They can also avail of Inspire Workplace Services and the NICS confidential Employee Assistance Programme (EAP), which provides bespoke counselling services.Prison Officers can also seek support through the NIPS Critical Incident Response Stress management (CISM) programme, which provides a confidential and voluntary debriefing procedure in response to minor, moderate and severe critical incidents.  The NI Prison Service also provides additional support for both serving and retired Prison Officers through the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT), which provides a range of services including one to one counselling sessions, psychology and resilience training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 16655/22-77, (i) why her Department does not routinely capture the data of victims and witnesses accompanied by the Victims Support NI Witness Service to courtrooms and live links; and; (ii) why NSPCC Young Witness Service does not capture the data showing how many young people they support once their court case has been completed.
answer_text: The funding agreements between my Department and both Victim Support NI and the NSPCC Young Witness Service set out the agreed outcomes and targets to be met as part of the delivery of support services to victims and witnesses.  While the information in the question is not part of the performance data requested by my Department, these are routine or standard elements of the wider support role which is closely monitored. Every witness who gives their evidence by live link must have an accompanying officer present while they do so, and this role is performed by Victim Support NI’s Witness Service for adults and NSPCC’s Young Witness Service by NSPCC for children and young people. Similarly, each young witness who engages with the Young Witness Service is offered the opportunity to speak to their allocated caseworker after the case has concluded about how they are feeling and is offered additional support or onward referral if it is deemed appropriate.The performance monitoring information provided by Victim Support NI in relation to the Witness Service includes the number of witnesses referrals received, the number and percentage of witnesses contacted by telephone in advance of the trial to offer the service, the number and percentage of witnesses supported at court, the number of witnesses supported at a pre-trial visit before the day and at a pre-trial familiarisation on the day of the trial, and the number and percentage of hours of support provided by staff and volunteers. The performance monitoring information provided by the NSPCC Young Witness Service includes the number of young witness referrals received, the number of young witnesses declining or not engaging with the service, an age and gender breakdown of the number of young witnesses supported at each level of service and the court location, and the number of cases where evidence is provided by video link and at the Remote Evidence Centres.Grant agreements are adjusted as required at the start of the annual funding process, these will be reviewed with partner organisations at that point to consider whether any additional performance metrics should be added.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 15150/22-27; and (ii) AQW 15341/22-27.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer (i) AQW 15673/22-27; and (i) AQW 15889/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 15889/22-27 was answered on 19 November 2024 and AQW 15673/22-27 was answered on 18 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer (i) AQW 15408/22-27; (ii) AQW 15411/22-27; (iii) AQW 15890/22-27; and (iv) AQW 17111/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 15408/22-27 was answered on 11th December 2024.  AQW 15411/22-27 was answered on 17th December 2024. AQW 15890/22-27 was answered on 29th November 2024.AQW 17111/22-27 was answered on 20th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether the Access and Inclusion Programme will open for applications this year; (ii) when it will open; and (iii) the budget that has been allocated to it.
answer_text: Since 2018, the Department has been running an Access and Inclusion Capital Programme which aims to improve access and increase participation for persons with a disability at arts, cultural and recreation venues. The 2024/25 reduced capital budget available to my Department has presented significant challenges. Therefore, the vast majority of capital budget allocated to arts, culture and sport has been utilised to meet contractual commitments, statutory, and health and safety projects. Given the constrained financial environment there has been no budget available to allocate to the Access and Inclusion Programme and currently there are no plans to launch in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the financial position within his Department.
answer_text: The Department of Health is facing significant financial challenges not just for 2024-25 but in future years.The financial position facing the Department of Health prior to October monitoring was a shortfall of around £130m to secure the ongoing delivery of core health and social care services to our community. In addition, recent estimations for 2024-25 pay settlements to staff are in the region of £320m based on the latest pay announcements in England.This means the Department has a total shortfall of £450m.  Following October monitoring which has now been agreed by the Executive the Department has been allocated £350m leaving a £100m shortfall.This leaves me in a position where I cannot achieve both financial breakeven and fully meet pay parity.  I am currently considering options on how we move forward.The level of savings measures required to fully fund a pay award in 2024-25 would require the implementation of measures that will be contrary to the interests of health and social care, requiring us to actively shrink the HSC and resulting in more pressures on services and staff and yet more gaps in provision. Our expectation for 2025-26 is that the funding position will not significantly improve for Health and it will be remain extremely challenging to secure funding for new activity.  Therefore, we will need to continue to focus on making best use of the resources we already have through greater levels of productivity.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the differences, relating to the work of the Executive Office, between the Intergovernmental Relations structures and the Council of the Nations and Regions.
answer_text: The Prime Minister and Heads of Devolved Governments’ Council was established as the top tier forum at the inter-governmental structures introduced in 2021. It consists of the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, the First Ministers of Scotland and Wales, and the First Minister and deputy First Minister of the Northern Ireland Executive. The functions of the Council include overseeing and setting strategic direction for the conduct of intergovernmental relations at all levels, and considering policy issues of strategic importance to all governments.The Council of the Nations and Regions met for the first time in Edinburgh on 11 October 2024.  It includes the heads of the four governments, the Mayor of London, and the mayors of the combined authorities and combined county authorities. It is intended to be a forum to examine and determine actions for tackling major cross-cutting challenges on a structured and sustained basis.						     We will be full participants in both Councils and contribute as appropriate to the work of both.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the level of sick absence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, by percentage, in the last 12 months, broken down by department.
answer_text: The level of sickness absence in the Northern Ireland Civil Service between 1 April 2023 and 31 March 2024 by department can be found on the NISRA website by accessing the ODS tables spreadsheet - specifically table 1.2.7.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how the policy for staying in full-time education or training up to the age of 18 will affect those who wish to join the military at 16 with training completing before their 18th birthday; (ii) whether he will engage with the Army Foundation College and other military training providers in relation to this policy; and (iii) whether this will be in conjunction with the Minister for the Economy.
answer_text: In line with the Independent Review of Education recommendation, and subject to Executive agreement, I intend to legislate to ensure that all children remain in education or training until they are 18 years old.It should be noted that, in order to comply with the Education and Skills Act 2008, the Ministry of Defence requires all recruits in this category to enrol on an apprenticeship as part of their military training, unless they are studying for a higher qualification. Whilst the detail of the arrangements for Northern Ireland will be further developed, it is expected there will be no impact on young people who want to join the military at 16.There will be engagement with a range of relevant stakeholders on the further development of proposals and, given the links with Further and High Education, this will include the Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will ensure any work being conducted on Knock Iveagh has archaeological supervision; and (i) whether the Historical Environment Division will investigate the work recently conducted at night at the sight of a wind turbine on Knock Iveagh.
answer_text: My Department’s Historic Environment Division continues to ensure the protection of the Scheduled Historic Monument on Knock Iveagh hill and to provide advice to the local planning authority - Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council – on the archaeological impacts of any development works on the hill and on the appropriate archaeological mitigation of those impacts through the planning process.Regarding the recent report of works on the hill, the site was inspected subsequently by an HED Archaeologist. During this inspection, no evidence of unauthorised work or of any archaeological disturbance was noted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of how the Minister of Education's policy for individuals to stay in education or training until the age of 18 will affect the number of places available in further and higher education.
answer_text: The Minister of Education’s policy stems from recommendations from the Independent Review of Education, and how this will impact on further and higher education institutions is still under consideration.
