question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of deaths of those in custody within the Northern Ireland Prison Service estate in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Any death in custody is a tragedy and I would like to extend my sympathy to any families who have been affected by the death of a loved one in prison.In the past 12 months eight people have died while in the custody of the Northern Ireland Prison Service. Two of these deaths occurred while the individual was an in-patient in hospital and one death occurred while the person was an in-patient at a hospice.  A further death occurred immediately on return to the prison from hospital.  One death is potentially self-inflicted and in three, the individuals were found unresponsive in their cell.In all deaths in custody the appropriate authorities are notified of the deaths.  The Prisoner Ombudsman will take forward investigations into the deaths and inquests will take place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any digital solution her Department is examining that will make it easier for victims of domestic abuse to report and engage with the Police Service of Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether her Department will engage with digital service providers in the design and build phase of delivering this service.
answer_text: Given the PSNI’s operational independence, my Department does not become involved in procurement of digital solutions that would ultimately be used the PSNI. However, I have been informed that the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Directorate within The Executive Office (TEO) has recently launched the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI).  Challenge one of this initiative is funded to the value of £225k and invites suppliers to identify ways in which digital platforms can be used to improve the detection and reporting of violence against women and girls.  I am supportive of this initiative and consider that any outworkings could also potentially benefit victims of domestic and sexual abuse, particularly given that women and girls are disproportionately affected by these crimes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any action he has taken to ensure Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI promote visitor attractions in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any input his Department has in assisting both Tourism Ireland and Tourism NI in formulating their strategy for promoting tourism destinations such as the Game of Thrones Studio Tour.
answer_text: Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland both develop their Business Plans in consultation with my Department to ensure alignment with my economic objectives for a regionally balanced economy providing good jobs.Both organisations are represented on the Tourism Partnership Board due to the role they will have in delivering any agreed actions, including promotion of our visitor attractions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the new Ministerial Code, including when it will be published for debate in the Assembly.
answer_text: A draft revised version of the Ministerial Code has been prepared. Changes to the text mainly reflect the amendments already made to the Code by the enactment of the Executive Committee (Functions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2020.  Following consideration of a small number of outstanding issues Executive agreement to the draft will be sought before onward referral to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of times she has met the Independent Monitoring Board (IMB) in the last four years; and (ii) the steps she has taken to ensure the IMB annual report is promoted and shared with interested stakeholders.
answer_text: In the past four years, which include the 15 months I was not Minister of Justice, I have met with the IMB on three occasions and hope to meet them in November, following publication of their annual report.The IMB Secretariat will publish the IMB annual reports on the IMB website and circulate the reports to a range of stakeholders including; local political parties, criminal justice organisations, other statutory bodies in Northern Ireland and across Great Britain, community and voluntary sector organisations, and local media outlets. Hard copies of the report are also available to those who request them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners released from a custodial sentence without a permanent forwarding home address in the last three years.
answer_text: Information in the table below has been extracted from the Prison Service Records Information System (PRISM) and details the number of prisoners released in the last three years who have been recorded as having no permanent forwarding home address.YearNumber of  prisoners releasedNumber of prisoners released with no permanent forwarding address 20221,41322420231,6492562024(up to 30 September)1,226198*Data is based on the latest recorded prisoner address used on PRISM

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps she is taking to ensure prisoners with a sentence of one year or less receive adequate rehabilitation before release.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) continues to prioritise rehabilitation work to support and challenge prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour.  However, it is recognised that there are an increasing number of prisoners serving short sentences and it is important that their needs are considered and met during their limited time in custody.Recognising the importance of meeting the needs of this cohort, NI Prison Service are taking forward a number of initiatives:Introduction of a bespoke programme, in partnership with VCS, to manage the transition of short-term prisoners from custody into the community. The programme supports their resettlement for up to six months post custody by targeting the factors which positively impact on their desistance from reoffending;Enabling access to our existing support services, including housing advice, employment support and benefit advice.  We have also worked closely with our service providers to tailor the content of existing provision to make it more accessible to this cohort; and   Commissioning an external review of the current framework for the delivery of rehabilitation, the Prisoner Development Model. The review will consider the optimum delivery model for rehabilitation in the context of a rising population and the diverse needs of different cohort groups, including those serving short sentences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 16196/22-27, to detail the number of witnesses and victims that have (i) taken up the opportunity to visit court before giving evidence in the last five years; (ii) been accompanied to a courtroom or live link room under Victim Support NI Witness Service and the NSPCC Young Witness Service; and (iii) taken up the service to engage after the case has ended.
answer_text: From 1 April 2019 to 31 March 2024 Victim Support NI Witness Service provided 6,985 victims and witnesses with a pre-trial visit to court prior to the day of the court case commencing. A further 11,496 victims and witnesses engaged in pre-trial familiarisation sessions with the Victim Support Witness Service on the day they attended court. These sessions either involved a tour of an empty courtroom or the viewing a virtual courtroom.  The following table provides the yearly breakdown of these statistics:2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24TotalPre-trial visit to the court room before the day of the trial1,9036311,4891,5191,4436,985Pre-trial familiarisation on the day of the trial2,9111,0822,3782,5772,54811,496 Victim Support NI Witness Service is not in a position to provide exact figures on the number of victims and witnesses that they accompanied into a courtroom or live link room as this information is not routinely captured. However, Victim Support NI Witness Service provide support throughout the court process to all victims and witnesses referred and for the same period a total of 19,628 victims and witnesses have been supported.  The following table provides a yearly breakdown of the number of victims and witnesses supported by the Victim Support NI Witness Service: 2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24TotalVictims and witnesses supported by Victim Support NI Witness Service5,2011,7514,0864,3324,25819,628          The Victim Support NI Witness Service do not routinely capture the number of victims and witnesses who take up the service to engage after the case has ended.  However, it offers all victims and witnesses supported appropriate guidance to other services following the completion of a case, which may include referral to their Community Service for emotional support.   The NSPCC Young Witness Service is not in a position to provide specific figures to respond to your question as this information is not routinely captured.  However, from 1 April 2019 to 31 March 2024 NSPCC Young Witness Service received a total of 1,889 young witness referrals, all of whom would have been offered a pre-trial visit to the court and support in the live link room.  Each young witness who engages with the service would have had the opportunity to speak to their allocated caseworker after the case concluded about how they are feeling and offered additional support or onward referral if it is deemed appropriate.  The following table provides a yearly breakdown of the number of young witnesses supported:2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24TotalYoung witnesses supported by the NSPCC Young Witness Service4802064434123481,889

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when she intends to put the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime position on a statutory footing.
answer_text: I recognise the important contribution already being made by the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in giving a voice to victims and representing their needs and interests.I have been planning on the basis of introducing legislation to put the role of Commissioner for Victims of Crime on a statutory footing early in 2026. However, this will be dependent upon the progress of earlier Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she intends to improve victims' and witnesses' understanding and awareness of the Victim Charter.
answer_text: On 25 February 2024, I launched a public awareness campaign to raise visibility of the Victim Charter in partnership with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime.  The campaign ran across television, digital channels, social media and the three main local newspapers until the end of March 2024.  Analysis shows that it reached 56% of the adult population and was viewed by over 340,000 people on Facebook and Instagram, and over 700,000 on Google. 86% of the sample surveyed about the campaign stated that the advertising reassured people that a victim of, or a witness to, crime has rights that are protected. In marketing terms these figures were viewed as excellent given the relatively short advertising window. My Department has promoted the Charters by attending at Mela and Pride festivals and at the Balmoral Agricultural Show. A series of Charter information sessions were also held with the voluntary and community sector in the past year.Plans to promote awareness of the Charters will continue. Yesterday my Department launched a Call for Views to inform the development of a new Victim and Witness Strategy for 2025-2030, and promotion of the Charters will be included as part of the engagement and consultation process. The Victim and Witness Steering Group, comprising key leaders from across the criminal justice system, will shortly give consideration to how best to seek victim feedback including about Charter awareness. In addition, Victim Champions in each of the Criminal Justice Organisations, will shortly host a Summit on ensuring that a trauma informed approach is taken to support victims in the justice system - the Charters are one of several core tools which support this vision.As Minister of Justice, I will continue to do what I can to raise awareness of the Charters so that victims and witnesses know what to expect from the criminal justice system, what their entitlements are, and where to turn for the support and information they need.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 15589/22-27, (i) whether the establishment costs of £8.3 million is equally shared between each office; and (ii) to detail the anticipated annual running costs for each office.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.The business case to enable the establishment of all three bodies has been approved by DoF and is for £8.3m.To establish the bodies, each body will be provided with comparable funding. Each body’s corporate and business plans will inform their future budget structures.The Acts Explanatory Memorandum, prepared by the NIO, advises that the running costs for the bodies when fully established, would amount to £9 million annually.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 15586/22-27, and in relation to the discount given to tenants, whether this applies to those who have received housing benefit at any time during the five-year qualifying period.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The discount calculation is based on the eligible secure tenants and leaseholders’ total number of years spent as secure tenants of the Housing Executive or relevant other authorities. This applies to those eligible tenants who have received housing benefit at any time during the five-year qualifying period or any other period of their secure tenancy.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will (i) immediately reform and reconstitute the Shadow Family Justice Board; (ii) appoint an independent chairperson to bring about change to family justice as outlined in the Gillen Review; and (iii) extend membership of the Board to include lay stakeholders with relevant experience.
answer_text: The shadow Family Justice Board, together with the shadow Civil Justice Council and their advisory groups have proved very valuable fora for considering and progressing reform. Changing the status of the Board is however a cross-cutting matter which would require investment and, potentially, legislative change. In the short time available in this Assembly, I wish to focus resources on areas which will generate the more immediate benefits for citizens. The formal establishment of the Family Justice Board is among matters I propose be considered in the longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of terrorist related offenders (TROs) presently being supervised by His Majesty's Prisons and Probation Board; and (i) whether there has been application to return any of these TROs to prison for breaking their licence conditions.
answer_text: Staff from His Majesty’s Prisons and Probation Service (HMPPS) currently supervise, on behalf of the Department of Justice, 39 Terrorist Risk Offenders (TROs). Since commencement of the Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) in September 2021, 5 TROs have had their licences revoked and have been recalled to prison custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail which rivers' water quality are below (i) national standards; (ii) European Union Directives; and (iii) international agreements.
answer_text: National standards for water quality (i) are the same as European standards (ii), as they were derived from the EU Water Framework Directive. Results for Northern Ireland rivers are publicly available on the NIEA Water Catchment Data Map Viewer: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/daera-map-viewers .NIEA also provide water quality information on rivers to OSPAR (Oslo and Paris Conventions) via Defra and the OECD (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development), who collate these for their own analysis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15054/22-27, to detail the delay in implementing recommendations (i) FJ28; (ii) FJ29; (iii) FJ30; (iv) FJ31; (v) FJ32; (vi) FJ33; (vii) FJ65; (viii) FJ66; and (ix) FJ168 of the Gillen Review.
answer_text: The Gillen Review of Civil and Family Justice was a significant and detailed Report. While my Department did not commission the Review, and many of the recommendations, including some of those to which the question refers, are cross-cutting or are matters for the judiciary, legal profession or the Departments of Finance and Health, it has helped shape my priorities. That includes my ongoing focus on early intervention to help avoid the breakdown in parental relationships and stop the type of issues the referenced recommendations are seeking to address from arising in the first place. That approach reflects learning from other jurisdictions and informed initial steps under the Private Law Early Resolution Action Plan I have progressed jointly with the Department of Health. Good progress has been made in that regard and I expect the plan to evolve further in the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will reform the family justice system to ensure parents and children retain contact with each other.
answer_text: The legal framework governing the rights of parents and children and the criteria the courts must apply in determining contact arrangements is a matter for the Department of Finance. I am, however, keen to do all I can to ensure the best outcomes for children. I am continuing to work with the Minister of Health under the joint Private Family Law Early Resolution Action Plan to ensure vital family relationships are maintained and to help parents avoid the type of acrimonious dispute that can lead to the breakdown of contact arrangements, bearing in mind the primary consideration must always be the welfare of the child. A number of tools under the Action Plan are in train, which I hope to launch in coming months, and potential next steps are under consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether guidelines on child safeguarding will form part of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The production of child safeguarding policies and procedures is a statutory function of the Safeguarding Board Northern Ireland. In line with established policy process, Cooperating to Safeguard Children and Young People in Northern Ireland (CTSCYP) and underpinning operational policies and procedures are subject to review and revision as necessary.The Safeguarding Board Northern Ireland were a co-design partner in the development of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Strategic Framework. An outcome of the Strategic Framework is for children and young people to develop healthy, respectful relationships. In part, this means introducing whole school approaches to developing healthy and respectful relationships, within a wider safeguarding framework and culture, including policies and interventions that equip and support young people to address harmful attitudes and behaviours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether her Department provides references for an employee who is no longer in employment if they are subject to a criminal investigation; and (ii) whether her Department would inform any prospective employer requesting a reference that a previous employee is under a criminal investigation on a child safeguarding matter.
answer_text: It is Civil Service practice when providing references to supply factual employment details only, such as confirmation of employment start and end dates, grade, position and type of employment i.e. temporary or permanent.  If requested by the prospective employer, the reason for employment ending with the Civil Service can be provided, including circumstances where the former employee is subject to a criminal investigation and/or for child safeguarding purposes.  Data protection legislation permits the proportionate and lawful disclosure of such information where there is a legitimate interest in doing so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 15342/22-27, to detail the other strands of her Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint.
answer_text: The Department’s Outline Sustainability Strategy has been developed across the three sustainability pillars of: Economic, Social and Environmental, each of which has its own themes and a series of supporting actions. The range of themes under which the supporting actions will be delivered are Water, Energy, Buildings, Waste, Travel, Fleet, Biodiversity, Procurement and People.  The department’s Outline Sustainability Strategy is underpinned by the sustainability goals and activities of the NI Courts and Tribunals Service, the Northern Ireland Prison Service and the Police Service for Northern Ireland.  The sustainability goals and activities of these three organisations are contained within their respective estates and other strategies which are reported on separately. Having established 2021/22 as our baseline year for measuring our energy consumption the supporting actions have been adopted to support our goals of reducing energy consumption and our reliance on fossil fuels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey in which 63 per cent of victims felt that their crime was not thoroughly investigated by the PSNI, to detail how she intends to address this given the current number of PSNI officers.
answer_text: Whilst the operational response to the findings of this survey will be a matter for the Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland, I have a wider responsibility to ensure confidence in all parts of the criminal justice system, including investigations, and I am therefore concerned by this finding. I intend to follow up with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board on this matter.I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. My Department will engage with the Commissioner Designate and criminal justice partners on the detail of the recent survey results to establish whether any specific gaps in services can be identified and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans she has in place to ensure victims, who provide a personal statement during the court process, have an opportunity to have this heard in court during sentencing.
answer_text: Section 33 of The Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 sets out the provisions for making a victim statement, including that the statement is made in writing and provided to the court. The opportunity to have the statement read out in court is not currently mandatory.I have asked the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime to consider the use of victim personal statements in court. I understand that her report will issue later this month. I look forward to considering the details of the report when available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey in which 63 per cent of respondents felt their crime was not thoroughly investigated, for her assessment as to whether this is linked to the current number of PSNI officers.
answer_text: I am concerned by this finding and I intend to follow up with the Chief Constable and the Policing Board on the survey, including whether the current number of PSNI officers has impacted its results.I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system. My Department will engage with the Commissioner Designate and criminal justicepartners on the detail of the recent survey results to establish whether any specific gaps in services can be identified and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey which found over 39 per cent of cases took two years or more to reach court, to detail any plans in place to address current court times.
answer_text: In 2023/24, over 26,500 criminal cases initiated by PSNI or PPS were dealt with at courts here.  Departmental statistics indicate that the average (median) time taken for a case to be dealt with, from date offence was reported to police, was 190 days.  This was a 16% improvement over the past two years.  The time taken to complete 80% of all cases has also improved over the same period, by 27%, to 402 days.  However, the average time taken varies according to court type:  the average time taken for cases dealt with at Crown Court was 702 days, whereas, for cases dealt with in adult magistrates’ courts, the average time taken was 176 days.I am very conscious of the particular impact of delays on victims and I am concerned that whilst my Department is seeking to implement initiatives to address delays in the system, the significant budgetary pressures we are facing will affect our ability to do so. The Criminal Justice Board (CJB) has prioritised a programme of work, based on five workstreams, each led by a CJB member, which will seek to reform the way in which cases are handled, getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, and dealing with cases in the most effective manner. Progress has been made across each of the workstreams, however, the pace and scope of change will be greatly dependent on funding.  A brief update on the priorities for each workstream is provided below.Committal Reform – The Department is working with criminal justice partners to take forward the second phase of committal reform (direct committal), which aims to remove the traditional committal process entirely for the most serious offences, with the majority of cases being directly transferred to the Crown Court at the first court appearance in the magistrates’ court.  Early Engagement – The Early Engagement workstream explores opportunities for early engagement across the justice system including between police and prosecution and prosecution and defence, to maximise efficiencies.Out of Court Disposals – The Out Of Court Disposals workstream explores options to deal with cases more proportionately outside the traditional court process, for example expanding the use of Community Resolution Notices (CRNs) and Penalty Notices for Disorder (PNDs), and exploring other options such as Prosecutorial fines and Conditional cautions.The Remit of the Magistrates’ Court explores options to provide a proportionate response to cases within the court structure to reduce volume and backlogs in both the magistrates’ court and the Crown Court.Digital – The Digital workstream explores initiatives that are designed to improve efficiencies within the system and enable the sharing of information in an easier and timely fashion such as managing digital evidence, single electronic case files, and electronic service of documents. This workstream also incorporates the major task of procuring a new ‘Causeway’ system.  This work is being taken forward by the Causeway Team. This will be an ong

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Commissioner for Victims of Crime 2024 Victim Survey which found that over 77 per cent victims of crime felt unsupported during the court process, to detail any plans in place to ensure victims feel they receive the appropriate support.
answer_text: It is vital that victims and witnesses of crime can access the appropriate support services as they participate in the court process.  My Department provides over £1 million per year to the Victim Support NI Witness Service and the NSPCC Young Witness Service to ensure that all prosecution witnesses and victims attending court are offered emotional support and practical information.  This includes a chance to visit the court before giving evidence, explaining the court process, accompanying the victim or witness into the courtroom or live link room, and offering the opportunity to talk when the case has ended.  In 2023-24, 4,606 victims and witnesses were supported by these services.  Victim and witness feedback through satisfaction surveys and the Northern Ireland Victim and Witness Survey (2022-23) reflect a very high level of satisfaction with the witness support services.Special measures to assist vulnerable victims and witnesses to give their evidence are also available and my Department has established new Remote Evidence Centres (REC) in Belfast and Craigavon, in addition to the existing NSPCC led facility in Derry~Londonderry.  These provide a safe and comfortable space away from the court building for vulnerable victims and witnesses to give evidence directly to the court from a video link room in the centre.  There has been overwhelmingly positive feedback received from REC users and the conviction rate for cases where the witness was based in a REC stands at 94%.Sexual Offences Legal Advisers (SOLAs), funded by my Department and operated by Victim Support NI, have supported more than 2,000 adult complainants in serious sexual offence cases. Again, feedback has been overwhelmingly positive and plans are at an advanced stage to extend the scheme to children and young people. I also plan to introduce legislation in this mandate to clarify access to independent pre-trial legal representation for complainants and enhanced protection of complainants’ privacy in the third-party material disclosure process.I am grateful to the Commissioner Designate for engaging with victims of crime and seeking feedback on their experiences of the criminal justice system.  I am determined to improve confidence in the criminal justice system and will continue to do all I can to secure additional resource, which will be invested in ways that improve victim experience.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to ensuring there is sufficient school provision in the catchment area of new builds in central Craigavon.

To ask the Minister for Communities under what circumstances the Housing Executive would refuse the offer of available temporary accommodation suitable for families in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In considering any offer of temporary accommodation the Housing Executive will consider a range of factors which include:Value for moneyLocation/accessibilityAge/conditionQuality of furniture & fittingsWhile the Housing Executive is keen to explore all options for temporary accommodation, offers from providers may not result in a property being used if there are any concerns over any of the above factors, or any other factors that may be raised when engaging with a provider.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman Investigation of a complaint against the Housing Executive, published in July 2023, for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Housing Executive complaints system.
answer_text: The administration of the Housing Executive’s complaints system is an operational matter. I am aware that the Ombudsman made several recommendations to the Housing Executive as a result of its consideration of the complaint you have referred to. I have sought an assurance from the Chief Executive on the Housing Executive’s implementation of the recommendations, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive accepted the recommendations of the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman in this case and apologised to our customer for any distress caused.The Housing Executive made a commitment to learn from this situation.This includes an expansion of the team involved in the management of complaints and development of a training package regarding complaints policies and procedures in order to provide staff with the skills to effectively deal with customer complaints.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has undertaken an assessment of the performance of the Housing Executive in responding to complaints directed to its Chief Executive.
answer_text: The administration of the Housing Executive’s customer complaints process is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have not undertaken an assessment of its performance in responding to complaints directed to its Chief Executive.Complaints handling is outlined in the Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s Annual Report and Accounts, which my Department lays in the Assembly, following review and sign off by the NI Audit Office Comptroller and Auditor General.  Furthermore, the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive undertook a significant review of its complaints policy which led to a reduction in the number of stages included in its complaints process and its new policies and procedures came into effect in July 2022. This also incorporated the Northern Ireland Public Service’s Ombudsman (NIPSO) recommendations as part of The Local Government Model Complaints Handling Procedure.In 2023/2024, the Housing Executive received 482 First stage complaints and 116 Final stage complaints. Whilst the Housing Executive endeavour to respond to all complaints within the timeframes (First Stage Complaint – Initial Target of 10 working days to respond; Final Stage Complaint – Initial Target of 20 working days to respond), dependent upon the complexity of the complaint, some may take significantly longer to investigate. The Housing Executive ensure issues of complaint are agreed with the customer within the initial 10/20 working days, (this will be dependent on relevant stage) however, the focus is on ensuring that an in-depth investigation of the issues raised by the complainant is carried out and a comprehensive response issued which may fall outside the timeframes in which we aim to respond.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have had with the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland to ensure that the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Recovery is promoted in Great Britain.
answer_text: We have had no engagement with the Secretary of State on the promotion of the ICRIR.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the criteria used by Housing Executive staff when selecting temporary accommodation; and (ii) why hotels are often selected rather than serviced accommodation which is particularly suitable for families.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In making placements in temporary accommodation Housing Executive staff will seek to establish availability of accommodation across options including, but not limited to:Voluntary sector hostelsSingle LetsDispersed Intensively Managed Emergency AccommodationHousing Executive HostelsNon-standard accommodation (Hotels/B&Bs)Dependent on availability, staff will seek to align support needs with the availability of accommodation and in such circumstances voluntary sector hostels will be the preferred option. In cases where a placement in a voluntary sector hostel is not possible, staff will explore other options with non-standard accommodation used in the absence of other options and for as short a duration as possible while other forms of temporary accommodation are sought.In considering any offer of temporary accommodation for non-standard options the Housing Executive will consider a range of factors which include:•            Value for money•            Location/accessibility•            Age/condition•            Quality of furniture & fittingsWhile the Housing Executive is keen to explore all options for temporary accommodation including serviced accommodation, offers from providers may not result in a property being used if there are any concerns over any of the above factors, or any other factors that may be raised when engaging with a provider.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the Housing Executive criteria for adverse incident payments; and (ii) whether there is any financial or claim limit.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“In making placements into private properties the Housing Executive does so with the agreement of the provider. The Housing Executive does not assume liability for the property, nor assume the role or responsibility of the provider in managing the property. There is no legal transfer of responsibility and any agreement which is entered into is between the household placed, and the provider of the property. The Housing Executive has an Ex-Gratia option whereby it will consider making a payment to contribute to costs that may have resulted from the placement as a result of exceptional damage to the property. This is not intended to take the place of appropriate insurance cover, nor to address issues of general “wear and tear” of day-to-day living. There is no financial limit on the above process. It should be noted that any Ex-Gratia payment that may be offered as a result of an incident and assessed through the Housing Executive’s Insurance and Claims Department, is at the discretion of the Housing Executive, and not a legal or contractual obligation placed upon the Housing Executive.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the steps being taken to ensure victims living in Great Britain are made aware of the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme; and (ii) the number of applications received from those in Northern Ireland in comparison to Great Britain.
answer_text: We recognise that many relevant incidents took place outside this jurisdiction and would encourage anyone who may have suffered a permanent disablement (physical or psychological) from a relevant incident to consider making an application to the Scheme.The Victims’ Payments Board has issued information on the Scheme and how to apply, to wider victims’ sector groups, healthcare professionals and relevant authorities in England, Scotland, Wales and further afield as well as to the Victims’ Commissioner for England and Wales. Information packs on the Scheme were distributed to all MPs and the House of Lords as part of the Board’s awareness campaign in February 2024.  The Board are also planning further outreach and awareness on the Scheme across England, Scotland & Wales during 2024/25. To date, the Scheme has received 8,811 applications from here and 520 applications from England, Scotland & Wales.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the current repairs taking place at Parliament Buildings; and (ii) the expected cost of these repairs.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is committed to maintaining Parliament Buildings in accordance with its Grade A listed status, which necessitates ongoing repairs and maintenance.Scaffolding has been erected in preparation for upcoming roof repairs which will be carried out alongside routine maintenance tasks such as replacing boiler room pumps, fixing faulty taps, repairing broken radiator valves, and replacing damaged tiles.This financial year, a total of £90,867 has been spent on repairs and maintenance.  The estimated total repair costs for the roof are £2.453m.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any funding used to assist communities to transition away from paramilitary activity and control that is provided to (i) groups with links to proscribed organisations; and (ii) people who have been convicted of terrorist-related offences.
answer_text: As part of the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime the Communities in Transition (CIT) project operates to increase community resilience and reduce vulnerability to exploitation by paramilitaries. A condition of contract for the award of all CIT contracts is that individuals working on CIT funded projects are required to sign a behaviours framework confirming that they are not engaged in paramilitary activity and will uphold the rule of law. The framework also requires that projects and individuals accept no authority, direction or control over activities associated with delivery of the project which have not been agreed with TEO.Past convictions do not rule out involvement in CIT projects provided the behaviours framework is observed, thus no central record of such convictions is held.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 14880/22-27, to detail the amount her Department has spent on legal fees in the recovery of unpaid rates in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the legal fees paid by Land and Property Services associated with the recovery of debt in each of the last five financial years. Data is at the 31st of March in each Rating Assessment year. The reduction in legal fees from rating year 2019-20 is attributable to the impact of the Covid 19 pandemic. The increase in legal fees incurred in 2023-24 reflects the increased court actions being taken to reduce rating debt.Rating YearLegal Fees Total2019-20£1,475,2502020-21£378,5292021-22£214,2242022-23£193,5482023-24£709,503

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) how often the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership briefs them on their work; and (ii) for their assessment of whether a quarterly report for MLAs from the Northern Ireland Strategic Migration Partnership would be beneficial.
answer_text: The Executive Office has assumed responsibility for the delivery of most of the Strategic Migration Partnership functions. Officials regularly update Ministers on key issues, associated impacts and mitigations.  We will continue to keep MLAs updated on refugee and asylum matters through Assembly Questions and debates.  In addition, we and our officials regularly update the Committee for the Executive Office and will be providing a further update on 2 October.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Reform and Delivery Unit will have powers to compel Departments to make change in relation to the delivery of services.
answer_text: In the draft Programme for Government, we recognise the need for urgent Reform and Transformation to support and sustain the delivery of many of our public services. Reform and transformation cannot just be about money and how it is allocated. Instead, the focus needs to be on what we do, why we do it, how we do it, and being held to account for the results we achieve.To ensure we lay the foundations for this scale of change, we have committed to establishing a new Reform and Transformation Unit at the heart of government. Work is underway to scope out how such an office would operate across Government and we will make further announcements in due course as this develops.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the planned funding for each commissioner office under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.The business case to enable the establishment of all three bodies has been approved by DoF and is for £8.3m.To establish the offices, each Commissioner will be provided with comparable funding. The bodies’ corporate and business plans will inform future budget structures for each body.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount their Department has invested in events for Good Relations Week 2024, broken down by region.
answer_text: Good Relations Week 2024 focused on the theme of ‘Opportunity’ – a call to action to create a brighter, inclusive future for all.Over 200 events were delivered - showcasing the creativity and dedication of local groups, organisations and individuals who are working together to address challenges such as sectarianism, racism, inequality, and well-being.  Events and activities included workshops, lectures, panel discussions, feature talks, podcasts, films, music performances, and exhibitions.Delivered by the Community Relations Council on behalf of The Executive Office, £27,000 was allocated to support Good Relations Week 2024.Events delivered in each council area are shown below.OrganisationEventAntrim and NewtownabbeyArtsEktaAntrim Mini MelaLibraries NIConnect with Connect North in Ballyclare LibraryLibraries NIArt Workshop with Razieh at Antrim LibraryLibraries NIRazieh Art Exhibition in Antrim LibraryLibraries NIDementia Friends WorkshopCommunity Relations ForumThe Great Glengormley Get TogetherAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilLet’s Connect Through Food - Antrim Civic CentreAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilLet’s Connect Through Food - Mossley MillAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilBilingual Story time event – Hindi language, culture and musicAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilLet's Connect Football ChampionshipsAntrim and Newtownabbey Borough CouncilArabic Storytelling and MusicCommunity Relations ForumCommuniTEAApex HousingBallycraigy PS – Small Worlds CaféJoin the Dots TogetherLand and Lough ForumLibraries NICloughfern Library Intergenerational Crochet ClassLibraries NIBallyclare Library presents Northern Ireland Screen Libraries NIBilingual Storytime in Rathcoole LibraryLibraries NIGreenisland Library presents International Rhythm and RhymeLibraries NIGood Relations Quiz Morning in Greystone LibraryLibraries NIRathcoole Library Presents: Community Cookbook  Sixmile Methodist CircleHarvest Around the WorldArds and North Down Ards and North Down CouncilCeasefire Babies PerformanceUlster Folk MuseumCúl Trá-il at Ulster Folk MuseumUlster Folk MuseumRhyming Rab’s Ramble at Ulster Folk MuseumUlster Folk MuseumHarvest at Ulster Folk MuseumUlster Folk MuseumAutumn Equinox: Film Screening with Live Score from RóisWovenWoFUNd  - Good Relations Fund. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) who is responsible for setting the purchase price of Housing Executive properties; and (ii) the criteria for purchase.
answer_text: After an application to buy a Housing Executive home has been accepted, an independent professional valuer will calculate the market value of the property at the date the tenant applied to buy it. If the tenant does not agree with the valuation, they can challenge it, and Land and Property services will then assess it. A discount is then applied dependent on the number of years they have been a tenant. The maximum discount is £24,000. Housing Executive tenants must have a minimum of five years tenancy with the Housing Executive or a qualifying landlord (for example a registered Housing Association). If a former tenancy is not with the Housing Executive, proof of the tenancy is required for eligibility and discount purposes. Tenants without the five years qualifying tenancy may still be eligible to apply if they succeeded or were assigned their tenancy from a spouse or parent. Introductory tenants are not eligible to apply, but the time as an introductory tenant will count towards eligibility and discount.Up to four people (including the secure tenant) can apply to purchase a Housing Executive home. A joint purchaser must be one of the following: The spouse of the tenant, or A person who has resided with the tenant for 12 months prior to the applicationTenants of sheltered dwellings are not eligible to purchase their home; and there are restrictions on the sale of the majority of 1 and 2 bed bungalows.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: My Department fleet consists of 540 vehicles, inclusive of light & heavy goods vehicles, cars, vans and motorcycles. My officials are undertaking a range of actions and wider policy initiatives to support the transition to net zero vehicles and help meet targets set out in the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. My Department has established an NICS fleet working group, bringing together all departments, to ensure a co-ordinated approach to the decarbonisation across the NICS which includes consideration of the use of alternative fuelled vehicles across the wider public sector fleet.Progress will be reliant on the development of solutions which enable innovation in vehicle and fuel technologies, and manufacturing sectors keeping pace with the requirements of legislative arrangements to achieve net zero targets by 2050. The working group also shares best practice and experience of trials such as the use of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil (HVO) in HGVs, retrofit capabilities for hydrogen and investigating the use of HVO for heavier vehicles which represent 56% of my department’s fleet.I am also continuing to support Translink through investment in growing their fleet of electric, and hydrogen buses. I have made clear my commitment to delivering greener, cleaner, sustainable transport and have recently taken the decision to bring forward legislation relating to the Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate / Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes (VETS) Order 2023, subject to Assembly approval. These schemes will set sales targets for vehicle manufacturers in relation to the percentage of new zero emission vehicles they will have to sell. Switching fuels to low or zero emission alternatives is a key tenet of our approach to transport decarbonisation. I believe that the introduction of the VETS Order is an important and positive step towards encouraging those that depend on vehicles for their transport requirements to switch away from fossil fuels. My officials will continue to engage with industry stakeholders and officials from both the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), and the South’s Zero Emission Vehicles Ireland (ZEVI) to consider how new technologies and alternative fuel solutions can be adopted to meet the challenge of decarbonising the transport Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy owns 11 vehiclesOne vehicle (car) is a petrol/Electric hybrid. Electric vehicles options will be considered during the service life replacement process for our other vehicles which are mainly LGV and HGV.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: My Department currently owns 462 motor vehicles. This includes cars, tractors and other motorised plant used by the Department to carry out its wide-ranging activities.DAERA is a member of the NICS Public Sector Fleet Decarbonisation Working Group which is developing a coordinated Action Plan. Individual business areas are also considering fleet rationalisation measures and the consideration of low and zero emission vehicles during procurement of new vehicles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: i) My Department currently has one motor vehicle in use and one awaiting disposal.ii) The current Departmental vehicle is a self-charging hybrid. Consideration will be given to hybrid, electric and alternative fuel vehicles when this vehicle is due to be replaced.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by her Department; and (ii) her Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: The Department is committed to reducing its carbon footprint. Electric alternatives will be sourced when replacement vehicles are required. My Department has 8 owned and 2 leased motor vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of cases the Child Maintenance Service Financial Investigation Unit have examined in the last three years that have (i) been passed to the Public Prosecution Service; and (ii) resulted in a successful fraud prosecution.
answer_text: In the last 3 years none of the cases examined by the Child Maintenance Service Financial Investigation Unit have been passed to the Public Prosecution Service.In the last 3 years none of the cases examined by the Child Maintenance Service Financial Investigation Unit have resulted in a successful fraud prosecution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the discretion that the Child Maintenance Service has in relation to writing off contested Child Support Agency and Child Maintenance Service arrears.
answer_text: The circumstances under which the Child Maintenance Service can consider the write-off of arrears are detailed in Part 4B of the Child Support (Management of Payments and Arrears) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 and The Child Support (Management of Payments and Arrears) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2023.  https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2009/3151/part/PART4B https://www.legislation.gov.uk/nisr/2023/189/regulation/2 When considering write off action, the Decision Maker should exercise discretion and consider all the circumstances in the case. In accordance with public law principles, the discretion must be exercised reasonably, in good faith, for proper purposes and in accordance with the statutory power.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how the Child Maintenance Service deals with contested arrears that have been carried forward from the previous Child Support Agency system; and (ii) the Child Maintenance Service records that are held that may be examined by a paying parent previously within that system.
answer_text: The Child Maintenance Service (CMS) was introduced in 2012 to replace the Child Support Agency (CSA). Before each case migrated from the CSA computer system to the new CMS system, notifications were issued to both parents detailing the arrears balance. At this stage, both parents were given the opportunity to dispute the balance. CMS holds a record of all maintenance calculations and payments made under the CSA statutory schemes. If a dispute is raised in respect of arrears CMS caseworkers will analyse the details of how the arrears accrued and consider any evidence provided.   CSA computer systems have been decommissioned and all information exported to the new CMS system is held on legacy archive records. Records are accessible to CMS staff and consideration can be given to the release of this information under a Right of Access Request. All records are maintained in line with General Data Protection Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the level of diligence applied; and (ii) the records which must be provided, by Child Maintenance Service staff when considering seeking a Deduction of Earnings Order for a paying parent that has never missed a payment and contests arrears.
answer_text: (i) The following points are considered before a decision maker (DM) proceeds to issue an enforced Deduction from Earnings Order (DEO):    Child Maintenance payments must be in arrears (arrears may arise in ways other than missed payments but are nonetheless collected and enforced in the same way, for example backdated changes of circumstances, or a delay in putting a payment schedule in place).The Paying Parent (PP) must be notified in writing of the amounts to be paid and the due date.An arrears notice must have been issued to the PP and attempts made to speak to the PP.(ii) Before a DM can proceed with issuing a DEO, they must confirm on the Child Maintenance Service IT system that they have given due diligence to all the points noted above.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any awareness he has in relation to what is commonly referred to as contested Child Support Agency or Child Maintenance Service arrears.
answer_text: I am aware that on occasion parents may dispute child maintenance arrears balances. Child Maintenance Service caseworkers operate within legislative, policy and procedural guidelines set out by my Department when making decisions in respect of arrears recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by her Department; and (ii) her Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: As of 31 March 2024, my Department owns 92 road registered vehicles.  Of this fleet 7 are fully electric vehicles and 4 are hybrid vehicles. A further hybrid vehicle was purchased in August 2024. Excluded from these figures are non-road vehicles and static workshop training vehicles.My Department currently plans to purchase a further 3 electric vehicles and another hybrid vehicle. The purchase of Electric Vehicles forms only one strand of my Department’s plans to reduce its carbon footprint where it is appropriate and feasible to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: My Department owns 38 motor vehicles. The Department’s draft Asset Management Plan 2024-2029 includes several objectives to decarbonise and improve energy efficiency, including the decarbonisation of its fleet of motor vehicles. As vehicles approach the end of their useful lives, the objective is to replace them with low/zero emission vehicles where practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Banbridge has been removed from the Sunday and holiday chemist rota.
answer_text: During 2023/2024, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health (SPPG/DoH) in conjunction with the community pharmacy representative body, Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) completed a review of Community Pharmacy Rota provision across Northern Ireland to ensure that level of provision of community pharmacy services adequately meets the needs of the population. The review process was informed by monitoring of activity during Sunday & Public holiday rotas and feedback provided from participating pharmacies via CPNI.  As part of the review process, feedback on the proposed changes was obtained via a consultation process with the Community/Voluntary and Elected Representatives Engagement Forum.As a result, it was proposed that there was no longer a need for a rota to be provided in Banbridge on Sundays and Public holidays.On Sundays, pharmacy services are available in Rushmere, Craigavon from 1-6pm and rota in Lurgan / Portadown from 11am to 12noon.No objections to this proposal were received during the consultation period and it was, therefore, agreed to cease rota in Banbridge from 1st July 2024.The above information has been shared with Community Pharmacies, GP Practices and Out-of-Hours medical services so they can advise patients of available pharmacy services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of motor vehicles owned by his Department; and (ii) his Department's plans to reduce its carbon footprint by way of electric vehicles.
answer_text: (i) My Department owns 1 motor vehicle.(ii) This is a hybrid vehicle which is powered by an internal combustion engine and an electric motor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will publish all of the findings of the independent review of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Paws for Thought multi-agency group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of sexual offences against children, with an online crime flag, recorded in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of offences reported to the PSNI.  The recording of information in relation to offences reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI. The Department holds information on the number of convictions at courts.     However, prosecutions and convictions datasets held by the Department of Justice do not contain a marker or flag to allow the identification of online crimes.Information on the number of convictions at courts for offences relating to making, copying, distributing or possessing prohibited or indecent images of children in the five-year period up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, has been provided. These may relate to online activities, though they should not be considered an exhaustive count of the number of convictions for offences specified in the question.   Convictions at courts for offences relating to making, copying, distributing or possessing prohibited or indecent images of children, 2019 - 2023YearConvictions20193920204620211032022101202391Notes:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at courts where there was a conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, Harbour Police, Airport Constabulary, NCA or PBNI.4. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and magistrates' courts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the level of antisocial behaviour; and (ii) to detail the work being undertaken by her Department to address this issue.
answer_text: Statistics published by the PSNI record that for the period 1 July 2023 to 30 June 2024 there has been a decrease of 8.2% in recorded incidents of Antisocial Behaviour (ASB) when compared to the 12 months previously. This decrease was seen across all eleven policing districts. While this drop in recorded incidents is encouraging, PSNI note that these figures should be interpreted as 'calls for service' rather than as figures which reflect the true level of victimisation. The statistics relate only to those ASB incidents reported to PSNI and may exclude incidents reported to other agencies such as NIHE and local councils.Addressing ASB requires collective partnership working as it is rare that any community safety issue can be addressed by the criminal justice system alone. My Department works across local and central government, as well as with other relevant agencies, to put in place joined up, long term solutions to prevent incidents arising and to tackle the cumulative effect ASB has both on individuals and our communities.Policing and Community Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board play a key role in the Departments operational response to community safety concerns at a local level. Tackling ASB is a key strategic priority for PCSPs, which engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful, structured interventions to address the issue in each Council area.To support a collaborative approach my officials led the establishment of a multi -agency, cross-departmental Community Safety Network, to ensure a collective response to multi-faceted community safety issues, including ASB. My Department also financially contributes to Support Hubs, multi-agency partnerships designed to help vulnerable people, which can include victims of ongoing ASB, access the right support, at the right time, from the right organisations in their local area. Furthermore, to ensure that relevant authorities have effective and proportionate enforcement powers, my Department led a multi-agency, cross departmental review of ASB legislation in advance of holding a joint public consultation with the Department for Communities on potential legislative change, and is now progressing the outworkings of that consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department will take to address (i) parental alienation; and (ii) contact denial as it pertains to the justice system.
answer_text: While there is no legal definition of parental alienation in Northern Ireland, I am aware of its increasing use and that it is not a universally supported term.  The family court in Northern Ireland has a range of powers to deal with cases involving family conflict and separation under The Children (NI) Order 1995. However, the legislation is clear that when considering any change to a child's living and contact arrangements, the welfare of the child is the court’s paramount concern. Contact decisions which come before the court are a matter for that court. Court Children’s Services, located within Health and Social Care Trusts, are responsible for discharging the Trusts’ legal responsibility to assist the family courts in making decisions about the care and contact arrangements for children when separating parents are unable to come to an agreement. Each Court Children’s Officer’s role is to represent the voice of the child/children in private law court proceedings. They advise the court on matters that impact upon the children, including the presence of any alienating behaviours for example, and make recommendations as to what may be in the child’s best interests.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they intend to provide an annual report on the six outcomes of the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: An Oversight Board has been established whose purpose is to review the development and implementation of the Strategic Framework, related delivery plans, and to manage interfaces across government Departments and the public sector.  The Oversight Board will be chaired at Ministerial level to reinforce the leadership and commitment of this Executive Strategy.  The Executive will receive a biannual update on progress against the EVAWG Strategic Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the actions her Department will take to ensure that custody and visitation court orders are upheld; and (ii) how her Department intends to address how legal aid is being used in the family courts to stall such court orders.
answer_text: Breach of a residence or contact order can be dealt through contempt of court proceedings or a summons initiated under general enforcement powers in Article 112 of the Magistrates’ Courts (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 which gives the court the power to fine or imprison. It is right that enforcement in individual cases be a matter for the relevant Judge, and in reaching decisions the Judge will strive to ensure the paramount consideration of the welfare of the child is not compromised. I am conscious however that the best outcomes for children will derive from helping parents avoid the type of acrimonious dispute and entrenched relationship difficulties that can lead to the breakdown of contact and residence arrangements in the first place. I did therefore commission my officials to work with colleagues in the Department of Health under the joint Private Family Law Early Intervention Action Plan to identify means of helping parents agree arrangements as early as possible outside the acrimonious court environment. A number of tools are in train and potential next steps are under consideration. Any other change to the private law legislative framework would be a matter for the Departments of Finance and Health. I also asked my officials to undertake a review of civil legal aid with the aim of identifying a menu of potential options for improving access to justice, value for money and most importantly better outcomes for citizens. I expect to receive advice shortly to inform decisions on the future direction of legal aid reform which I will share with the Assembly and stakeholders at the appropriate time.  If you have evidence or information about how legal aid is being misused at present that you would be willing to share, I will ensure that this is incorporated into the review process and ask my officials to bring forward potential policy responses in their advice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a new Victims Commissioner will be appointed.
answer_text: We have instructed our officials to formally proceed with the recruitment process for a new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.Preparations are at an advanced stage, and we anticipate that the recruitment competition will be formally launched in the Autumn.The Commission for Victims and Survivors is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI) and, as such, the appointment process must comply with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments. The process could take six months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the anticipated timeline for legislation she intends to introduce during the remainder of the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: Having reviewed and re-prioritised my legislative plans upon my return as Minister of Justice, I now intend to bring forward three Assembly Bills during the time remaining in this shortened mandate.They include:a mixed-content Justice Bill – which was introduced to the Assembly on Tuesday 17 September 2024;a Sentencing Bill – for planned introduction by Autumn 2025; anda Victims Bill – for planned introduction by March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the enforcement that takes place regarding the breaking and implementation of court orders in relation to parental contact.
answer_text: Magistrates’ courts can deal with breaches of contact orders under their general enforcement powers (Article 112 of the Magistrates Courts (Northern Ireland) Order 1981), which allow the court to fine or imprison a parent who has failed to comply with the order. More complex contact cases may be transferred to the higher courts, and they can attach a penal notice to a contact order. Such a notice warns that breach of the order could result in imprisonment for contempt of court. However, the exercise of any of these powers in any individual case is a judicial decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of obscene publication offences against children recorded in the last five years.
answer_text: Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not hold information in relation to victims of offences, other than where victim details are specified in an offence description.  Offence descriptions relating to obscene publications do not specify details of any victims involved.  However, there were no convictions at courts for exhibiting or publishing obscene articles or for possessing obscene articles with a view to publication in the five year period up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) actions; and (ii) policies that have been established to support retail and other customer-facing workers who have reportedly been subject to an increase in physical assaults and threats.
answer_text: Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm. In light of increasing concerns about assaults on a range of workers who serve the public, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a person engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty, or providing services to the public.  The new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty in the magistrates’ courts than that currently available for the offence of common assault which applies regardless of the victim’s occupation. This would include retail workers.Alongside this new offence I intend to make assault on such a person in more serious cases a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect that fact in their sentencing decisions.I intend to bring this new offence forward via my proposed Sentencing Bill later in this mandate.Policy responsibility for the retail sector lies with the Department for the Economy (DfE).  It would, therefore, be for DfE to provide details of any specific policies that have been established to support the retail sector.  My Department remains fully committed to working with all partners, including businesses, the community and voluntary sector, the PSNI, as well as other Departments and organisations to ensure that we have a community where we all feel safe to live and workThe Department of Justice is a member of the Business Crime Partnership (BCP).  The BCP is a networking, information sharing and engagement forum, set up to share best practice and crime prevention advice to support businesses.  Membership is open to organisations who wish to collaborate with statutory agencies to reduce crime and the fear of crime affecting the business community.Additionally, my Department, alongside the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides approximately £4.25m annual funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnership (PCSPs) to support a range of community safety initiatives.  Each PCSP addresses the issue of business crime within their respective action plans and ongoing initiatives include on-street projects such as Community Safety Warden schemes, radio link and text alert schemes as well as the Business Watch scheme which provides crime prevention advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of offences the PSNI has recorded on sexual communication with a child in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of offences reported to the PSNI in relation to sexual communication with a child.  The recording of information in relation to offences reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI. The Department holds information on the number of convictions at courts.     Information on the number of convictions at courts for offences relating to sexual communications with a child in the five year period up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, has been provided.
