Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at courts where there was a conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, Harbour Police, Airport Constabulary, NCA or PBNI.4. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and magistrates' courts.5. Figures relate to offences prosecuted under Article 22A of the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the amount her Department has recouped in unpaid domestic and non-domestic rates in the last five years; and (ii) any plans in place to address the rising level of unpaid rates.
answer_text: (i) The reported level of unpaid rates as at the 31st March each year is a cumulative figure that includes all unpaid rates remaining from prior rating years. Rating debt carried forward annually is subject to the same rate billing, collection and legal recovery cycles as current year rate assessments. The tables below show the amount of unpaid rates reported at year end for the last five rating years (at 31st March) split for Domestic (Table 1) and Non-Domestic properties (Table 2), and the comparative position at 1st September 2024 of unpaid rates that are still subject to rate billing, collection and legal recovery processes. Table 1 – Unpaid Domestic Rates for 2019/20 to 2023/24 (as at 31st March each year) and debt remaining at 1st September 2024*Rating YearDomestic Year End Debt (at 31st March)Comparative Domestic Debt Remaining at 1st September 2024Reduction in Domestic Debt at 1st September 20242019-2020£64,882,599£19,314,279£45,568,3202020-2021£83,377,203£28,886,154£54,491,0502021-2022£91,011,865£41,029,108£49,982,7572022-2023£99,728,343£57,137,127£42,591,2162023-2024£97,884,930£83,693,327£14,191,603* timing of Direct Debit collection and payment plans can influence the amount remaining at a mid year position. Table 2 – Unpaid Non-Domestic Rates for 2019/20 to 2023/24 (as at 31st March each year) and debt remaining at 1st September 2024*Rating YearNon-Domestic Year End Debt (at 31st March)Comparative Non-Domestic Debt Remaining at 1st September 2024Reduction in Non-Domestic Debt at 1st September 20242019-2020£60,673,007£13,930,628£46,742,3792020-2021£71,824,791£16,648,350£55,176,4412021-2022£62,279,367£22,442,071£39,837,2962022-2023£74,578,693£37,514,691£37,064,0032023-2024£82,652,167£69,324,644£13,327,523* timing of Direct Debit collection and payment plans can influence the amount remaining at a mid year position. (ii) Land & Property Services (LPS) has robust billing, collection and legal recovery processes in place to maximise rate collection and reduce rating debt. This includes issuing annual rate bills and final demands for payment. Where rates have not been paid in full, recovery procedures are initiated, these include formal written correspondence, e-mails and direct telephone contact with ratepayers. Legal recovery actions are progressed for persistent non-payment. These actions include progression through the Magistrate Courts, the Enforcement of Judgement’s Office and bankruptcy proceedings. Ratepayers experiencing difficulties with rates are encouraged to engage with LPS as soon as possible to discuss payment options which can include restructuring current payment plans or establishing payment arrangements to enable payment of rates over a longer period of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department (i) has taken in the last three years; and (ii) plans to take in the next three years, to give Northern Ireland a competitive advantage over the Republic of Ireland as a tourist destination.
answer_text: Over the last three years, my Department has provided £9.5m of funding to Tourism NI for marketing campaigns in the South.  As a result, NISRA reported that in 2023, we attracted 1.3 million visitors from the South, generating 3 million bednights, with an associated expenditure of £268m. Tourism Ireland has presented the North as a key part of the island of Ireland campaigns globally across the last three years. In 2023, North of Ireland related publicity from Tourism Ireland had a reach of over half a billion people. Their campaigns had Derry Girls actors at the core, bringing a focus to the North as part of their overall global campaigns.  Through its International Tourism Events Fund, Tourism NI has provided over £1.9m funding support over the last three years to events to enhance the quality of their programme and increase their international marketing. 	In this period around 1,500 industry spaces on international platforms have been awarded to local operators to engage with key markets. An annual programme of familiarisation trips is delivered bringing international operators into the region to experience the destination first hand.  Around 110 trips, attended by 1,750 international operators have taken place over the last three years.To secure business tourism, Tourism NI work with a range of stakeholders such as Visit Belfast, Visit Derry and Tourism Ireland to conduct sales and marketing activity to promote the North as a business tourism destination.  In 2023/24, funding secured current and future international conferences worth £43.6 million in economic value to these cities.  Department of the Economy funding promotes the North as a world class golfing destination. In the past three years this has included 18 trade shows, providing opportunities for over 75 golf tourism suppliers. The value of Golf Tourism in the North has risen from £52m in 2019 to £68m in 2022.The Irish Open returned to Royal County Down this month, the venue has been voted the number one golf course in the world. The return of The Open has also been secured for 2025, providing another opportunity to showcase our golf tourism offering to around 250,000 spectators who attend, and across the world through the media coverage.	My economic vision set out aims to create a more regionally balanced economy by developing industries with a strong sub-regional presence such as tourism and hospitality.I recently established the Tourism Partnership Board consisting of representatives from both the tourism industry and hospitality sector, Executive Departments and local government to support my Department in the co-design and development of a 10 Year Tourism Action Plan, which I anticipate will be published by the end of the year.This will determine the strategic direction of Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland in driving forward growth in the sector.In its 2024 Business Plan, Tourism Ireland has already committed to targeting +6.5% revenue growth on average per annum to 2030 for overs

answer_text: Total funding (including core and jurisdictional):Financial YearFunding provided by Department for the Economy2023/24£18.3m2022/23£18.1m2021/22£22.9mFor 2024/25 I have increased core funding by an additional £6.143m to bring DfE funding in line with agreed ratios.Tourism Ireland is a company set up under Irish Company Law. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether any prisoners released in England and Wales under the early release scheme will be in residing in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail any processes in place to link such prisoners to the Probation Board for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold any information in regard to whether prisoners released early in England & Wales will be residing in Northern Ireland. To date, the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) has not received any requests from HM Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS) (England & Wales) for post-custody management of an offender released under the early release scheme. Should HMPPS request that PBNI caretake a prisoner on Licence on their behalf, they will provide all relevant risk assessment information (Pre-sentence Report, criminal record, assessment of likelihood re-offending). A home circumstances assessment is completed by the PBNI to assess the suitability of the home address and complete any relevant safeguarding checks prior to acceptance of the individual. Enforcement responsibility will remain with HMPPS in the event of a recall application to custody, and as such there is a requirement for an allocated Probation Officer in England and Wales for ongoing liaison throughout the duration of the Licence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of funding allocated to the Office of the Attorney General for Northern Ireland through the Executive programme for tackling paramilitary activity and organised crime in the last five years.
answer_text: During Phase 1 (2016-2021) of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), the Office of the Attorney General for Northern Ireland was provided with a total of £127,000 for work aimed at promoting the rule of law. No further investment was sought from EPPOC by the Office of the Attorney General after the financial year 2020/21.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what action he intends to take to support further education institutions to reduce the level of economic inactivity within the workforce.
answer_text: Our economic inactivity level rate is currently over 27%. Almost 60% of people with a disability are economically inactive. This compares to 16% for people without a disability. In addition, 31% of women are classified as inactive, compared to 24% of men. Further education institutions support participation in education and learning for cohorts who experience barriers such as women returners and people with a disability. This is seen in particular through a number of targeted interventions such as Skills for Life and Work, Skill Up, Step Up and All Age Apprenticeships. There is also a range of financial support available to FE learners including the Additional Support Fund for learners with a disability as well as support towards childcare costs. Ultimately, this is a complex and persistent issue which will require a range of approaches and collaboration across government agencies and the private sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people currently in temporary housing in the Upper Bann area; and (ii) the steps he is taking to support the Housing Executive in order to speed up the process of moving tenants out of temporary housing and into permanent housing.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024 contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024.	Section 3 provides details on households in temporary accommodation. The Housing Executive’s Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation can be found at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/fd320853-cf6e-4403-908f-d0a0b7dbbaea/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf.  I am also looking at a broad range of initiatives to address the wider issue of housing provision, a part of which, includes expediting the process of moving households from temporary to permanent accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the reform of the Housing Executive points system; and (ii) when the review will be complete.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing with the Fundamental Review of Allocations. Stage One was implemented on 31 January 2023. I gave approval to Stage Two in June 2024, and my officials are now working with the Housing Executive on the early stages of Stage Three. The fourth and final phase is due to be completed in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently on the Housing Executive list to be rehoused in the Upper Bann area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that, as at the end of June 2024, there were 3,024 applicants for the Upper Bann Parliamentary Constituency.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive or the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations directly. Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Portadown College, including (i) the preferred site; and (ii) the current estimated timescales associated with (a) commencement on site; and (b) completion of the project.
answer_text: The Portadown College major capital works project is in the very early stages of development. Procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team (ICT) for the project is in progress, with appointment anticipated in December.Once appointed, the ICT will prepare a Technical Feasibility Report which will detail potential options and costs to provide the school with suitable accommodation.  Unfortunately, until the Technical Feasibility Report is approved by the Department, and the associated Business Case is completed and approved, it is not possible to provide details of the preferred site or potential timescales for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will engage with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Human Resources to change the Staff Handbook paragraph 3.09 relating to Excusal of Sick due to injuries, disease and assault to include stress and psychological injuries brought about due to work-based environments.
answer_text: The NICS Injuries, Disease and Assault policy (section 3.09 of the HR Handbook) provides for excusals of sickness absences from counting towards the maximum period of paid sickness absence (i.e. up to six months of full pay and up to six months of half pay) in certain circumstances. 	The normal period of paid sickness absence prescribed by the NICS Sickness Absence policy may be waived, and an extension of up to a maximum of six months full pay granted, where an industrial injury or disease (including stress or psychological injury) meets the qualifying conditions of the Civil Service Injury Benefit Scheme.   All Civil Service HR policies are kept under review to reflect best practice and legislative changes, and a rolling programme of priority policy renewal has been developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a statement of special educational needs in Upper Bann who are still awaiting an individual learning assistant.
answer_text: This information is not held or recorded by my department or the Education Authority Statutory Assessment and Review Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for Portadown Integrated Primary and Nursery School, including (i) the preferred site; and (ii) the current estimated timescales associated with (a) commencement on site; and (b) completion of the project.
answer_text: (i) Business Case approval for the new school build project was granted on 11 May 2023. The preferred site option identified in the Business Case was Mandeville Road North, Portadown.(ii) The current programme estimates commencement on site in August 2025 with completion in January 2027, however these projected timelines may be impacted by several factors including site acquisition and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the measures that are taken to ensure that those eligible for legal aid, and meet the financial criteria, do not have funds outside this jurisdiction that would have made them ineligible.
answer_text: As part of the application process for legal aid, financial eligibility assessments are completed to ensure those who apply meet the financial criteria. Some applicants will be financially eligible due to being in receipt of specific benefits. This outlined in Regulation 5, The Civil Legal Services (Financial) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.Applicants who are not in receipt of those benefits are subject to a full financial eligibility assessment and must provide details of all income and capital. Applicants will be required to provide evidence of bank accounts and any other money or property held. This includes details on funds held outside the jurisdiction. If there is any evidence of further funds, whether in this jurisdiction or outside the jurisdiction, which have not been declared, for example by reference to transactions in bank statements, this will also be investigated before eligibility is determined.If a person has concerns regarding a grant of legal aid to their opponent or wishes to provide the Agency with copies of documentation to substantiate their concerns, these should be sent by email to: enquiries@lsani.gov.uk. Such concerns and correspondence are welcomed and considered carefully by the Agency on receipt. Where a person suspects any abuse of the legal aid system, they may also contact the Agency’s Counter Fraud Unit directly in a number of ways. Counter-Fraud Hotline 0800 328 7360, via email counterfraud@lsani.gov.uk or the Agency’s dedicated Legal Aid Fraud reporting e-portal Report legal aid fraud online | nidirect, which also provides an option to report the matter anonymously.  Upon notification of a suspected fraud, an investigation will be conducted by the Counter Fraud Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the progress on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: Question 12 - AQO 651/22-27Last year, my Department completed a review of current and planned broadband coverage to identify where intervention under Project Gigabit is needed.  Pre-procurement meetings with companies interested in the project were held in May of this year, and an Outline Business Case for the project is being completed.  I expect a procurement to commence later in the summer, aiming to award a contract for the delivery of gigabit-capable services by the end of the financial year, subject to the necessary approvals and Value for Money criteria being met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will bring forward legislation to ban the sale and use of (i) prong collars; and (ii) electric collars for dogs and cats.
answer_text: My Department’s code of practice for the welfare of dogs recommends that only positive, reward-based training should be used. Further, that potentially painful or frightening training methods, such as e-collars, should be avoided.	In this context I am aware of the merits of banning these devices and I am keen to explore if this could be achieved, within the lifetime of this mandate, subject to available resources. I intend to discuss this and other animal welfare interventions with stakeholders over the time ahead.To note, while the use of shock or e-collars is not banned in Northern Ireland, a person can only use an e-collar on an animal if it does not cause unnecessary suffering. For example, if a person sets the intensity of the device at a level which is deemed to be excessive, they could be causing that animal unnecessary suffering and liable to prosecution under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will commission a review of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (“the 2011 Act”) is a key piece of legislation which provides the foundation upon which statutory bodies, including local councils, the PSNI and my Department, carry out their duties in order to improve animal welfare.		It is a robust piece of legislation which provides a range of powers for dealing with animal welfare cases. These include being proactive in dealing with cases in order to prevent unnecessary suffering and tough fines and penalties for serious animal welfare offences. The 2011 Act is on a par with animal welfare legislation in the rest of the United Kingdom.  		Whilst I currently have no plans to review the 2011 Act, I wish to engage with relevant stakeholders over the time ahead in terms of future animal welfare policy and legislation. The suitability and capability of all legislation is something I am willing to consider as and when evidence is presented for change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what consideration she has given to reviewing tuition fees.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils that avail of special educational needs transport in the Upper Bann area.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the home to school transport policy and the Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. The EA informs me that is unable to provide the number of pupils that avail of special educational needs transport on a constituency basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11034/22-27, to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Justice for Ireland in relation to (i) the effects of illegal migration; (ii) the deployment of 200 Gardaí to the border to deal with those people who cross the border illegally; and (iii) any illegal migrants, who may have broken the law in Ireland, being returned to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Immigration is a reserved matter. Primary responsibility for policing of immigration lies with Border Force and Home Office Immigration Enforcement (HOIE). Policing of immigration crime by the PSNI is an operational matter, and I am committed to respecting the Chief Constable’s independence on operational matters.I have not had any specific discussions with Minister McEntee in relation to policing matters linked to asylum seekers, refugees or illegal migration in either jurisdiction, including those moving between jurisdictions, as these are not devolved matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of school bus passes in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the home to school transport policy and the Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. Whilst my Department receives information on home to school transport expenditure for financial monitoring purposes, it is not disaggregated geographically. The EA informs me that it is not possible to provide the cost of school bus passes in the last five years broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils that (i) avail of the Education Authority music service; and (ii) receive instrument rental discount, in Upper Bann, broken down by (a) primary school; and (b) post-primary school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), who have operational responsibility for the Music Service, have provided the following information.The EA Music Service charges schools directly for the provision of instrumental tuition annually, based on an hourly rate of £33. It is the responsibility of each school to pay for this provision. Some schools pass a charge to parents dependant on class/group size while some schools subsidise the charge to parents at their discretion. Charges to schools for whole-class tuition are not passed on to parents.The number of pupils who avail of the EA Music Service in the former South Eastern Library Board area, which includes Upper Bann is:Primary - 7626 (2421 instrumental + 5205 whole-class Pathways*) Post-Primary - 1159 (1067 school based + 92 Music Centre based).In the same area, the number of pupils who hire musical instruments from the EA Music Service at a reduced rate, as they are entitled to free school meals is:Primary – 170 Post-Primary – 26*All children in whole class Pathways programmes are provided with an instrument to participate with no rental fee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Home to School Transport policy.
answer_text: A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused, by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy supports other policies within the Department, such as parental preference and Special Educational Needs (SEN).  My Department is currently considering the report published by the Independent Review of Education and work is progressing on an end-to-end review of SEN.  Work is also underway on an action plan arising from the EA Landscape Review, to include detailed consideration of the operational delivery of services such as school transport.It is important that any review of the Home to School Transport Policy complements and can build on the outcomes from these workstreams and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my Department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of her Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: Currently, there are no functions of the Department of Justice which are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State, pursuant to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of her Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: No parts of my Department are currently subject to the direction and control of a Secretary of State. The guidance that accompanied the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 did not list any specific function of my Department which would be subject to Direction and Control, however where my Department exercises a function that is supportive of the parts of other Departments that are subject to that Direction and Control, my Department may be required to complete those functions subject to Direction and Control.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of their Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: There are no functions of The Executive Office subject to regulation 3 of Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of his Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: Department of Health (DoH) senior officials engaged regularly with the Windsor Framework Task Force (WFTF) within United Kingdom Government as part of overall discussions on the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.  Through direct engagement with the WFTF, I understand that the Secretary of State has exercised discretion not to apply direction and control in a broad range of areas, with matters engaged by the Regulations, including two statutory areas of responsibility identified for my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of her Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: None of the functions of my Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of his Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: The Secretary of State has exercised discretion not to apply direction and control in a broad range of areas, with matters engaged by the Regulations principally concerning a limited set of 19 goods regulations in the DAERA portfolio.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of his Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: None of the functions of the Department of Education are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of his Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: I am informed by my officials that there are no functions of my Department subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State due to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to regulation 3 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, what functions of his Department are subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.
answer_text: The day-to-day functions of my department have not changed since the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024. However, there are currently 19 pieces of EU legislation where the Secretary of State has not exercised his discretion not to exercise direction and control, that is, they are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State. A list of these has been deposited in the Assembly Library and can be accessed via the link below.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/raise/deposited-papers/2024/dp1767.pdfFrom 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised discretion in the areas of disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these matters are now under my ministerial responsibility.This discretion should be understood in the context of the Secretary of State’s criteria for retaining direction and control, which includes:operational arrangements at Northern Ireland ports and airports concerning the movement of goods between GB and NI;policy developments and legislation that would impact movements between Great Britain and Northern Ireland; andnew EU laws which replace regulations under Annex II of the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) what financial bid the Executive Office made for setting up the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression; and (ii) whether this is a recurring bid.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) did not submit any Resource bids in relation to the establishment of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression as part of the 2024-25 Budget process.  TEO did advise as part of their return that they would bid during the in-year monitoring process when necessary.  TEO did submit a Capital DEL bid for £54k in relation to leasehold property for the language bodies. This bid was not recurrent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the roads that have been identified for resurfacing in the 2024-25 financial year, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total amount of funding for blue/green infrastructure; (ii) how this funding has been allocated; and (iii) whether there has been any unspent funding and the rationale for this, in the last three years.
answer_text: The Blue Green Fund was last launched in 2022/23. The amount recorded through the Blue Green Fund for 2022/23 is detailed are as follows:£mGreenways (including contributions from DAERA and DfC)3.09Divisional Roads8.8Active Travel Schemes to Councils0.37Funding to other Departments1.96Blue Green projects to Councils0.45Living Places and Spaces 0.74Strangford Ferry Decarbonisation 0.04DVA E-vehicles0.25Solar Panels0.04Electric Vehicle Match funding 0.47 In addition, approximately £6.5m worth of letters of offer were issued to Councils for multi-year greenway projects beyond 2022/23 – the Department continue to engage with Councils on these projects. The details for 2021/22 are as follows: AIMS Portal (niassembly.gov.uk)The details for 2020/21 are as follows: AIMS Portal (niassembly.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 11035/22-27, to detail (i) the financial bid their Department made for the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression; and (ii) whether this will be a recurring bid.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (The Act) for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.This will be in-line with our decisions.Our current plans include provision for the preparatory work on the Rights, Language and Identify bodies.  Bids will be required for further resources following agreement on the way forward.Members will be kept informed on progress.

answer_text: In 2023-24 my Department invested a total of £108.7m in Rural Communities, where this can be separately identified, as detailed in the table below.DfI 2023-24 Investment in Rural CommunitiesAreaDescriptionInvestment 2023-24*£mAdditional InformationTransportMajor Projects24.4Attributed to road schemes in rural areas such as:A8 Belfast to Larne; A26 Glarryford - A44 Dualling; A31 Magherafelt Bypass; A5WTC; A6 Randalstown to Castledawson; A6 Londonderry to Dungiven; A2 Broadbridge; St Patricks Barracks; A24 Ballynahinch Bypass; Newry Southern Relief Road; A1 Junctions - Phase 2; A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass; A5/B48 Strathroy Link Road / Strule Campus; and Cookstown Eastern Distributor.Roads Recovery Fund8£10m was allocated to the Roads Recovery Fund, of which £8m was directed towards rural roads. Translink does not report against route types. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of jobs created through foreign investment in the last three years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2023 being the most recent available.  During the period 2020-21 to 2022-23, Invest NI provided support to externally owned businesses that will lead to the creation of 5,564 new jobs over the next 3 to 5 years and will result in an increase of £158m in annual salaries.In addition to support offered towards job creation over the past three years, Invest NI secured investment of nearly £200m by externally owned businesses to undertake other business development activities such as R&D/innovation, skills development, etc. This investment will underpin business competitiveness and lead to further growth and employment opportunities.For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of times children with special educational needs were unable to go to school due to the unavailability of their regular school transport buses in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. The EA transport service has provided details of the number of occasions on which there was disruption to all or part of a daily journey on an EA bus service for children with special educational needs (SEN). Not all these occasions will have resulted in absence from school as in these circumstances, parents make alternative transport arrangements for their children where possible. The EA is unable to provide figures on the number of times children with SEN were unable to attend school due to the unavailability of their regular school transport. Number of occasions on which all or part of a SEN bus service was disrupted*2021/222022/232023/24696457586*The EA does not hold these figures prior to 2021/2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support provided to looked after children by the Education Authority, to include (i) the number of staff employed, including Education Welfare Officers, youth workers and administrative staff; and (ii) the number of vacancies at each grade, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Within the Education Authority (EA), Children Looked After (CLA) are supported across a range of pupil support services, depending on any assessed learning need they may have, as would happen for any child. The EA provides a dedicated support service for primary aged CLA in recognition of the identified changes in their level of educational attainment as they move into Key Stage 2.  The Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service (PCLAAS) is a regional early intervention, prevention and advocacy service.  Support offered by the PCLAAS is multidisciplinary and interagency, consisting of social workers, teachers, youth workers, practitioner clinical psychologists, consultancy occupational therapy, administration and planning and research staff.The staffing complement for the PCLAAS is for twenty-nine posts (including four currently vacant posts) and it is not structured to allow this to be broken down by constituency.  I can, however, confirm that support is offered by PCLAAS to all primary schools with CLA.Within the post-primary school sector, support is provided by the Education Welfare Service (EWS) with referrals for CLA being allocated to an Education Welfare Officer (EWO).  The EWO’s liaise with the relevant Health & Social Care Trust, school staff, parents and/or foster parents and other agencies to ensure the needs of the child are addressed and that the child has access to appropriate education provision and support.There are 137 EWO posts (including 7 vacancies).  Information on these posts is not available by constituency, however, EWO posts are located in Ballymena, Dundonald, Belfast, Armagh and Omagh.  The vacancies at present are Armagh area (3), Dundonald area (3) and Ballymena (1).Industrial action is continuing to have a significant impact of the Education Welfare service.  In spite of the impact of industrial action and vacant posts within the EWS, the EWS has developed a number of operational principles to manage waiting lists for support for CLA.Where a child looked after is on the waiting list, they will continue to be supported by staff in their educational setting. In addition to this, CLA can avail of supports from a statutory social worker as part of the Health and Social Care Trust’s functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps being taken to ensure that adequate (i) nursery; and (ii) primary one places are available to meet demand in Upper Bann.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.  Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting.  Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 146 funded pre-school places still available in the Upper Bann Parliamentary Constituency area at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process.The EA PEG continue to monitor pre-school provision in all areas including Upper Bann.In respect of primary admissions, my department works with schools to create additional capacity in sectors and areas where oversubscription arises.  The EA has advised that every child resident in Upper Bann has secured a P1 place for September 2024.Children with a statement of specialist educational needs (SEN) follow a different process than the general admissions process, one which seeks to secure an appropriate placement which meets their individual needs.  There is significant demand for specialist education places particularly for pre-school and P1 children, with pressures for both special school places and specialist provision in mainstream schools in the Upper Bann area.  The EA, supported by my Department, is working to create additional capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient appropriate provision for all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps are in place to ensure the number of pupils left with no offer of a primary school or post-primary school place this year is significantly reduced.
answer_text: My department works with schools to ensure sufficient capacity exists in areas and school sectors where oversubscription arises.  This has been done proactively, through the allocation of additional places to post-primary schools by way of the Year 8 Additional Places Exercise, and in response to demand as it arises at both primary and post-primary level, through the Temporary Variation process.My department also works with the Education Authority (EA) to ensure parents have access to appropriate and timely information to support them in submitting their applications at the right time, while nominating a sufficient and realistic list of school nominations.The primary school admissions process has concluded for 2024-25 and almost 21,000 children secured a place with only 16 children still to be placed.  This is a reduction from 27 children in 2023.  Parents of these children may now apply to schools with places available and almost 7,000 places remain available across Northern Ireland.The post-primary admissions process concluded on 18 May with 136 children still to be placed and over 3,000 places remaining available.  This process also saw a reduction from the position in 2023 when 206 children were still to be placed at the same stage.The position for September 2024 in relation to school placements for children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) remains challenging.  A significant programme of work is ongoing to create additional capacity across the education system.There has been significant progress made by the EA, supported by my department, in developing solutions with schools across all sectors to meet the increasing demand for special education provision.  There will be a focussed effort over the coming weeks to engage with schools to ensure that all learners have access to an appropriate education placement which fully meets their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children left with no offer of a school place in the last five years, broken down by (i) primary schools; (ii) post-primary schools; and (iii) Special Educational Needs schools.
answer_text: Detail on the number of children who were still to be placed at the end of the primary process (as at late April in each year) and the post-primary process (which generally closes in mid-May each year) is set out in the following tables.Primary:YearNumber202074202174202237202327202416Post-primary:YearNumber20201742021280202224420232062024136At the close of both procedures, parents are supplied with details of schools with places remaining available to enable them to secure a placement well in advance of the commencement of the next school year.Children with statements of special educational needs (SEN) are placed in special schools and special education provision through a process based on consultation between parents, schools and the Education Authority (EA).  As such, children are not placed in special schools via open enrolment.  Consultations and the naming of a school on statements occurs throughout the school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the properties owned by his Department in Upper Bann; and (ii) how many of these properties lie vacant.
answer_text: My Department rarely has to purchase properties, unless required for major schemes. These are usually acquired through Blight, where the landowner asserts that the property has been severely impacted by the design of the scheme, and submits a claim for the Department to purchase the property. There are no such properties in the Upper Bann Constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail the properties owned by his Department in Upper Bann; and (ii) how many of these properties lie vacant.
answer_text: My Department owns four properties in Upper Bann: Banbridge Jobs and Benefits Office (JBO); Marlborough House, Craigavon; Portadown Crown Buildings; and Lurgan Crown Buildings.  I understand that the Department for Economy has a small presence in Banbridge JBO, Portadown Crown Buildings and Lurgan Crown Buildings.None of these properties are vacant.

answer_text: We refer the member to the written answer for AQO 346/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to their Department's plans for the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, to detail (i) the proposed budget for the first five years; (i) the number of staff to be employed; and (iii) the proposed pay scales for employees.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (The Act) for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.This will be in-line with our decisions.A business case in respect of establishing the new bodies is well advanced and is currently in the process of being approved. Given this, and as Ministerial decisions are required, it would be premature at this stage to provide any detail in respect of potential costs. Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she has had engagement with her counterpart in the Irish government in relation to the policing of illegal migration from Northern Ireland to the Irish Republic; and (ii) whether she has had further engagement with the Home Office to identify the source of this illegal migration into Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Immigration is a reserved matter. Primary responsibility for policing of immigration lies with Border Force and Home Office Immigration Enforcement (HOIE). Policing of immigration crime by the PSNI is an operational matter, and I am committed to respecting the Chief Constable’s independence on operational matters.I liaise with the Irish Justice Minister on a regular basis on matters of mutual interest. I have not had any specific discussions with Minister McEntee in relation to policing matters linked to asylum seekers, refugees or illegal migration in either jurisdiction, including those moving between jurisdictions, as these are not devolved matters. I have, under the terms of the Inter-Governmental Agreement, discussed Human Trafficking and Modern Slavery, which is a devolved matter.  I have had no recent engagement with Home Office Ministers on these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of fraud cases in relation to Criminal Injuries Compensation claims that have been (i) reported; (ii) investigated; and (iii) resulted in prosecution, in the last ten years.
answer_text: In the last ten financial years there are no records of any criminal injury compensation claims being reported or investigated for alleged fraud. Consequently, there has not been any prosecutions arising from fraudulent applications for compensation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the role of Intertrade UK in the delivery plan of his Department's A 10X Economy strategy.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Ministerial Code has been updated to reflect the provisions in the Executive Committee (Functions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2020.
answer_text: Amendments to the Ministerial Code to incorporate changes made by the Executive Committee (Functions) Act 2020 were agreed in February 2022.However, in the absence of a First Minister and deputy First Minister it was not possible to seek the consequent agreement of the Assembly.  Ministers will consider the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what role her officials have in dealing with suspected fraud in (i) her Department; (ii) arm's-length agencies funded by her Department.
answer_text: In line with the Department of Justice’s (DoJ) Anti-Fraud and Anti-Bribery Policy and Response Plan, the Department is responsible for considering all cases of suspected fraud relating to the core Department and its Executive Agencies.In line with the Policy and Response Plan, DoJ officials conduct an initial fact-finding investigation into suspected frauds and report the suspected fraud, as necessary, to the Department of Finance and the Comptroller and Auditor General in line with the requirements of Managing Public Money Northern Ireland.  Should the initial investigation indicate there is prima facie evidence of fraud, officials will undertake a fuller investigation, including reporting to the Police Service of Northern Ireland if deemed necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the powers to control rents set out in Section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, adding 5C to the 2006 Order, expired on 28 October 2023, whether he has plans to renew or bring forward new legislation to protect private renters from rent rises.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to renew or introduce new legislation on rent controls.  Research carried out by my Department concluded that the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters was by ensuring the benefit system properly takes account of the cost of housing and by increasing housing supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what criteria do Victim Support NI apply when considering the validity or otherwise of supporting an application to the Criminal Injuries Compensation service.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Victim Support NI (VSNI) to deliver a range of support to victims claiming Criminal Injuries Compensation.  This includes providing assistance to any victim who wants to be supported to make an application to the Criminal Injuries Compensation service.  In doing so VSNI does not apply any criteria in terms of validity or otherwise and this service is available to any victim of crime who wishes to access it.  It is the remit of Criminal Injuries Compensation Services to determine whether a claim meets the eligibility criteria of the Criminal Injuries Compensation Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the footbridge at Moylinn, Craigavon, will be replaced; and (ii) a timeframe for completion.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to taking forward the replacement of Moylinn East Footbridge which will be subject to the completion of a business case, the need to secure funding, and statutory processes such as planning permission.  It is hoped that planning approval will be in place within the next 6 months, to allow the project to be procured, subject to available funding.Unfortunately, at this stage, we are not in the position to provide a timeframe for completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what impact the challenges faced by NI Water has on his Department’s ability to achieve its plans to build new social homes over the next five years.
To ask the Minister of Justice how many staff in the Northern Ireland Prison Service are currently on sick absence longer than three months, broken down by (i) grade; and (ii) position.
answer_text: At the 01 April 2024 a total of 60 Prison (operational) Grade staff were on sickness absence for three months or more.  Given the small numbers of staff in some of the named grades, the figures have been grouped into an overall figure in the table below:Grade:Number of staff absent 3 months or more:CPO/Main Grade Officer35Night Custody Officer 14Operational Support Grade/Prisoner Custody Officer6Senior Officer/Governor5

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many HM (a) Prison; and (b) Probation Service staff, based in England, are working with terrorism-related offenders in Northern Ireland; (ii) to detail the annual cost of the service; and (ii) how many offenders they are responsible for.
answer_text: The Department of Justice has published statutory guidance setting out the arrangements to assess and manage the risks posed by Terrorist Risk Offenders (TROs). This is available at: Guidance to agencies on Multi-Agency Review Arrangements (MARA) | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk)As noted in the guidance, the Department is responsible for the deployment of Supervising Officers whose role is to engage with TROs to support the assessment and management of their risk.  The Department has worked with His Majesty’s Prison and Probation Service (HMPPS) to secure Supervising Officers and administrative support to undertake this function.  6.5 full time equivalent HMPPS staff are assigned to deliver this service on the Department’s behalf. The Department has allocated £500,000 for the 2024/2025 year for delivery of this service. There are currently 42 individuals classified as TROs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the annual turnover of staff in the Northern Ireland Prison Service; and (ii) the actions she is taking to retain prison officers.
answer_text: The annual turnover of staff in the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) covering the period 1 April 2018 to 31 March 2023 is set out in the table below together with comparison data from the NICS and HMPPS: Uniformed Prison Staff NICS Overall HMPPS1 April 2018 - 31 March 20198.5%3.5%10.0%1 April 2019 - 31 March 20208.1%3.6%10.5%1 April 2020 - 31 March 20213.8%2.8%8.3%1 April 2021 – 31 March 20227.6%4.6%12.6%1 April 2022 – 31 March 20237.3%4.8%14.6%Please note that the data covering 1 April 2023 to 31 March 2024 is not yet available.The actions she is taking to retain prison officers:	RecruitmentIn the past year, the Prison Service, in partnership with NICS HR, has undertaken four operational grade recruitment competitions.  These were for Custody Prison Officer, Night Custody Officer, Prisoner Custody Officer Driver/Escort and Prisoner Custody Officer Escort.  Since January 2022, 315 officers have joined the Service, the majority of whom have been deployed to Maghaberry prison given the particular pressures experienced there.The Prison Service is also currently working with HR colleagues to launch two further competitions for Night Custody Officers and Prison Custody Officers.  This will ensure supply continues at all levels against the increasing prisoner population.Pay The rate of pay has significantly increased for NIPS Operational grade staff. The Prison Service Pay Review Body recommendations for 2022 and 2023 were accepted in full. This included revalorisation of 4% for most grades in 2022 and revalorisation of 7% for OSG, PCO and NCO grades and 5% for CPO, MGO post 2002, Senior Officer and Governor grades in 2023.For example, in 2021, the CPO minimum was £22,149. In 2023, it was £29,773. Over the course of two years, it has increased by £7,624 or some 34%.In addition, the Supplementary Risk Allowance was increased to £3,924 per annum from 1 September 2023.Training and Development In addition to the mandatory and operational training provided to staff the Prison Service is also working to develop a service-wide approach to leadership, learning and development.  This will complement the existing career development opportunities that exist for staff within the Service.Leaver FeedbackThe Prison Service is now introducing exit interviews to understand the reasons for departure and seek to address any issues raised that can improve retention rates.Staff Well Being & Support ServicesNIPS’ collaboration in the provision of support services with the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) continues to evolve, for both serving and retired staff.  In 2023, over 400 staff were trained and supported through PRRT.  The support provided included preventative resilience training to teams such as those working in Care and Supervision Units, to Governors and to new recruits.In October 2023 around 100 operational staff attended a new health and wellness day at PRRT aimed at providing advice on a range of well issues such as resilience, diet,

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work they have undertaken since taking up office to protect children from exploitation by paramilitary and criminal gangs.
answer_text: We refer the Member to the answer given to AQW 9497/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the (a) annual; and (b) continuous training requirements for prison officers; and (ii) what percentage of that training is being met within the required timeframe.
answer_text: (i) (a) Annual training for prisoner officers includes training for specialised purposes and key skills such as:Control and Restraint (C&R) BasicControl &Restraint (C&R) AdvancedFire TrainingFirearms Training(i) (b) Continuous training requirements are focused on meeting particular business needs.  The refresher timescale for each course varies.  Between 1 April 2023 and 31 March 2024, the following courses were delivered to prison officers:  First AidSupporting People At Risk (SPAR) EvolutionEmergency Control RoomIncident ManagementHostage NegotiatorPrisoner Development Unit SupportSeparated ConditionsCare and Supervision Unit (IT)(ii) The target is to have 80% of staff at each prison complete Control & Restraint Basic training annually.  Training courses are scheduled throughout the year to ensure this target is met.  Detailed below are the current completion figures at 16 April 2024: Maghaberry Prison					81%Magilligan Prison					70%Hydebank Wood Secure College		78%Prisoner Escort & Court Custody Service	76%Control & Restraint Advanced training is delivered annually to a number of staff based on identified business need in each establishment.  The levels of staff who have completed this training currently exceeds business needs.Refresher fire training is provided to prison officer and administrative staff throughout the training year at each establishment.  The current percentage of staff who have completed refresher training is set out below: Maghaberry Prison					49%Magilligan Prison					45%Hydebank Wood Secure College 		35%Prisoner Escort & Court Custody Service 	70%Work is ongoing to increase the number of staff who have received refresher Fire training.  The ability to release staff for training has been impacted by the pressures of a rising prison population and the need to balance safety and security of prisons with ensuring staff receive appropriate training.Firearms training is based on approved applications for a Personal Protection Weapon with 208 staff currently awaiting refresher training.  Long-term staff sick absence has impacted the delivery of this training.  This matter has now been resolved through the appointment of an additional instructor and range control officers.  A plan has been developed and is being implemented to clear the training backlog.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce a scheme, similar to that in the South, for constituents who have been effected by the mica, pyrite, and sulphites defective bricks.
answer_text: Currently there has been no evidence provided to my Department to suggest that there is a systemic issue within Northern Ireland pertaining to properties constructed using these defective materials. As such, I have no plans to introduce a scheme to provide financial assistance towards the remediation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether the new United Kingdom government definition of extremism will apply to the Northern Ireland justice system; (ii) for her assessment of the effect it will have in our separated prison regime; and (iii) for her assessment of how it will impact the work of the Probation Board.
answer_text: The new definition of terrorism announced by the United Kingdom Government recently will not apply in Northern Ireland. It will not therefore have any effect on the separated prison regime nor any impact on the work of the Probation Board for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce emergency legislation to overturn the convictions of sub-postmasters wrongly convicted in the Horizon scandal.
answer_text: I remain of the view that Northern Ireland should be included within the scope of the Post Office (Horizon System) Offences Bill, which was published on 13 March 2024 by the UK Government.  This remains the best way forward, given that postal services are a reserved matter.  This would also be the fastest legislative solution and avoid differential treatment between jurisdictions, including in terms of accessing compensation.  I recently met, along with the First and deputy First Minister, Minister Hollinrake and the NI Secretary of State to explore the option of Government amendments to include Northern Ireland within the Bill and am also liaising with NI Members of Parliament regarding the potential for them to table amendments if Government fail to do so.   	I have, however, also asked my officials to review what other options may be available to provide a remedy for sub-postmasters in Northern Ireland, should the decision remain that the legislation will not be extended, though none of these are optimal.  		These options include taking forward separate legislation through the Assembly to replicate the Westminster provisions, which would create significant additional delays even if accelerated passage is granted, given the time that would be required to develop a draft Bill. It would also have a significant impact on other legislation possible in this mandate.  An alternative approach would be to require the individuals affected to appeal their convictions through a bespoke administrative process in the courts.		Given work is ongoing to identify a way forward, I cannot yet confirm whether emergency legislation will be required.  However, I remain committed to ensuring that a swift and equitable remedy is delivered to Northern Ireland sub-postmasters as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the assessment he made before indicating that he would not consider raising tuition fees.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline how many MLA sponsored events were held in Parliament Buildings between March 2022 and March 2024.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her plans for reviewing the current processes for Access NI applications for individuals who move to new employment within the same sector.
answer_text: At present AccessNI disclosure certificates are accurate on the day they are issued, meaning it cannot be assumed that they continue to be accurate with the passage of time.  This is the main reason why employers, voluntary groups, etc seek a new disclosure when an individual commences a role with them.  My Department is working to introduce a portable disclosure solution that is practical and affordable; plans in this regard are currently predicated on joining the Disclosure and Barring Service’s ‘Update Service’ once the DBS complete its review of the current arrangements and a new and modernised solution is available.In the meantime, it is a matter for relevant departments, regulators, sectors, employers, in undertaking their own risk assessments, to determine the need for a new disclosure if someone moves role with the same employer / sector.  Factors they will wish to consider in this regard include:-The time that has passed since the last AccessNI check was completed;If the new role includes working with the same or with different groups, ie, children or vulnerable adults; a new check may be required if the group changes; andIf there has been a meaningful break in employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has in place for an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  Poverty is a complex and multi-faceted issue, and I am considering work to date and the next steps to be taken in relation to the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy. I am determined to deliver a Strategy that helps people in Northern Ireland get the best possible start in life; the opportunity to live in safe, stable homes, for example, and to have access to an excellent education and a good job. I look forward to working with my Executive colleagues to develop a sustainable, deliverable Strategy which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his proposals to address concerns about illegal puppy farming.
answer_text: Unlicenced, commercial breeding of dogs is a criminal offence.  Local councils are responsible for enforcement and conviction carries a maximum penalty of six months imprisonment and a fine of £5,000.My department continues to support local councils and other statutory agencies, such as the PSNI, to combat the illegal breeding and trafficking of pups. This includes cooperation under the Paws for Thought initiative.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the procedure for moving horses between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: Guidance on the movement of horses between Northern Ireland (NI) and Great Britain (GB) and vice versa is available on the DAERA website.There has been no change to the way horses are moved directly from NI to GB following the end of the EU-UK transition period on 31 December 2020.Horses can continue to move as before accompanied by a valid passport issued by any approved Passport Issuing Organisation.However under the terms of the Windsor Framework Agreement, GB to NI movements require an Export Health Certificate and pre-notification to DAERA SPS Inspection facilities at least 24 hours in advance of arrival through the submission of a Common Health Entry Document for live animals, known as a CHED A on the TRACES NT online system.I am aware that support is available from UK Government for those moving horses from GB to NI through the Movement Assistance Scheme.  This support includes the cost of Export Health Certificates and some costs incurred in meeting the certification requirements.In relation to the delivery of official checks at NI SPS Inspection facilities, my staff endeavour at all times to prioritise inspections on live animals to minimise delays and associated impact on travel times.For movements in both directions, DAERA continues to regulate animal welfare during domestic transport in line with The Welfare of Animals (Transport) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006, which have been implemented since 5th January 2007.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to recruit more driving examiners in Upper Bann to clear the backlog of applications for driving tests.
answer_text: Since the resumption of driving test services following the pandemic the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased their driving test capacity by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners, use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave, they also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted 68,540 driving tests, which is the highest number of driving tests conducted since 2009.  Almost 82% (56,132) of all driving tests in 2022-2023 were Category B (private car) tests.  Disappointingly, 2,812 people failed to attend their test, which is the highest number on record. The DVA is in the process of recruiting new full time driving examiners who on successful completion of their training will be allocated to test centres, including Craigavon, from late March 2024 onwards. The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month.  Additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.Due to the demand for services the advice to customers, is to book and pay for the earliest date available and check the booking system regularly for an earlier date as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available and when cancellations are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the occurrences of road traffic collisions on the Northway, Portadown.
answer_text: The majority of the collisions have occurred at the junctions along the A3 Northway, however there are no common causation factors identified that could be resolved through further safety engineering measures.  The Department has therefore no plans to implement any further road safety interventions on the Northway at this time.  However, the Department will continue to monitor the injury collision information provided by the PSNI to identify if further safety measures are required.I can confirm that the Department is planning to resurface a section of the Northway adjacent to Portadown Train Station and Mill Avenue, as well as refresh the road markings from West Street to the Seagoe Road junctions which will help with road safety on this busy A3 road.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the areas which will benefit from the additional £8.1 million for road resurfacing, broken down by constituency; and (ii) for a breakdown of how the funding will be allocated in Upper Bann.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the cases that were surveyed as part of the Northern Ireland Victim and Witness Survey; and (ii) whether the cases included victims of (a) domestic violence; and (b) sexual crimes.
answer_text: A total of 611 individuals (396 victims and 215 witnesses) were interviewed as part of the latest 2022/23 Northern Ireland Victim and Witness Survey. The offence categories covered by the survey included violence against the person, theft/handling of stolen goods, burglary, robbery, criminal damage and offences against the State. Victims of domestic violence and sexual crimes are not covered by the survey. The survey methodology is outlined in the Victim and Witness Experience of the Criminal Justice System: User Guide Victim and Witness Experience of the Northern Ireland Criminal Justice System: User Guide (justice-ni.gov.uk)  which is published on the Department of Justice’s website.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps are being taken to ensure the Victims' Payment Scheme information campaign for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme, reaches those entitled throughout the United Kingdom, not just those in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Victims’ Payments Scheme is administered by the Victims’ Payments Board on behalf of The Executive Office. To date, the VPB have received over 500 applications from outside this jurisdiction since the Scheme opened on the 31 August 2021.The VPB recognises that many relevant incidents occurred outside this jurisdiction and encourages anyone who may have suffered a permanent physical and/or psychological disablement through a relevant incident to consider making an application to the Scheme.  On 19 February 2024, the VPB launched an awareness campaign, working alongside the relevant authorities in England, Scotland, Wales and further afield, to increase awareness of the Scheme.  Leaflets and accompanying posters have been shared with health care providers, public sector bodies and with all MLAs, MPs, members of the Scottish and Welsh Parliaments, as well as with Dáil Éireann and Seanad Éireann.  The leaflet will also be distributed using the Churches network and victims' support services. In addition to literature, the VPB will be delivering a number of awareness sessions to groups who support victims throughout England, Scotland and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many people have availed of the Lone Pensioner Allowance; and (ii) the total amount given in rates reduction through the Lone Pensioner Allowance, in the last 5 years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services publishes annual statistics showing the number of persons and net amount of Lone Pensioner Allowance (LPA) awarded each year in Northern Ireland.https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/lone-pensioner-allowance-and-disabled-persons-allowance-statisticsAs of 31st March 2023, 33,710 owner-occupiers were awarded LPA totalling £7,699,507 across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people given a licence order as part of their sentence have had their licence revoked, broken down by (i) age; and (ii) gender, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response to AQW 7352/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many people who received a custodial sentence which included 50 per cent on licence, had that licence revoked, broken down by (i) gender; and (ii) age group, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 makes provision for the courts to impose fixed sentences which include a statutory period on licence.  These sentences fall into three categories: Determinate Custodial Sentences (DCS), Extended Custodial Sentences (ECS), and Indeterminate Custodial Sentences (ICS).  It is not the default position that a person must spend 50% of their overall sentence on licence. In terms of DCS sentences, the period of time a person must spend in custody must not exceed 50% of the sentence.  As a result, the period a person spends on licence may be longer than the period of time they spend in custody.   In terms of ECS sentences, the court must set an appropriate custodial term of at least 12 months.  The licence period must be of a length the court considers is necessary to protect the public from serious harm.In terms of ICS sentences, the court must set a minimum tariff to be served before the prisoner may be released.  After release, the person is subject to a licence period of at least ten years.The Department has recently started to collate information on the profile of recalled offenders to include age and gender.  A full breakdown of case data is not yet available.  However, general data indicates that the majority of individuals whose licences are revoked are males aged in their twenties and thirties, a profile which broadly reflects the prison population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will install speed control measures at Ballydown Primary school, Banbridge.
answer_text: The Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI. The current speed limits at this location on the Castlewellan Road are in line with our current policy document and considered to be appropriate.Ballydown Primary School was also assessed as part of the part-time 20mph speed limit at schools programme in accordance with the Department’s policy, ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070, and regrettably it did not score highly enough to be considered for inclusion in either of the first two tranches.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.The Department has recently provided additional safety measures at the school with double white line system to prevent overtaking and school warning signs.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding, or parking which causes an obstruction to other road (and footway) users should be directed to the police using the non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when his Department will open the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme.
