answer_text: Officials are at an advanced stage of finalising a Business Case for the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme. Subject to my consideration and budget availability I anticipate being in a position to make an announcement regarding the Scheme early in the 2024/2025 financial year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to replace the Moylinn East Footbridge, Craigavon.
answer_text: The Department remains committed to taking forward the replacement of Moylinn East Footbridge which will be subject to the completion of a business case, the need to secure funding, and statutory processes such as planning permission.  It is hoped that planning approval will be in place within the next 6 months, to allow the project to be procured, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will commence the A1 road safety improvements.
answer_text: I remain fully committed to delivering this important road safety project.  The statutory Orders for the scheme were made in summer 2021 and work on the project is continuing through the development of contract documents and Business Case. In recognition of current and future potential funding challenges, my Department is exploring options to deliver the project in smaller phased packages.  Details of the current status and timescales for of the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a1-junctions-phase-2-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many extended custodial sentences have been handed down in the last five years.
answer_text: An Extended Custodial Sentence is a public protection sentence which can be imposed when an offender has committed certain violent or sexual offences listed in schedule two of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008, on or after 15 May 2008, and the court believes that they are likely to commit further similar offences in the future.An Extended Custodial Sentence was handed down in 115 cases that were dealt with in the period 2018 to 2022, the most recent five years for which information is available.Convictions where there was an Extended Custodial Sentence, 2018 - 2022YearConvictions2018                         21 2019                         17 2020                         19 2021                         25 2022                         33 Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to cases where an extended custodial sentence was handed down for at least one offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated direct and indirect costs for each of the options considered for the Mobuoy Remediation Project, broken down for each of the zones.
answer_text: A draft Optimum Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site will shortly be issued for public consultation and a business case for the Mobuoy Remediation Project is currently under development and remains subject to appropriate assessments and required public finance approval processes. Until these processes are completed, I am unable to provide the breakdown requested.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions in his Department in relation to granting local authorities the power to take ownership of derelict buildings.
answer_text: My officials have been working to prepare a Dilapidation Bill. I am considering introducing the bill to the Assembly and will be receiving briefing from my officials in due course. Introduction would of course be subject to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. The proposed Bill would provide district councils with the full suite of powers similar to those currently available to their counterparts elsewhere in the UK in order to tackle dilapidated and / or dangerous buildings and neglected sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is considering developing greater enforcement powers for local councils to address public complaints relating to derelict sites.
answer_text: I am considering introducing a Dilapidation bill to the Assembly and will be receiving briefing from my officials in due course. Introduction would of course be subject to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. The proposed Bill would provide councils with a consistent, modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including clear powers to tackle dilapidated and / or dangerous buildings and neglected sites. These powers would be similar to those available to local authorities elsewhere in the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) derelict; and (ii) unoccupied homes in Upper Bann.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. You should contact the Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services (LPS) who maintain a register of Vacant Domestic Properties to obtain this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action his Department is taking to ensure there is no dereliction of listed buildings.
answer_text: To address the issue my Department is taking the following approach:	Keeping and monitoring statistics on the condition of listed buildings: Since 1993 the Department has funded Ulster Architectural Heritage (UAH) to compile and maintain a Heritage at Risk register for Northern Ireland;	Ensuring that owners, councils and the public are aware of the Department’s concern: The Department and UAH engage directly with owners, and the register is published on line at https://apps.communities-ni.gov.uk/harni/	Ensuring that information and support is available for owners, councils, and stakeholders: The department publishes guidance for owners and, with UAH, ensures that case studies and helpful information are available.  The Department supports the Architectural Heritage Fund (AHF) to engage with communities to develop solutions for HAR. The Repair Stream of the Historic Environment Fund has HAR as a priority category for support and with DAERA and AHF a dedicated grant scheme to tackle HAR in villages, the Village Catalyst Scheme, has been developed; 	The Department seeks to ensure that levers such as planning, regeneration, environmental, or fiscal policies and legislation that could positively influence the issues of dilapidation and listed buildings take this into account;	The Department is also working with District Councils to tackle the issue and has funded two councils to undertake condition surveys of important heritage at risk in their areas in the last year.		Necessarily constrained by budget this action is not sufficient to ensure no dereliction. Over recent years, as budgets have reduced, we have focused available funds on listed properties most in need, prioritising those on the Heritage at Risk (HARNI) register.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider (i) the use of electronic tagging; and (ii) the development of bail support and supervision schemes, to reduce the number of prisoners on remand.
answer_text: As Justice Minister, I respect the independence and autonomy of the Judiciary who consider each application for bail in accordance with the facts and evidence presented to the court.  However, I am keen to ensure that a range of viable alternatives to remand are available.Chapter 5 of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 makes provision for curfew and electronic monitoring requirements as a condition of court imposed bail.  These requirements can be used to restrict a person’s movement through a daily curfew period of up to 12 hours.  In line with the legislation, the courts routinely set electronic tagging as a condition of bail.   This can help to reduce the number of prisoners on remand by providing a public protection safeguard and alternative to custody.  I have asked my officials to consider the range of electronic monitoring technologies available; and how these might assist in reducing the prison population, whilst maintaining public safety.My officials are also engaging with their counterparts across the UK and Ireland to gain an understanding of the effectiveness of initiatives introduced.  This includes exploring the potential for a bail support pilot for women which, if proven successful, could be adapted to meet the needs of the larger male remand cohort.  A number of socio-economic factors are particularly prevalent among those individuals who come into contact with the justice system and must be taken into account in the design of such a pilot.  Many of these factors lie beyond the gift of justice, such as poor mental health, addictions and homelessness and will require significant incremental investment.I believe a cross-departmental approach is fundamental to supporting individuals at the earliest opportunity so that, where possible, they are prevented from coming into contact with the justice system at all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what improvements she will consider to the operation of pre-charge bail.
answer_text: My role in relation to pre-charge bail is to set the legislative framework, which is contained in the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE).  My officials are undertaking a wider review of PACE to consider what amendments are required, and they will engage with the Police Service of Northern Ireland in this regard.  The operation of pre-charge bail is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what actions she will take to reduce the number of prisoners on remand; and (ii) what measures she will put in place for safeguarding the public in relation to these actions.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response in AQW 6658/22-27. I am keen to ensure that a range of viable alternatives to remand are available to the Judiciary, who will ultimately determine whether bail should be granted and, whether certain bail conditions should be imposed to address any risks identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many prisoners on licence have been recalled to prison for breaching their licence, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In the last five calendar years (2019 – 2023) a total of 937 prisoners have been recalled to prison for breaching their licence.   This is broken down as follows:Licence type20192020202120222023Total Determinate, Extended and Indeterminate Custodial Sentence licences195156186160217914Conditional Early Release  Scheme licences001203Life licences5154520

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will ensure there is a formal link with Further Education lecturers and school teachers for future pay awards.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what mechanisms are in place, under the (a) Northern Ireland Civil Service HR Handbook; and (b) Prison Service Framework Document, to address outstanding annual leave payments for retired prison officers; and (ii) what action she will take to rectify outstanding annual leave payments.
answer_text: The Prison Service Framework is an historical document that is no longer utilised.  All staff who have left the Prison Service had any untaken annual leave calculated in accordance with the relevant terms and conditions as set out in the NICS HR Handbook at the time of their departure.  Therefore, no further action is necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action she will take following the latest Criminal Justice Inspection report on HMP Magilligan which found (i) purposeful activity between prisoners rated as poor; and (ii) the reduction in rehabilitation and release planning from the inspection in 2018.
answer_text: I am pleased that inspectors recognised where progress has been made in their recent Independent Review of Progress at Magilligan Prison, however I appreciate that there are areas where more work is required.The Governor of Magilligan Prison, in conjunction with education partners and probation services, is implementing a plan to address the inspection recommendations and to build upon the improvements identified by inspectors.  Progress made since the inspection includes completing a review of the education curriculum, and implementing a “Step up” programme to support progression from entry-level education courses. There has also been an increase in the number of prisoners placed in full time paid employment in the community from the working out unit in Magilligan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) why Prison Service staff, who left in service, were paid in full under the Prison Service Framework Document, yet staff who left under Voluntary Early Retirement Scheme were not paid in full; and (i) what action she will take to rectify this.
answer_text: The Prison Service Framework is an historical document that is no longer in use.  All staff who have left the Prison Service had any untaken annual leave calculated in accordance with the relevant terms and conditions as set out in the NICS HR Handbook at the time of their departure. An agreement was reached with the trade unions in respect of serving staff and annual leave.  Therefore, no further action is necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has plans to ensure pay parity for Further Education lecturers with school teachers.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether she will bring forward a ban on XL Bully dog breed, similar to that in England and Wales.
answer_text: As Minister with responsibility for legislation on dog control in Northern Ireland, a proportionate and effective way forward to deal with potentially dangerous dogs is a key issue for me.I recognise the need to move quickly in this area and I am keen to engage with a wide range of stakeholders, to hear their views, so that any decision I take will be based on the best evidence available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward an animal welfare strategy for farmed and non-farmed animals, with a focus on dogs.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the need for reform in this area and will be considering options with both stakeholders and my officials in the time ahead.  This will assist in me in determining what is needed, and what is deliverable, within the lifetime of this assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commission and commence a formal review of legal and illegal dog breeding in Northern Ireland in partnership with the PSNI and local government.
answer_text: There are a broad range of key priorities for reform of animal welfare in Northern Ireland.  While I have no current plans for a formal review of legal and illegal dog breeding, I am conscious that more action may be required in the time ahead and so I am keen to work with stakeholders on this and other related issues.		My Department already has strong working relationships with local councils and the PSNI.  This including cooperation under the Paws for Thought initiative. Through this forum, statutory agencies work closely to combat the trafficking of low welfare pups, including illegal breeding.  Councils are responsible for enforcing the legislation and a maximum penalty for breaching the law is six months custodial sentence and a fine of £5,000.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he will commission and commence a formal review of animal welfare service delivery in local government; and (ii) for his assessment of animal welfare.
answer_text: Each local council decides how best to deliver its statutory obligations for an animal welfare enforcement service, and to do so in a way which meets the specific requirements of its region.   My Department has no recourse to intervene and to do so would undermine the legal authority of councils.  It is important that councils operate as autonomous bodies, accountable to locally elected representatives, delivering services tailored to the needs of their citizens.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how environmental considerations for the Mobuoy Remediation Project are being met.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work of the Historical Environment Division in relation to the location of the wind turbine on Knock Iveagh.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is aware of a planning application that has been lodged for Havelock House in Belfast.
answer_text: No planning application has been submitted to my Department for this property and on the basis that any such an application would probably not be a Regionally Significant Planning Application it would be dealt with by Belfast City Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 28/17-22 on 3 February 2020, (i) why she has not met the Save Knock Iveagh community group to date; and (ii) whether she will arrange to meet with the group.
answer_text: I have recently accepted a request to meet with the Save Knock Iveagh group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many of the recommendations in the Review of Support Services for Operational Prison Staff have been fully implemented; and (ii) what is the time frame for full implementation of those that have not.
answer_text: (i) The 12 strategic recommendations contain 26 actions.  Of these 17 are assessed as fully implemented, seven as partially implemented and two as not yet implemented as these are being held in abeyance pending the outcome of other related work outside of the Prison Service.(ii) It is not possible to give a time frame for full implementation as that is dependent on other related work outside of the Prison Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will work with the Department for Infrastructure to ensure Translink bus services from town centres coincide with school end and start times and therefore supply a sufficient bus service for school children.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and it works closely with Translink on route planning and ensuring capacity where pupils in receipt of transport assistance are issued passes for use on the public transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will work with the Education Authority to ensure Translink bus services from town centres coincide with school end and start times and therefore provide a sufficient bus service for children.
answer_text: As you may be aware, the Education Authority (EA) is responsible for home to school transport and Translink’s delivery of these services is conducted under a contract with them.The scheduling of bus services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many cases of unfair dismissal have been taken against the Northern Ireland Prison Service by serving officers, of all categories, in the last three years; (ii) how many of these cases were upheld; and (iii) how many times did the Northern Ireland Prison Service reinstate the individual.
answer_text: (i) There have been seven cases of unfair dismissal taken against the Northern Ireland Prison Service in the last three years.(ii) No cases have been upheld.(iii) No individuals have been reinstated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many prison officers, in all categories of officer, are presently on long term sickness absence as of September 2022; and (ii) what was the figure for the previous two years.
answer_text: (i)	Records show that at 30 September 2022, there were 89 Prison Grade staff on long-term sickness absence.(ii)	For the same period in 2021 there were 96 Prison Grade staff on long-term sickness absence; and in 2020 there were 46¹.  ¹ The figures reported during this period reflect the COVID-19 temporary arrangements introduced across the NICS which included additional protections for those staff deemed to be Clinically Extremely Vulnerable (CEV).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many times in the last 12 months has the Northern Ireland Prison Service not reached its Regime Delivery Quota by leaving posts uncovered; and (ii) whether all search areas within prison estates are routinely staffed.
answer_text: Regime Delivery Quotas are used as operational guidelines for how the regime in each residential area in a prison can be adjusted dependent upon staff availability.  Governors work on an ongoing basis to maximise the regime that can be made available to prisoners on a daily basis taking into account a range of factors including safety and security.  For that reason the information requested is not recorded in a format consistent with the question asked.All search areas are staffed proportionately and appropriately according to risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) the number of newly trained prison officers, covering all categories, entering the prison service in the last 12 months; and (ii) what is the attrition rate for officers leaving the service during the same period.
answer_text: (i) In the financial year 2021/2022, 41 new recruits joined the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS).  (ii) The attrition rate for officers leaving the service during the same period is set out in the table below.Attrition Rate 2021/22Uniformed Prison Staff NICS Overall HMPPS1 April 2021 - 31 March 20227.6%4.6%12.6%Notes:Source - HR Connect and additional DoJ DatabasesLeaver rate calculation – is calculated by dividing the number of leavers by the number of Staff in Post * 100In relation to the attrition rate for prison officer grades, NIPS reports a higher leaver rate when compared to the NICS overall but a lower leaver rate when compared to HMPPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to outline why the Northern Ireland Prison Service does not recognise the Community Union; and (ii) what criteria it applied in recognising the Prison Officers' Association.
answer_text: (i) The Prison Officers’ Association is the recognised Trade Union with bargaining rights for all operational staff/business areas in the Northern Ireland Prison Service. The Community Union has sought bargaining rights for one specific business area within NIPS, this has not been granted as representation already exists. 	(ii) The Prison Officers Association (POA) in Northern Ireland was formed in 1939. A certificate of independence for the POA in Northern Ireland was obtained in 1940 from the Minister of Home Affairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what criteria his Department uses when deciding if a road has a good safety record.
answer_text: When responding to requests for improvements due to road safety issues, my Department takes into account traffic speeds and volumes, road layout, pedestrian facilities, the three year injury collision history, as well as individual site constraints. The injury collision data is provided by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the waiting times for a MOT.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect.  This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) to its customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The DVA has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The next tranche of new vehicle examiners started their training on 28 September and a further tranche are due to commence training in mid-October.To further increase vehicle testing capacity across its network, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  As a first step, the DVA is currently constructing a new test centre at Hydebank in Belfast, which is due to open in 2023.  A second new test centre at Mallusk, which is scheduled to open in 2024, is currently at the planning stage.  These two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help meet the future demand for this service.The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many applicants, who were successful in the latest PSNI recruitment drive, have progressed through training and into a placement.
answer_text: The application stage for the latest PSNI recruitment drive closed on 19 November 2021.  I have been advised by the PSNI that none of the successful applicants have yet progressed through the pre-employment stages to be offered a placement at the Police Training College. The recruitment of trainee officers to the PSNI is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board and I have no role in this process.  The prioritisation of policing resources is a decision for the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail any discussions he has had with the Westminster government regarding the establishment of freeports in Northern Ireland; and (ii) for his assessment of where and how freeports would best benefit Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have been calling for clarity from the British Government for some time now on what a Freeport offer for the North might look like. I wrote to the then Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST), Steve Barclay, twice in July and August 2021 with no response, then raised this directly with his successor, Simon Clarke, when we met in June this year, and where he committed to providing a full and substantive response. However no such response has been received. Until we get the detail of an offer, we cannot fully assess if or how a Freeport might benefit the North, nor where it might be located.  I reiterated my disappointment at the nature of the British Government’s engagement on Freeports when I met with the new CST, Chris Philp, on 23rd September and where he suggested further discussions on the Treasury’s latest initiative on Investment Zones, where there is similarly little detail as to how they might stimulate new economic activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what grant or funding streams are available from her Department for the restoration of derelict or dilapidated housing.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) under its remit to “Protect the Environment” has overall responsibility for Dilapidation.  DAERA officials are currently assessing the legislation used by councils to deal with dilapidated/dangerous buildings and neglected sites.The Housing Executive offers a limited number of grants to keep homes in a reasonable state of repair. For example:Repair grant can help landlords where a council has issued a ‘statutory notice’ about repairs on their property. Owners or landlords of a vacant property can apply for this grant to repair or replace existing items. Renovation Grant is for homes that have been inspected and found to be unfit to live in. The property must be the owner’s main or only home. This grant is only available in the exceptional circumstances. Replacement grants are available where a home is unfit to live and repair is not an option, these grants are also only available in exceptional circumstances. All grants are subject to available funding. Further information can be found on the Housing Executive website https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Housing-Help/Grants/Types-of-grants-available

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on (i) the Review of Civil and Family Justice; and (ii) when an independent chair will be appointed, as outlined in the Review Group’s Report on Family Justice, September 2017.
answer_text: The Gillen Review of Civil and Family Justice made a significant number of wide ranging recommendations. Most fall outside the remit of the Department of Justice and are instead matters for the judiciary, the legal profession and the Departments of Health and Finance, but Sir John’s detailed work will no doubt inform decisions on the relevant issues.  While my Department did not commission the Review, it helped shape my initial priorities for civil and family justice reform in the Modernisation Programme I launched last year and, I expect it will continue to help shape priorities as I take stock and consider what can be achieved in the current mandate, within the constraints of the very challenging budget. The shadow Family Justice Board, together with the shadow Civil Justice Council and their advisory groups have proved very valuable fora and how the bodies might evolve will be among matters considered this mandate. However, in a context of constrained resources I will want to ensure focus continues to be on areas likely to generate most significant benefits for citizens and actions will be subject to prioritisation.  Establishment of a Board with an independent Chair is also a cross-cutting issue which would require agreement of the other Departments with responsibility for civil and family justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many staff members have been assaulted in Maghaberry Prison in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In the past 12 months (June 2021 – May 2022) there have been 33 assaults by prisoners on staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps are being taken to ensure that those who benefit from EU funding are able to avail of an alternate stream of funding once these payments stop.
answer_text: The loss of EU Funding and the failure of the British Government to provide full replacement has resulted in a loss to the Executive budget in excess of £70m per annum over the next three years, this figure being the baseline loss without applying inflation. In particular, the Shared Prosperity Fund, introduced by the British Government to replace EU Structural Funds will be operating directly from Whitehall. This Fund is delivering too little money, in a very fragmented way that is not targeted to the groups that previously received EU funding.My department leads on these issues and is seeking, through the cross-departmental Future Policy and Finance Workstream, to assess the totality of the current position on funding including continuing EU funds, Whitehall funds and other funding streams. However the refusal of the British Government to afford us an appropriate decision making role in new funding streams prevents me from ensuring that those groups currently funded from EU sources will be able to continue to avail of these funding streams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the current staffing levels at Maghaberry Prison.
answer_text: As HMP Maghaberry is a high security facility, it would not be appropriate to comment on specific staffing levels as to do so could impact upon the safe and secure operation of the prison.Overall the Northern Ireland Prison Service is currently managing a significant increase in the prison population and a higher than normal level of staff absence, due to a combination of sickness absence and absence as a result of COVID-19.  As a result, Governors are required to undertake daily dynamic risk assessments which include reviewing staffing levels, ensuring essential services are delivered and assessing the level of regime that can be provided to facilitate the rehabilitation of those in our care.  Such assessments allow the Governor to make balanced decisions in relation to staff deployment, the level of regime that can be offered and what activities and services can be delivered each day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether mental health charities are subject to assessments by his Department on the work they carry out.
answer_text: The oversight of all charities here is regulated by the Charity Commission. Neither the Department, nor the Commission, has any remit to assess the professional competency of mental health organisations that happen to be registered as charities. This question would be more appropriately addressed to the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many prisoner on prisoner assaults have taken place in Maghaberry Prison in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In the past 12 months (June 2021 – May 2022) there have been 40 assaults by prisoners on prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients have had an ESSURE coil removed in the last five years.
answer_text: As the Department of Health does not collect this information centrally, each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) has provided the following information:Belfast HSCT has recorded 52 removals of Inter-Uterine Coil Devices (IUCDs) as the primary procedure and 120 as a secondary procedure in the last five years.  However, they have indicated that they are unable to specify if they were the ESSURE coil type of IUCD.Secondary procedures may not be the main reason for a hospital admission and may be a consequence of the primary procedure/ main admission reason, for example, cancer surgeries or hysterectomies.Northern HSCT has indicated that there were two ESSURE coil removals within the last 5 years.South Eastern HSCT has recorded one ESSURE coil removal within the last 5 years.Southern HSCT has specified that there were no ESSURE Coil removal procedures within the last 5 years.Western HSCT has responded that they do not have this information readily available, and that they could only provide such information at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what the current policy is for victim support services in relation to retaining contact with victims throughout an ongoing court case.
answer_text: The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and standard of services that victims should expect from criminal justice organisations.  This includes entitlements to be updated at key stages and be given relevant information.   The Victim and Witness Care Unit (VWCU) provides victims with a single point of contact from when an investigation or charge file is submitted to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS), through to and including the outcome of any court proceedings.My Department provides funding to Victim Support NI and the NSPCC Young Witness Service to provide court support to prosecution victims and witnesses.  Both organisations have policies in place in relation to the services they provide and the contact they have with victims and witnesses in relation to the court case. On receipt of referrals from the VWCU, the organisations make contact with the victims and witnesses to explain and offer their court support services.  Support is then provided in advance of the trial, when appropriate and accepted, and at court on the day of the trial.  Both organisations maintain contact with the victims and witnesses throughout this process.In 2020, the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland made a strategic recommendation that called for the establishment of a working group to examine a future VWCU service model and to produce a next steps options paper for the Criminal Justice Board (CJB).  An options paper was considered by the CJB on 25 August 2021.  That paper proposed a restructured VWCU incorporating a new, integrated Needs Assessment Service, which would be delivered by VSNI.  The CJB was supportive of the guiding principles and approach proposed in the paper and accepted that it was the best model.  However, the Board noted challenges around implementation, particularly in the context of future budget uncertainties.   The Board noted the need for further discussions on resourcing between DOJ, PPS and PSNI and undertook to reconsider the paper at a future meeting when there is further clarity on future Departmental budget allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what protocol the victim support services follow when contacting victims.
answer_text: The Victim Charter sets out the entitlements and the standard of service that victims should expect from criminal justice service providers.  This includes the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Public Prosecution Service, the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service, The Northern Ireland Prison Service, the Probation Board for Northern Ireland, the Youth Justice Agency and the victim support providers (Victim Support NI and NSPCC YWS).  A copy of the Victim Charter can be found at: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/doj/victim-charter.pdf My Department provides funding to Victim Support NI to provide support services to victims and witnesses of crime.  These services are provided in line with Victim Support NI procedures and agreements/protocols with other organisations.  On receipt of referrals from PSNI, Victim Support NI makes contact with victims by telephone, letter or text to offer their services. Appointments are scheduled for those victims who wish to avail of the support services available.  Where ongoing specialised support is required, Victim Support NI refers the victim on to the relevant organisation.  The Victim and Witness Care Unit makes referrals to Victim Support NI’s Witness Service which contacts the victims and witnesses to explain and offer their court support services.  Support is then provided in advance of the trial, when appropriate and accepted, and at court on the day of the trial.My Department also provides funding to the NSPCC for the provision of the Young Witness Service to all young prosecution witnesses under eighteen years of age who are called to give evidence at court.  These services are provided in line with NSPCC Young Witness Service procedures and agreements/protocols with criminal justice organisations.  On receipt of referrals from the Victim and Witness Care Unit, the Young Witness Service would gain informed consent from parents/carers to provide relevant support to young witnesses in accordance with their needs both in advance of the trial and at court. Where ongoing specialised support is required, the Young Witness Service refers the young witness on to the relevant organisation.It is important that victims of crime receive the treatment and entitlements they are entitled to as outlined in the Victim Charter.  Part of the role of the newly appointed Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime will be to promote the Charter, identifying good practice and also highlighting were adherence to the Charter needs to improve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what support is given to victims of domestic violence.
answer_text: Ensuring that victims of domestic abuse are supported is a key priority for me.  A range of measures are in place to support victims of both domestic and sexual abuse, and a number of work streams are in train that will deliver further services and improved outcomes for all victims.My Department provides funding of £2.5m to ensure that support services are available to all victims and witnesses of crime, including victims of both domestic and sexual abuse.  These services are delivered through Victim Support Northern Ireland, which provides a range of services including the provision of emotional support, advocacy, information and advice to victims; assistance with claiming Criminal Injuries Compensation; and court support services; and through the NSPCC Young Witness Service, which supports young victims and witnesses called to give evidence at court.  In addition, PCSPs, funded by my Department in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, have prioritised domestic abuse for a number of years, and in 2022/2023, each PCSP is funding support and awareness initiatives.  Sexual Offences Legal Advisers can also offer legal advice and support to adult victims of a serious sexual offence, which may occur in a domestic context.A range of support services are available to those affected by domestic abuse.  In partnership with PSNI, my Department funds a domestic and sexual abuse advocacy service to provide support, information and a single point of contact for victims during their journey through the justice system.  The service is provided by ASSIST NI, comprising Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid, Foyle Women’s Aid and the Men’s Advisory Project. 17 female advocates and 3 male advocates are in post.  At the end of March 2022, around 1,400 victims had availed of the service.  In addition, a 24hr domestic and sexual abuse helpline is available for those affected by domestic or sexual abuse.   During the last mandate, I brought forward legislation to deal with a wide range of abusive behaviours, including the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, and the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  Many of the offences and behaviours captured by these new provisions will be at the heart of domestic abuse. In addition, a Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme is available where there is a concern about the risk posed by a partner or former partner, in order that they can make an informed decision about a relationship.  I introduced Domestic Homicide Reviews, to learn lessons from those tragic cases where someone has died as a result of domestic abuse.  Behavioural change programmes are also available. My Department, in conjunction with the Department of Health, has carried out a Call for Views to inform the development of a new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.  We intend to publish a summary in July.  My officials are

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what her plans are to help schools address the current fuel crisis.
answer_text: Firstly, my Department fully recognises the real difficulty which increasing energy and utilities prices presents for our schools.Secondly, it is my hope that a new Executive will reconsider the Finance Minister’s previous 2022-25 Draft Budget proposal, with the aim of approving a multi-year budget in due course.  The price pressures presented by the current fuel crisis is but one of the c£465m of unfunded, pre-committed or inescapable pressures which my Department faces.  In the last quarter of the 2021-22 financial year, I instructed the Education Authority (EA) to allocate almost £5m of additional funding to our schools to mitigate against the impact of rising energy costs during the winter months.  As we go forward, my officials seek periodic updates from the EA in relation to forecasted energy price pressures facing our schools, the most recent of which indicated c£11.9m of additional costs relating to rises in natural gas, heating oil and electricity costs in the 2022-23 financial year. These energy price pressures have been conveyed to the Department of Finance in the absence and in anticipation of an agreed budget for 2022-23. Since August 2020, the EA has proactively sought to mitigate the impact of increasing utility and energy costs by engaging an independent energy advisor to advise on purchasing energy, tariff changes and hedging purchase prices for electricity and gas on behalf of our schools. With all of this said, I am under no illusion in relation to the continuing and deleterious impact which ongoing market volatility and increased energy prices will have on our schools. I can assure you that my officials continue to keep the situation under regular review, in anticipation of the formation of a new Executive and agreement of a budget for 2022-23, which will enable pressures such as increasing energy costs for our schools to be more effectively addressed.  In the meantime any school facing financial difficulty should contact the EA, as funding authority for grant aided schools in the normal way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what the current curriculum for physical education is within (i) primary and; (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: Physical education (PE) is a core and mandatory element of the curriculum for all pupils from Foundation Stage to Key Stage 4 and my Department is committed to ensuring that all children enjoy high quality curricular PE that is suitable to their age, ability and aptitude.The curriculum provides schools with the flexibility to plan teaching to meet the needs of their pupils.  Subject to meeting the minimum requirements for PE provision, it is a matter for schools to tailor the curriculum to meet the needs of their pupils and to decide how it is delivered and the resources used. Schools are required to deliver a balanced programme of athletics, dance, games and gymnastics (and swimming from Key Stage 2 onwards) at both primary level and post-primary schools.At Key Stage 3 there is a focus on an active, enjoyable and challenging PE learning experiences to encourage pupils to develop:positive attitudes to and enjoyment of physical activity;an awareness of the positive impact of physical activity on health and wellbeing; andpositive sporting behaviour and a sense of fair play.At Key Stage 4 pupils are enabled to:plan and participate in a regular, frequent and balanced programme of physical activity that:develops their interests and talents;extends their knowledge, understanding and skills; andcontributes to, and helps sustain, a healthy and active lifestyle;evaluate their own performances and that of others; andrecognise and manage risk and apply safe principles and procedures before, during and after physical activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many schools within the Upper Bann area have access to a 3G pitch within their school grounds, broken down by; (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it is aware of two schools within the Upper Bann constituency with access to a 3G pitch within their school grounds. The schools are Lurgan Junior High School and St Patrick’s College, Banbridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what checks are carried out in schools to make sure they are wheelchair accessible.
answer_text: New build schools are designed to be accessible by all staff, pupils and visitors to meet the relevant statutory requirements and therefore would be wheelchair accessible.Given the size, age and condition of the School Estate, it is not feasible to make every space in every school fully wheelchair accessible.  When a DDA need arises in a school, they can submit an unavoidable minor works application to my Department or the Education Authority, dependant on the school type, and the application will be given priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many schools within Upper Bann have a Nurture Group room.
answer_text: Records are only maintained for those schools which receive funding from the Department for a Nurture Group.In response to AQW 961/22-27, I advised that my Department currently funds 3 Nurture Groups in primary schools in Upper Bann.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what is the Education Authority's policy on school adaptions for disabled teachers.
answer_text: The Education Authority Disability Employment Support Service (DESS) provides an advisory service to line managers on consideration of reasonable adjustments for eligible employees and schools based staff with disabilities.  Staff also have the option to self-refer to the DESS team.  Where reasonable adjustments involve specialist equipment and/or adaptations to the physical environment these will be explored with colleagues in relevant functions e.g. Operations and Estates, Procurement and ICT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many members of staff of the Education Authority are currently working from home and; (ii) the reasons why.
answer_text: (i)	EA employs approximately 44,000 staff, the vast majority of whom are school based or in front line services directly supporting schools.  Around 7% of staff work in a range of vital support functions. Many of these staff have been working from offices on a permanent or on a blended basis since the beginning of the pandemic. We currently have 1,010 corporate staff who are currently using home as their primary base, for many of whom, their role involves regularly visiting schools/homes, in addition to more desk-based work.(ii)	All EA services are fully operational to pre-pandemic levels and whilst the NI Executive guidance on 'working from home' has only recently been lifted, we have put in place a process that enables services to transition staff back into buildings should this be required. Further to this, EA are also developing a New Ways of Working Framework for our staff, seeking to build a new agile, flexible and responsive model that greater meet the needs of schools, parents and children and young people. This involves looking at a blended approach which would maximise staff time and resources and encourage innovation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has any plans to carry out a procurement review.
answer_text: My Department commissions all public procurement over the current EU threshold through Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD). It has teams of professionally qualified staff providing expert advice to the NI departments and a number of Arm's Length Bodies.  CPD provides the Department with a range of services designed to ensure procurement processes, tender documentation, evaluations and contract awards comply with NI Public Procurement Policy and the UK Procurement Regulations. I have therefore no plans to carry out a procurement review. It is worth noting that there are significant and transformational changes coming through the Procurement Reform Bill. The Bill was laid in Parliament 11 May 2022. It should have Royal Ascent within 12 months and then 6 months to come into effect with new Regulations, estimated around October 2023.CPD Officials continue to work closely with Cabinet Office on the outworks of these reforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) which schools in Upper Bann are currently non-compliant with the Building Handbook; and (ii) what are her plans are to address this.
answer_text: The Schools Building Handbooks set out the design requirements where it is proposed to build a new school. The Department of Education does not maintain a list of existing schools which are not fully compliant with the Building Handbooks.    Any deficiencies in school accommodation can be considered through an application to the various departmental capital funding streams that is the Minor Works Programme, the School Enhancement Programme and the Major Capital Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when she will be confirming payment of the Engage II Programme funding to schools.
answer_text: The Engage programme has been extremely successful to date, with more than £24 million provided to 1,700 educational settings during the 2021/22 academic year. Schools were notified on 28 March 2022 of their additional Engage II Programme funding to 30 June 2022.  This represents an additional £7.6m on top of the £16.6m already provided for the September 2021 to March 2022 period.Engage has ensured that our children and young people have been provided with a range of support to reduce the impact which the Covid-19 pandemic has had on their educational experience and learning. As regards a third year of the programme during the 2022/23 academic year, I have issued a Direction to my officials for the Engage programme to continue to December 2022.  The Department of Finance approved that Direction on 6 July 2022, and I am delighted that I have been able to allocate £10million to continue the programme until the end of December 2022.   I have recently written to schools and settings to inform them.It is my intention that the programme will continue until the end of the academic year. However, as this remains subject to the necessary funding being made available, settings are not able to commit to expenditure beyond December 2022.  I will update schools on the budget position for the rest of the academic year as soon as I am in a position to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why funding has not yet been allocated for the Communities Access and Inclusion Programme for the 2022-23 financial year.
answer_text: In the absence of a functioning Executive and agreed budget the Department is operating on a contingency planning envelope for 2022-23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how her Department monitors that children, who are being home schooled, are provided with a suitable education.
answer_text: The responsibility for a child’s education rests with the child’s parents. Article 45(1) of the Education and Libraries (NI) Order 1986 explains the duties of parents to educate their children. It states that “The parent of every child of compulsory school age shall cause him to receive efficient full-time education suitable to his age, ability and aptitude and to any special educational needs he may have, either by regular attendance at school or otherwise.”  Parents may therefore choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school, however they remain responsible for providing an efficient full-time education suitable to the child’s needs.  The Registration and Attendance of Pupils Regulations (NI) Order 1974 places a duty on parents who choose to educate their children outside of the formal school system to satisfy the Education Authority (EA) that their child is receiving an education as defined above.  The 1986 Order also places a duty on the EA to intervene if it appears that parents are failing to perform their duty to ensure their child is receiving an education as outlined above.  The EA guidance on Elective Home Education provides details of the process to be followed should a parent elect to home educate a child: https://www.eani.org.uk/services/elective-home-education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are criteria for home schooling children.
answer_text: The responsibility for a child’s education rests with their parents and parents may choose to secure their child’s education either at school or outside of the formal school environment. Article 45 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 (as amended) places a duty on parents to ensure that their child receives an ‘efficient full-time education suitable to his age, ability and aptitude and to any special educational needs he may have, either by regular attendance at school or otherwise’. Parents choose to home educate for a wide variety of reasons, including Religious or Spiritual beliefs or for ideological or philosophical reasons, however there are no criteria for home schooling children.    Further information on Elective Home Education can be found at https://www.eani.org.uk/sites/default/files/2020-12/Guidelines%20for%20EHE%2023112020.pdf.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the efforts, facilitated by Invest NI, to encourage investment in Upper Bann.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s compelling investment proposition is promoted on a whole of Northern Ireland basis and is focused on our talented and loyal workforce, advanced telecoms infrastructure, high skilled business clusters, a pro-business environment, competitive operating costs as well as proximity to major markets. The Northern Ireland proposition is facilitated by close collaboration between business, academia and government.During the period 2016-17 to 2020-21, Invest NI provided support to businesses, which will lead to the creation of 906 new jobs within the Upper Bann Parliamentary Constituency. Jobs arising from these projects will be created over the next 3 to 5 years and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £23.01m.  Invest NI supported these businesses with £10.45m of employment-related assistance which will leverage a total investment of £75.92m.  Invest NI also provided 937 offers of other developmental assistance to businesses, totalling £31.44m with a total investment of £96.22m, to accelerate their further growth including financial assistance for research and development, skills development and to support the export promotion of products and services from the Upper Bann constituency.Invest NI’s Western Regional team are key partners in Armagh Banbridge Craigavon Community Planning and also participate in the newly formed Labour Market Partnership to address skills needs and also with the Business Partnership Alliance (BPA) to increase demand for Invest Northern Ireland’s supports in the Upper Bann area.Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic Invest NI has worked closely with local businesses and stakeholders. In addition to important information sharing, we have also supported Council led business development programmes to assist with COVID issues.  Invest NI’s continues to support the £250m MSW Region Growth Deal which covers the 3 local authorities; Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon, Fermanagh & Omagh, & Mid Ulster.  To inform the Deal, the Region produced a Regional Economic Strategy [RES] which was officially launched by FM/dFM on 9th September 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many jobs have been created in 2022 with support from Invest NI in the Upper Bann area.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2021 being the most recent available.  During the period 2020-21, Invest NI provided support to businesses, which will lead to the creation of 144 new jobs within the Upper Bann Parliamentary Constituency. Jobs arising from these projects will be created over the next 3 to 5 years, and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £3.13m.  Invest NI supported these businesses with £3.23m of employment-related assistance which will leverage a total investment of £15.35m.In addition, Invest NI provided 182 offers of other developmental assistance to businesses, totalling £7.52m, to accelerate their further growth including financial assistance for research and development, skills development and to support the promotion of products and services made in the Upper Bann constituency in overseas markets.Information on the 2021-22 financial year will be available in due course.  For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will be taking to ensure the Energy Bills Support Scheme can be rolled out in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Finance is liaising with Treasury to determine how this might be delivered in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, given the current cost of living crisis, will he (i) review the eligibility criteria for university grants; and (ii) increase the grants.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that the cost of living crisis is having on households across Northern Ireland, and in particular on students who are living away from home whilst studying. For the current 2021/22 academic year my Department continues to provide student support to eligible higher education students in the form of a means tested Maintenance Loan of up to £3,750 for students living at home, up to £4,840 for those living away from home and studying elsewhere in the UK outside of London, and up to £6,780 for those studying in London. Eligible students can also apply for a non-repayable means tested Maintenance Grant or Special Support Grant of up to £3,475 to help with living costs.  The level of support provided through the higher education student support package is assessed on an annual basis and subject to the financial position of my Department at that time. However, in all likelihood, the earliest any changes could be made to the student support package as it currently stands would be academic year 2023/24 given lead in times and the lack of an agreed Executive Budget.As an alternative source of funding, students who are experiencing genuine financial hardship can apply for additional financial help through Support Funds. The Funds are administered on my Department’s behalf by the five higher education institutions in Northern Ireland and each institution is responsible for assessing students’ needs and issuing support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the current cost of living crisis, will she review the eligibility criteria for free school meals.
answer_text: The Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place in the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the current cost of living crisis, whether she will (i) review the eligibility criteria for school uniform grants; and (ii) increase the grant.
answer_text: Uniform Grants are available to families who meet certain qualifying criteria: https://www.eani.org.uk/financial-help/free-school-meals-uniform-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grant-eligibilityThe Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place later in the year.  In the interim period, I have agreed an increase of 20% to the existing uniform grant allowances for the forthcoming academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many applications for pre-schools were (i) accepted; and (ii) declined, within the Upper Bann area.
answer_text: The Pre-school Education Programme (PSEP) has a two-stage admissions process which is based on parental preference.  Stages 1 and 2 of the process ended on 28 April and 16 June 2022 respectively. Following the close of Stage 2, the Education Authority (EA) has advised, that of those children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school place within the Upper Bann constituency, 1638 have been offered a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. The EA has also advised that fewer than 5 target aged children whose parents have stayed with the process to the end have not yet been offered a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference. Sufficient funded places remain available in the Upper Bann area to provide a funded place for every child in their immediate pre-school year whose parents want it and children will continue to be offered funded places prior to the beginning of the forthcoming pre-school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build planned for Portadown Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: The appointment of an Integrated Consultant Team to carry out the design of the proposed Portadown Integrated Primary School was concluded in February 2022.  The Integrated Consultant Team is now working on the early stages of the design process which will then allow my Department to develop a business case to identify the preferred option for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Nurture Groups in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools in Upper Bann.
answer_text: My Department currently funds the following 3 Nurture Groups in primary schools in Upper Bann:St Anthony’s Primary School, Tullygally Road, ArmaghSt Francis Primary School, Francis Street, LurganTullygally Primary School, Meadowbrook Road, LurganFunding is provided to primary schools only as the principles behind Nurture Group provision were developed to specifically address Social, Behavioural, Emotional, Wellbeing needs (SBEW) and attachment difficulties in young children. However, the Nurture in Education Programme, which is delivered by the Education Authority, is available to all primary, special, post-primary schools and Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) settings to allow staff to avail of training and support in nurture approaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the current cost of living crisis, whether she has plans to extend the Sure Start programme into additional areas that are facing deprivation.
answer_text: The Sure Start programme is delivered via 38 Sure Start projects, in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland (NI), as measured by the NI Multiple Deprivation Measure (NIMDM) 2010; research indicates this is where targeted early intervention can be most effective. Provision is universal within the Sure Start catchment areas, therefore all children under four (and their families) in these areas, can access services.Increased Department of Education (DE) funding of £1.56m in 2021/22 and 2022/23 is facilitating  temporary extension of services to an additional 22 Super Output Areas falling within the 25% most disadvantaged areas when applying the latest (2017) NIMDM.To ensure optimum early intervention, DE will take account of the findings of the ongoing Independent Review of Targeted Early Years Interventions (including Sure Start and its geographical scope), which is expected to complete by autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what resources are available to the Child Maintenance Service to ensure that children or adults are attending the full-time education to which they are registered.
answer_text: The Child Maintenance Service (CMS) will check whether Child Benefit remains in payment and will liaise with the Receiving Parent, to clarify the educational status of their child. The parent who is in receipt of Child Benefit has a requirement to report any changes, such as their child leaving full-time education, to the Child Benefit Office within Revenue and Customs.  If the Child Benefit is stopped, CMS will be notified through the automatic interface with Revenue and Customs which enables appropriate changes to be made to the child maintenance case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities under what circumstances can officials in Child Maintenance Service refuse to meet, either in person or online, a parent who is seeking to resolve issues in their case.
answer_text: Whilst the Child Maintenance Service (CMS) primarily delivers its services through its online digital platform and telephony service, there may be some situations where it is preferable to talk in person. Whilst CMS officials would seek to accommodate any request from a parent for a face-to-face meeting, there may be occasions, such as adhering to the Covid-19 public health guidance, where a face-to-face meeting may be declined. In these circumstances, the parent will be informed of the reason the meeting will not take place.  CMS provides an Outreach Service to MLA’s or staff within their constituency office. If you or a member of your constituency office would like to discuss any case specific queries raised by a constituent further details on how to contact the CMS Outreach Service is available at the following link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/ni-child-maintenance-outreach-service

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will review the Child Maintenance Service policy to ensure that children or adults are attending the full-time education to which they are registered.
answer_text: The onus is on the parent receiving child benefit to report any change of circumstances to HMRC, which includes a child leaving full time education.  It is not within the remit of the Child Maintenance Service to monitor a child’s attendance in full time education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when she will be announcing her decision regarding the Lurgan Campus of Craigavon Senior High School.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 574 which proposes that “Craigavon Senior High School will operate on a single site at 26-34 Lurgan Road, Portadown with effect from 1 September 2022 or as soon as possible thereafter” was published by the Education Authority (EA) on 22 April 2021.  The statutory objection period ended on 22 June 2021.A submission containing all pertinent information on the proposal has been presented to me for my consideration and ultimate decision.I appreciate that clarity and certainty is required by all involved and I will make my decision in due course.
